Preserve my stack for a better spot — fold and take the 50-chip blind loss.

Three-handed, on the button I should be opening very wide. K♥5♠ is a marginal hand but has a high-card blocker (king) and plays well in position; with deep stacks I can outplay the blinds postflop. Table tendency shows standard 3x opens to ~300, so I’ll use that sizing to get fold equity and keep my range honest. If I get a single call I’ll continue in position; I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet (or reassess vs a small 3‑bet based on the villain).

I'm in the small blind with 2h-Ts, one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni (button) has raised to 300 from late position, and I would need to call 250 more to see the flop. Even in short-handed play (3 players), this is a clear fold situation because: 1) 2-10 offsuit has minimal equity and very poor playability, 2) I'm out of position relative to the button raiser, 3) I'd be investing chips into a marginal spot with poor post-flop prospects. My stack is healthy at 10,150, so I can afford to be selective and wait for stronger hands. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300 (3x the big blind). While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (200 to call into a pot of 450), T7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but T7o still lacks the equity or playability to justify a call against a button open, especially given Minni's active playstyle. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

AK is the third-best starting hand in poker and should be played aggressively, especially on the button. I'm facing a small blind and big blind, and I need to build the pot with premium holdings. Raising to 350 (3.5x BB) is a standard aggressive play that prices in weaker hands while maintaining fold equity. My stack position (10,150) is healthy enough to play this hand strongly, and dominating the action with AK preflop maximizes my expected value.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 350, which is 3.5x the big blind. In a three-handed game, button opening ranges are wide, but Jack-Six offsuit is still significantly below the defensive threshold for the Small Blind. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a weak, non-connected hand that doesn't flop well. 3-betting as a bluff is also suboptimal given the hand's lack of blockers or post-flop playability. The most disciplined and +EV play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m in the BB with Q♠4♠ facing a BTN open to 350 (I must call 250 more). With deep stacks (18,350 vs 9,800) and a wide button open, Q4s has enough playability (backdoor flush/straight potential and a queen blocker) to defend. 3-betting OOP with this hand is too ambitious and folding is too nitty given the pot odds and implied odds. I’ll call to see a flop (total committed = 350). Plan postflop: proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, float/attack favorable textures, and fold to heavy sustained aggression when I miss.

I have Q♠4♠ on A♥ Q♣ Q♦ — I’m holding trips (QQQ). This is a very strong hand and I should be extracting value from Ax, Kx and weaker hands that the BTN would open with. There is risk (villain could have AA or an unlikely Q♥ for quads/full house), but my Q blocks many Q-combos and those very strong hands are relatively rare. I’m OOP and the stacks are deep, so I’ll lead for value to charge A-highs and medium pairs while keeping bluffs in. A ~2/3-pot bet gives good value and information: a call means I can continue extracting; a sizable raise likely indicates a full house/quads and I’ll re-evaluate (and be prepared to fold to very large aggression). Bet 500 into the 750 pot.

Raising to 1500 with top pair and strong kicker (AK on Ah Qc Qd). Minni's 500 flop bet is relatively small, indicating he likely has marginal strength (weaker Ace, pocket pair, or semi-bluff). My AK is ahead of most hands in his range. Raising gains information, builds the pot with premium equity, and applies pressure. This is an aggressive but justified play at a high roller level with sufficient stack depth.

Board A♥ Q♣ Q♦; I hold Q♠4♠ — top trips. Claude’s raise to 1,500 over my 500 is strong, but the hands that actually beat me are few (mainly AA or AQ). My Q blocker removes many Q‑combos and quads are impossible, so AA/AQ combos are limited. The majority of his raising range (AK, A‑x, Kx, medium pairs, bluffs) is behind my trips. Calling the extra 1,000 (making my total committed 1,500 on the flop) keeps those hands in and maximizes extraction on later streets. A minimum reraise to 2,500 would fold out a lot of thin value and needlessly polarize the action against the small set of full‑house combos that beat me. Pot odds are favorable to call, so I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn — proceed cautiously if he shows very heavy continued aggression or the turn pairs the board.

I have Q♠4♠ — top trips on A♥ Q♣ Q♦. Claude’s flop raise to 1,500 polarized his range to very strong hands (AA, AQ) and thin value/bluffs (AK, Kx, medium pairs, some bluffs). The turn T♣ is dangerous because it suddenly gives pocket tens (TT) full houses as well — so the subset of hands that now beat me is small but non‑trivial (AA, AQ, TT). I want value from Ax/Kx/medium pairs and to induce bluffs, but I don’t want to bloat the pot and allow Claude to shove large with a full house. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in to be called later, and lets me fold cheaply to heavy turn aggression which is likely the full‑house range. Plan: check the turn. If Claude bets small (roughly ≤ ~½ pot, ~1.8–2.0k) I’ll call for value; if he jams or puts in a very large bet I’ll fold as he’s likely full‑house/AA/AQ.

Betting 2000 on the turn for value. I have top pair with top kicker (AK on Ah Qc Qd Tc). Minni's turn check suggests moderate strength - likely a weaker Ace, small pocket pair, or semi-bluff that missed. My AK is ahead of most hands in his range. This bet size (~53% of pot) is appropriate for extracting value while keeping the pot manageable for river decisions. My stack position (8300 total, 6300 remaining) allows for river flexibility.

I have Q♠4♠ on A♥ Q♣ Q♦ T♣ — top trips. Claude’s line (BTN open, flop 3‑bet, turn bet 2,000 ≈½ pot) is fairly polarized: the hands that realistically beat me now are few (AA, AQ and TT — the T on the turn makes pocket tens a full house), while a lot of his range is thinner value and bluffs (AK, A‑x, Kx, medium pairs). My Q in hand also blocks many Q‑combos, so quads/full houses are less likely. He chose a medium sizing, and per my plan I want to keep worse A‑hands/Kx/medium pairs and bluffs in to extract value on the river — raising would overly polarize the action and risk either folding out thin value or inducing a jam from the small set of full‑house combos that beat me. Calling 2,000 is the best line: it keeps his range wide, gets value from weaker hands, and allows me to fold cheaply to very large aggression on a later street if necessary.

I have Q♠4♠ on A♥ Q♣ Q♦ T♣ 2♠ — top trips but vulnerably blocked only one Q remains and several river/turn-full-house combos (AA, AQ, TT, 22 and Qh+X full/strong combos) can beat me. Claude’s line (BTN open, large flop raise over my c-bet, turn continuation) is polarized toward both very strong value and some bluffs; the turn T also gives pocket tens/twos full houses. Pot is 7,750 and Claude has ~6,300 behind, so any sizable bet risks an all‑in response that I’m unlikely to be happy calling given the remaining full‑house combos. Betting/raising would too strongly polarize my range (folding out thin value like AK/Kx or inducing a shove from the small set that absolutely crush me). By checking I (a) realize showdown equity against a wide portion of his range, (b) avoid bloating the pot and getting jammed by full houses, and (c) keep thin value hands and bluffs in to possibly bet/call. Therefore I’ll check to showdown (amount = 0).

River value bet with pair of Queens and AK kickers. Minni's river check indicates weakness or a showdown hand. I have a strong holding that beats most of his likely range (weak Aces, small pairs). Betting 3500 (approximately 45% of the 7750 pot) extracts value from his marginal holdings while sizing appropriately - not so large as to be exploitable, but substantial enough to build value. This bet allows Minni to make a mistake by calling with worse hands while leaving sufficient stack depth if he decides to raise (unlikely given his passive action).

I have Q♠4♠ on A♥ Q♣ Q♦ T♣ 2♠ — trips. Claude’s line (BTN open, big flop check‑raise, turn continuation, river ~3.5k) is polarized, but the bet size is not enormous relative to the pot. The hands that beat me (AA, AQ, TT, 22 and some Q‑combos with stronger kickers) are real but numerically limited; a large portion of his continuing range (AK, other A‑x, Kx, medium pairs, bluffs) is behind. Pot odds: calling 3,500 into a 11,250 pot requires only ~24% equity (3,500/(11,250+3,500) ≈ 23.7%), and my trips have far better equity than that versus a typical polarized raising range here. Raising would over‑polarize, fold out thin value, and invite a shove from the small subset that crush me. Call to realize showdown value and extract from worse A/K/medium pairs (if this is an effective all‑in for less because of his remaining stack, I will call the effective all‑in).

I am on the button with a pocket pair (3s 3c). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. Claude is relatively short-stacked at 27 big blinds, which means he might look to shove over an open, but I still need to raise to build the pot and take initiative. I will open for 300.

Facing a button open to 300 in a 3-handed game with Q♦T♥ in the small blind. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more (300 total) to call. QTo is worth defending here but I prefer to just call rather than 3-bet: I'm out of position postflop (vs both opponents), a larger 3-bet would bloat the pot OOP and could induce the short-stacked big blind to shove and force a tough decision. Calling is relatively cheap (risk 250 to win ~700 → ~2.8:1) and keeps the button’s range wide so I can realize equity and play postflop. I will call and re-evaluate on the flop (or fold to an isolating shove if the action gets extreme).

With 2♣5♦ (one of the worst possible hands in poker), this is a clear fold from the big blind. Despite the decent pot odds (3.5:1 to call 200), my hand has minimal equity against any reasonable range. Flash raised to 300 from the button (range likely includes many hands), and Minni called, indicating some hand strength. My 2-5 offsuit is dominated by most calling ranges and will be out-kicked in almost all scenarios. With a relatively short stack of 2700 chips, I need to preserve my capital for premium situations. Folding here minimizes losses on a negative EV hand.

I flat-called Q♥T♦ preflop and missed the flop — the board 4♥-7♣-9♣ gives me no pair, no club backdoor, and only very remote runner-runner straight possibilities. I’m OOP vs the button’s wide opening range, so leading here would bloat the pot with a very weak hand and make me vulnerable to check-raises or large C-bets. I’ll check and control the pot: if Flash fires a small-to-medium c-bet I can call to realize equity and look for a Q or safe turn cards; I’ll fold to a large pot-sized shove or overbet unless I improve. If he checks back I get to see a free turn and can continue with improved equity.

I opened pre-flop with a pocket pair of threes and was called by Minni in the small blind. The flop comes 9-7-4 with a flush draw. This board is somewhat coordinated, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I should often continue with a bet to represent a stronger range (like overpairs or high cards) and to fold out Minni's overcards (like KJ or AQ) which currently have significant equity against my small pair. If Minni raises, my hand is an easy fold, but a half-pot bet often wins the pot immediately here.

Call 350. I have Q♦T♥ on 4♥-7♣-9♣ — I missed but I’m heads-up vs the button’s wide opening/c-bet range. The bet is a small-to-medium c-bet and I’m getting ~25% pot odds (350 to win 1,400), so a call is justified to realize equity and look for a Q/T or a safe turn. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by raising; raising here would invite difficult decisions and reduce my ability to fold to strong action. Plan: call and re-evaluate on the turn — raise or value-bet if I pair up, fold to large aggression or overbet barrels on a brick, and proceed cautiously on club/connected cards that complete obvious draws.

I have Q♦T♥ on 4♥-7♣-9♣-7♠ and missed — no pair, no club draw, just two overcards. Flash checked to me on the turn after c-betting the flop. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that has little fold equity; a probing bet can be raised and put me in difficult spots. Checking controls the pot (current pot 1,400), preserves my stack, and lets me see a free river to try and hit a Q or T or pot-control a showdown. If Flash leads the river I can fold to large aggression and only call reasonable-sized bets given pot odds. Therefore I check.

The turn pairs the seven, which is a card that could reasonably be in Minni's pre-flop calling range from the small blind (such as 78s or 67s). Since Minni called my flop c-bet, it is likely she has at least a piece of the board or a draw. My pocket threes have very little showdown value here and are unlikely to fold out a better hand like a nine or a seven. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a river card for free, potentially realizing what little equity I have left if the river doesn't complete any draws.

Board: 4h 7c 9c 7s Jh. I hold Qd Th — missed the river and have no pair or meaningful showdown edge. Pot is 1400 and it’s checked to me. Flash was the preflop raiser, c-bet the flop (I called) and then checked the turn — a line consistent with many made hands (9x, 7x, Jx) and some turn/passive lines with overcards. Betting here as a bluff is thin: many hands that beat Q-high will call, and I have poor blockers and no fold equity against a player who can have pairs. With deep stacks relative to the pot, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is to check to showdown and avoid spewing chips with a marginal hand. If Flash bets river I’ll fold to medium/large bets and only consider calling very small probes that offer correct pot odds.

On this board of 9-7-4-7-J, my pocket threes have very little showdown value. However, Minni has checked three times. My flop c-bet was called, which suggests she has a piece of the board (a 9, a 7, a 4, or a draw). While she checked the river, it is unlikely she will fold a 9 or a 7 to a single bet, and I might still win against some missed draws (like Ac6c or 65s) by checking. Betting here as a bluff with 33 is risky because I don't block many of her calling hands. Checking back allows me to get to showdown for free.

I’m on the button with J♣4♦ in a 3-handed spot. With only the blinds in the pot (150) and lots of fold equity from position, an open-raise is the standard play to seize initiative and isolate the short-stacked SB. A 3x BB open to 300 is consistent with the table’s sizing and is large enough to pressure Claude (SB, ~26.5bb) while still not committing too much if Flash (BB, deep) defends or 3-bets. I’ll open to 300 to steal the blinds and play the flop in position. If Claude jams his stack (~2650) I’ll fold this weak offsuit hand; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold as well and avoid getting tangled in a big pot out of position. If called, I’ll proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting favorable textures and folding to strong resistance when behind.

JQ is a premium hand that should be played aggressively from the small blind, especially against a button raise from Minni who has shown frequent aggression. With a relatively short stack of 2,650, this is an ideal spot to put chips in with a strong hand. Raising to 800 serves to isolate Minni, potentially fold the big blind, and apply pressure while maintaining some stack depth. This aggressive play maximizes my equity with a quality hand in a favorable opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sixes. Minni, the chip leader, opened from the button, and Claude, who is extremely short-stacked (only 1,900 chips total, or 19 BBs), 3-bet to 800 from the small blind. This 3-bet represents nearly half of Claude's remaining stack, meaning he is essentially pot-committed. Pocket sixes are in a difficult spot here; against a standard 3-bet range (high pairs and big overcards), I am at best flipping and at worst heavily dominated. Furthermore, Minni is still to act behind me and has a massive stack, which creates a high-variance 'sandwich' situation. If I call the 700, I am likely to see a 3-way flop where I'll be out of position and forced to fold on most boards unless I hit a set. Given that the implied odds for set-mining aren't sufficient against Claude's small stack and the risk of Minni 4-betting or pressuring me post-flop, folding is the most prudent professional play to preserve my 120 BB stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♣4♦ and Claude (SB) 3-bet to 800, leaving him ~1,100 behind. J4o is a very weak, easily dominated hand vs a SB 3-bet range that will include lots of Ax/Kx/pairs and shove-capable hands given his short stack. Calling 500 to play postflop is marginal — although I have position, I’ll be in lots of uncomfortable spots if he jams or continues; 4-betting as a bluff is thin because he can just shove and I’ll face a tough decision with junk. This is not a spot to gamble with J4o — preserve a big stack for better spots and fold.

Folding 6-9 offsuit with a short 31BB stack. While position is valuable in 3-handed play, this hand lacks the equity to warrant a call. Short stack strategy dictates playing only premium hands or pushing with reasonable holdings, not speculating with weak hands. Better spots will come.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c8c, a strong speculative hand. Since the Button folded, I am heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot with a hand that flops well. I will raise to 300 total.

Fold. 3♠2♣ is a very weak, poorly playable hand out of position facing a standard SB open to 300. Calling 200 requires ~33% equity to break even — 3-2o will be dominated by a large portion of the opener's range and will put me in tough post‑flop spots OOP. A reraise would be a thin bluff with poor blockers. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with J2o, which is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, J2o is near the bottom of my range. Claude in the big blind has a relatively short stack (30 BBs) and might be looking for an opportunity to shove, while Minni is a very active player with a huge stack. There's no reason to contest this pot with such a poor hand.

I have 8♦4♣ — a very weak, low-playability hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Pot is 150 and I must put in 50 more to complete (100 total) or raise to at least 200. Claude is a short stack (3,000 ~30bb) and could easily shove or play back aggressively; raising to steal risks getting committed with junk, and calling simply sees a flop OOP with little equity. Folding and conceding the small blind saves chips and avoids a marginal, high-variance spot.

I'm on the button with K♦7♥ in a 3-handed pot — position is a big advantage and my open-raising range should be wide. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 is passive; opening applies pressure to a fairly short-stacked SB (Claude ~31bb) and a deep-stacked BB (Flash ~120bb). I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300) — big enough to get folds and protect my hand, small enough to avoid committing too much if I face a big 3-bet. Plan: fold to a shove from SB (likely dominated risk/reward), and if called use positional advantage to c-bet favorable boards or proceed cautiously on heavy resistance from the big stack.

Folding Js7c from the small blind. This is a weak, unsuited hand that plays poorly out of position. While Minni has been aggressive, calling 250 chips (leaving me with 2850) with Jack-seven is not profitable in the long run. The hand has poor equity against most raising ranges, and I'm already in a relatively short-stacked position (3100 chips). I should preserve my chips for premium hands and better positions. Folding here is the mathematically sound decision.

In a 3-handed game, defending the Big Blind with suited hands against a Button open is standard due to the favorable pot odds. Minni is an aggressive chip leader, but with [Qc 2c], I have enough equity and playability to see a flop, even out of position. I am getting 2.25:1 odds on a call (200 to win a total pot of 450).

I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw on a J-T-3 rainbow board. Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and is the chip leader. Standard play in the Big Blind is to check to the raiser to see how they react, as my hand is currently weak but has some marginal equity and overcard potential.

I am the preflop aggressor in position with K♦7♥ on a T♠ J♣ 3♦ rainbow flop. My range contains many broadways and overcards, and the BB’s cold-call preflop then check on the flop looks like a wide, often marginal calling range (weak broadways, small pairs, Jx/Tx, connectors). I have one overcard and decent fold equity heads-up, so I want to seize the initiative with a small continuation bet to fold out unpaired hands and keep the pot manageable if I face resistance. I will bet ~1/3–40% of the pot (250) to maximize fold equity while preserving stack depth for postflop play. Plan: if he folds I collect the pot; if called I proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to strong check-raises or large turn/river aggression unless I improve.

On a board of 3d-Ts-Jc, I have Queen-high with one overcard (the Queen), a backdoor flush draw (Qc, 2c, and Jc), and marginal backdoor straight possibilities. Minni, an aggressive chip leader, has bet 250 into a 650 pot, providing me with pot odds of approximately 3.6:1 (requiring ~22% equity). Given the small sizing and my backdoor equity, calling is a mathematically acceptable play to see a turn card, especially since Minni's range for a small C-bet is wide and I can potentially outplay her on later streets if I pick up more equity or if the board texture changes.

On the turn, the board has paired the three (3s), and Minni has checked back after I checked. My hand, Queen-high with a backdoor club draw, has very little showdown value. However, Minni's check-back often indicates a marginal hand or a draw that she's willing to take a free card with. While I could attempt a bluff, the board texture (J-T-3-3) still connects with many hands in her opening range. Since I have zero equity if called and she has position, I will check to see a free river card and potentially improve or evaluate based on the final board state.

I have K♦7♥ and missed the turn — the board paired (3♦/T♠/J♣/3♠) and I still only have king-high. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor and induced a call, so Flash’s range here is wide (Jx/Tx, Ax, Kx, small pairs, missed broadways). Betting as a bluff is thinly effective — many of his callers beat me (Jx/Tx) or will simply call with Ace/King-high, and a lead from him would represent a strong/wet hand I don’t want to face out of position. With deep stacks and no improvement, pot control and position are best: check back the turn, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the river (call small probes, fold to large pressure unless I improve or get a clear read).

On the river, the board is 3d-Ts-Jc-3s-8c. I have Queen-high (Qc 2c), which has virtually no showdown value. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on the turn, which often indicates she has a marginal hand like a small pair or Ace-high that she is looking to take to a cheap showdown. Given that she is the chip leader and has shown a tendency to play reasonably logically in previous hands, a bluff here is unlikely to succeed against her marginal showdown range. I will check and hope to either win against a worse high card (unlikely) or simply concede the pot without losing more chips, as my hand didn't improve on the river.

Final board 3♦ T♠ J♣ 3♠ 8♣ and I hold K♦7♥ — no pair, just king-high. I c-bet the flop and was called, then both of us checked the turn and the BB checked to me on the river. His line looks like a wide calling/checking range (Jx/Tx, small pairs, some Ax/Kx and air). Betting here has very little fold equity — most hands that called the flop will still call a river bluff — so a bluff would be thin and high-variance. Better to control the pot and realize equity: check behind and take the showdown rather than bloating the pot with no showdown value.

I'm on the button with K8o, which is a playable hand from this position. The blinds (Flash with 11500 and Minni with 25250) both have deeper stacks. I'm relatively short (3100), so I need to take initiative while I have fold equity.

K8o is a standard button raise hand - it's strong enough to apply pressure against the blinds and has reasonable equity against their ranges. By raising to 300 (3x BB), I'm:
1. Taking control of the hand and playing in position postflop
2. Applying pressure to win the blinds outright
3. Committing only ~10% of my stack with decent equity
4. Using my positional advantage to widen my range appropriately

This is a standard aggressive button play that maximizes my tournament equity.

J4o is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind against a button raise. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive, this hand realizes its equity poorly out of position, and the risk of being 3-bet by the deep-stacked Big Blind (Minni) or forced into a difficult post-flop spot against Claude's 28BB stack is too high. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I have A♠J♠ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (pot ~450). This is a strong, well-blocked hand that plays well both for value and for fold equity. Claude on the button likely has a fairly wide opening range, and he only has ~2,800 behind, so a standard 3-bet to apply pressure and isolate is correct. I’ll raise to 900 total — big enough to deny equity to many speculative hands and pick up folds, but small enough relative to stacks that calling a shove is reasonable. If he calls we’ll play postflop OOP and c-bet or control the pot based on texture; if he folds I take the pot immediately.

Folding K8o to Minni's 3-bet. While I'm short-stacked and aggressive is usually correct, K8o is a weak holding with only ~30-35% equity against her 3-bet range. Calling 600 leaves me with just 2200 chips in a pot where I'm significantly behind. At 25 of 150 hands, I should preserve my remaining 2500 chips and look for stronger hands in better spots. Making -EV all-ins won't help my tournament position.

I am on the button with 2h 8c, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While position allows for occasional steal attempts, the current table dynamics involve an aggressive chip leader (Minni) in the Small Blind and a relatively short-stacked but active player (Claude) in the Big Blind. Risking chips with such a poor hand is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up preflop with K♠?K♥? (Kh Qd) in the small blind — this is a strong hand to play aggressively. I have a huge stack (256 BB) vs Claude’s short ~27 BB, so I should apply pressure and try to isolate him. A standard raise to 200 (2x BB total) accomplishes that: it wins the pot immediately a good portion of the time, keeps risk controlled, and if Claude shoves I have good equity vs a wide shove range and can call comfortably. If he just calls I keep positional advantage postflop. Limping or folding is too passive here. Raise to 200.

I'm holding AK (Ace-King of clubs and spades), one of the premium starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from Minni in the small blind, this is an excellent opportunity to build the pot and apply pressure. 

At 2700 chips (27bb), I'm still reasonably deep but want to capitalize on this premium hand. AK benefits significantly from building the pot preflop - it's a hand that wants to win now or get value from weaker holdings.

Key considerations:
- AK is the 3rd best starting hand in poker (after AA and KK)
- Minni's raise to 200 seems standard but exploitable with premium hands
- The 50/100 blind structure means this is a natural inflection point to apply aggression
- I want to narrow down Minni's range and get value from medium pairs or weaker broadway hands

A raise to 600 (3x her raise) is strong but not reckless. This builds the pot to ~900 and maintains position advantages while committing only ~22% of my stack. This is the correct aggressive play with a premium holding in the big blind against a moderate raise.

I opened the small blind to 200 with K♥Q♦ and Claude (BB, ~2,200) 3-bet to 600. Pot is 800 and it’s cheap for me to apply pressure. Claude is short-stacked so his 3-bet is likely a fairly wide, often non-premium range. I don’t want to play a marginal KQ out of position postflop; I can leverage my huge stack to force a fold or isolate him. Raising to 2,200 (effectively putting his stack at risk) maximizes fold equity, avoids playing OOP, and keeps the decision simple — if he calls we’re heads-up for his stack and KQ has reasonable equity vs his calling range. The extra risk (≈2,000 chips) is tiny relative to my stack but can win the pot immediately. Action: 4-bet to 2,200 total.

All-in with AK. With 2200 chips and facing a 1600 call, committing just 1600 leaves only 600 chips, making future decisions impossible. Going all-in avoids the commit-trap and maximizes fold equity. AK is a premium hand that has reasonable equity against Minni's likely 3-betting range (premium pairs, AQ, AK, broadways). This is the only mathematically sound option given the stack depth and hand strength.

Claude 5-bet to 2,800 after my 4-bet to 2,200, so he’s effectively all‑in and I only have to call an extra 600. The pot is about 5,000, so I need roughly 600/5,600 ≈ 10.7% equity to break even — KQo has plenty of equity vs a reasonable short‑stack shove range and Claude’s line (3‑bet, then a small shove) looks wide, not only AA/KK. My stack (23,500) is huge relative to this decision, so the extra 600 is trivial and calling is +EV. Raising isn’t meaningful because he’s all‑in; folding would be too tight. I’ll call the 600 (total commitment 2,800) to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with 10♣2♣. This is a playable, suited hand in position and I want to be aggressive to pick up the blind or take the initiative postflop. A standard min-raise to 2x the big blind (200 total) gives me fold equity, keeps the pot reasonable with deep stacks, and disguises my range. If called I’ll play the flop in position and c-bet many dry flops; if Flash 3-bets large (e.g. ~3x my raise or bigger) I’ll fold this hand, but I may call smaller 3-bets to realize equity due to the suitedness.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 2s, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although the price to call is only 100 chips and I am getting 3:1 odds, 8-2 offsuit is extremely difficult to play out of position, and I would rather preserve my stack for a more favorable starting hand rather than defending with junk.

K8o is a solid hand to open with from the button in a heads-up situation. Raising to 300 (3x BB) allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a post-flop play with a hand that has reasonable showdown value.

Heads-up in the big blind with Jh2h versus a button open to 300. This is a marginal but suited hand that plays reasonably well HEADS-UP — it has nut-flush potential and decent showdown equity vs a wide button opening range. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives ~3:1 pot odds (about 25% equity needed), and J2s should have enough equity vs a typical open range to defend. A 3-bet as a bluff is too thin here (hand is easily dominated by the button’s broadway/Ax portion) and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position. Plan: call, see the flop, proceed aggressively on favorable flops (heart backdoor or pair of J) and fold to heavy action on dry misses. Commit total of 300 chips.

Dry, paired 9s on the flop with Jh2h — I missed. This board heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (9x, pocket pairs, some 6x) and there are no hearts to make my backdoor draw meaningful. I’ll control the pot and induce information by checking. If Flash bets small I may call occasionally for pot odds and backdoor equity, but I will fold to a sizeable c-bet or re-raise. Checking keeps the pot manageable out of position and preserves my stack for better spots.

On a paired board like 9d-9c-6s, I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Although K-high has some showdown value, a small continuation bet (C-bet) of 1/3 the pot (200 chips) is effective here. It puts pressure on Minni's non-paired hands and allows me to take down the pot immediately without overcommitting. If Minni raises, I can easily fold knowing I am likely beaten by a 9 or a pocket pair.

Heads‑up on the flop with J♥2♥ vs button’s small c-bet on 9♦9♣6♠. Flash bet 200 into the pot (pot = 800), so calling 200 would cost me 200 to win 1000 — about 20% break‑even equity. My hand has realistic improvement chances: I have 6 outs to pair either hole card (3 jacks + 3 deuces) giving ~24% to hit a pair by the river, plus backdoor flush chance (two hearts) of ~5.1% — combined roughly ~29% chance to make at least a pair or a flush by showdown. That exceeds the pot‑odds threshold, so a call is +EV here. Raising as a bluff is thin (hand is easily dominated and I’m OOP) and I want to keep the pot controllable given the opponent’s range and stack sizes. Plan: call the 200 to see the turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (jack/heart/pair), and play aggressively if I connect.

Check. I have J♥2♥ on 9♦9♣6♠7♥ and haven’t improved to a pair; my only clean outs are 3 Jacks + 3 Deuces (6 outs ≈ 13% on the river). The turn 7♥ doesn’t give me a real draw (a five‑card heart flush is impossible now because the flop had no hearts), and the preflop raiser’s range (9x, other pocket pairs, some 6x, broadways) often dominates me. Betting OOP with a weak, largely showdown‑dependent hand would bloat the pot and force difficult decisions; I’ll control the pot, check to the river, and re-evaluate if I see a river card or a bet. If I improve on the river (pair J or 2) I’ll value accordingly; if I don’t I’ll fold to large river aggression but may call small bets given my limited outs.

I have King-high on a board that is now quite coordinated (9-9-6-7). While I had the initiative pre-flop and on the flop, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. The 7h on the turn doesn't help my hand. Checking back is the safest play here to control the pot size and see a free river, as I still have some showdown value against her air/floats, and I avoid being check-raised by a 9 or a slow-played straight/full house.

I improved to top pair on the river (J♥) and the opponent (preflop aggressor) has shown weakness: small c‑bet on the flop, then checked turn and checked the river. That line is consistent with marginal pairs and missed overcards/draws that will often call a small value bet but fold to large pressure. With the pot at 1000 a small value bet (~30% pot) maximizes thin value from worse pairs (7x, 6x, some T/x and Jx combos) while keeping the pot controllable OOP and minimizing loss to sets/trips. If Flash check‑raises big, I’ll assume a strong hand and fold. Bet 300 to extract value without bloating the pot.

The river Jack is a problematic card as it likely hits Minni's range of floats from the flop (like JT, QJ, or KJ). Her lead of 300 into a 1000 pot suggests she either improved to a pair of Jacks, is betting a 9 for value, or is value-betting a small pair like 6s or 7s. While the pot odds are roughly 4:1, my King-high has almost no showdown value against a betting range on this coordinated board. Even if she is bluffing, she could be doing so with Ace-high or a missed straight draw that contains an 8 (making a pair of 8s). I will fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♣6♠ — this is a clear open-raise hand. I want to take the initiative and use my positional advantage post-flop rather than limp/call. A standard 3x BB sizing is ideal here: it gives fold equity, builds a playable pot with an ace-blocker, and matches common table sizing so I won’t be giving cheap odds to speculative hands. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size and tendencies, but opening now keeps pressure on his wider BB range.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. In a heads-up situation, K4o has reasonable equity against a standard button/SB opening range. However, out of position, it is a difficult hand to play. Considering the pot odds (200 to call into a 400 pot), I am getting 2:1 on a call. Given that Minni has a significantly larger stack and has been active, I will choose to defend my blind here to see a flop, but I must be cautious of her aggression.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of 4c-Jh-9c. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many overcards and existing better hands in Minni's range (J-x, 9-x, or pocket pairs). By checking, I control the pot size and allow Minni to potentially bluff or check behind with weaker hands. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor out of position with only bottom pair is generally suboptimal here.

I continuation-bet here to take advantage of position and the initiative I established preflop. My A♣ is a useful blocker to a lot of Ax combinations and I have the betting lead; the flop (4♣ J♥ 9♣) misses a large portion of the BB’s defending range (small pairs, Kx, Qx, many missed connectors). A ~50% pot c-bet (300 into 600) applies pressure, buys fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds we win the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously, fold to a strong raise unless the turn gives me real equity or improved showdown value).

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of 4c-Jh-9c. Minni has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. With bottom pair and a King kicker in a heads-up pot, I have enough equity to continue, especially receiving 3:1 pot odds. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, so calling is the most appropriate action to maintain pot control and see how the turn develops. I must remain cautious as the board is coordinated with straight and flush draws.

On the turn, the Jack of clubs pairs the board. My hand remains bottom pair (fours) with a King kicker, which now plays as two pair (Jacks and Fours). Being out of position, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board pairing. If she checks back, I can likely get to a relatively cheap showdown. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the bet sizing against the likelihood she has a Jack, a nine, or is bluffing with a missed draw. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor on a board that just paired the top card rarely benefits a marginal hand like mine.

I opened preflop and c-bet the flop per plan; Flash called. The turn (J♣) pairs the board and brings a third club, but I still only have ace-high (A♣6♠) — I haven’t improved to a pair or a secure draw. Flash checked, which can be either medium-strength holdings (9x, 4x, K/Qx) or a trap (trips or a made club flush), so betting here would bloat the pot with very little equity and expose me to a check‑raise or a turn shove. I’ll control the pot and see the river: check and re-evaluate based on the river card and any bet size (call small rivers where I expect worse, fold to large river pressure unless I improve).

On this board of 4c-Jh-9c-Jc-9s, my pair of fours has been counterfeited by the two higher pairs on the board (Jacks and Nines). I am now effectively playing the board's two pair with a King kicker. Minni checked back the turn, which may indicate she doesn't have a Jack or a strong club draw, but she could easily have an Ace in her range which would give her a better five-card hand (JJ99A vs JJ99K). Since I am out of position and my hand has moderate showdown value but limited strength to call a large raise or get value from worse hands, checking is the most logical play. I will look to see a cheap showdown or evaluate based on her bet sizing.

Check. I only have A-high (A♣6♠) and the river makes a double-paired board (J J 9 9 4). Any Jack or Nine in Flash’s hand is now a full house or quads and crushes me; he called my flop bet and then played passively to the river, so his checking line includes both strong made hands and medium showdown holdings (Kx/Qx, air) that I can beat. Betting here would be a thin bluff: it will rarely get value from worse and risks a check-raise from a very strong holding. With a small pot (1,200) relative to stacks, controlling the pot and checking to showdown is the highest EV line. If Flash now leads I’ll re-evaluate — folding to large river pressure and only calling small bets when the price and line suggest a bluff.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 95-offsuit. While this is a heads-up situation where I should generally play a wide range of hands, 95-offsuit is significantly below the average strength for an opening hand. Minni, the Big Blind, has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #12, Hand #26), often 3-betting or making large calls. Given her stack size advantage and aggressive tendencies, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a more favorable spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦9♠ — a strong, raise-worthy hand in this spot. We’ve already posted the 50 SB and face 50 to call into a 150 pot. Raising to take the initiative is the best play: it applies pressure to the BB’s wide defending range, builds a pot when we’re often ahead, and lets us play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — big enough to generate fold equity and discourage super–light calls but not so large as to bloat the pot unnecessarily with deep stacks. Plan: if called, continue with aggression when appropriate; if Flash 3-bets big, re-evaluate and likely fold Q9o versus a large 3-bet, but consider calling smallish 3-bets for position and postflop playability. (Total commitment = 300 chips; we add 250 more on top of the 50 SB.)

In a heads-up situation, King-Nine offsuit is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise. Folding would be too exploitable. While I could 3-bet for value or as a semi-bluff, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has decent high-card value and play from there, keeping the pot manageable against a larger stack.

I have top pair with a Nine kicker on a board that contains several draws (diamonds, straight possibilities). As the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand is strong enough to check-call most bets, as Minni's button-opening range is wide and I am likely ahead of her air and some of her draws. Checking keeps the pot manageable while allowing her the opportunity to bluff or value bet worse hands.

We were the preflop raiser to 300 and Flas h checked the flop (J♦ K♦ 4♣). Holding Q♦9♠ on the button I have position and initiative, plus reasonable equity (Q gives 3 queen outs; 4 tens give a straight draw — ~7 outs to improve by river, roughly ~28% — plus a tiny runner‑runner diamond possibility). Versus a wide BB defending range a continuation bet is the +EV play to fold out weaker holdings (small pairs, A‑high, missed broadways) while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll bet about half the pot (300) — enough to apply pressure but not commit the stack. Plan: if I get a small call I’ll continue on a favorable turn (Q/T/♦) for value; fold or strongly re‑evaluate to a large check‑raise or surge in aggression from Flash.

I have top pair with a King on a Jack-King-Four board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot, and folding is not an option. While my Nine kicker isn't the best, Minni's continuation bet could come from a wide range of hands including diamonds, straight draws, or air. Raising might force out her bluffs and isolate me against better Kings or sets. Calling is the most balanced play, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to extract value from her bluffs or weaker hands on later streets.

On the turn, the 3c doesn't significantly change the board texture. I still have top pair (Kings) with a 9 kicker. Minni has checked back, which could indicate a pot-controlling hand like a Jack, a drawing hand that wants a free card (like diamonds or a straight draw), or a complete air-ball. Since she checked, I have the option to lead or check again. Checking again is a solid play here to maintain deception and potentially induce a bluff on the river or to see a cheap showdown if she has a better King or a Jack. By checking, I'm keeping the pot small and not overplaying my hand against a stack that significantly covers me.

Turn is a blank (3♣). Holding Q♦9♠ I have not improved — my backdoor diamond possibility is dead with one card to come, leaving only ~6 outs (~12–14%) to pair on the river. Flash called my half‑pot c‑bet on the flop and now checks, which is consistent with a wide, often calling/pot‑control range (Kx, Jx, medium pairs, some draws). I don’t have enough equity or reliable fold equity to profitably barreI here against that range, so I’ll exercise pot control and see the river for free. Plan: if Flash bets a small amount on the river I’ll evaluate calling (with implied outs); fold to large sizing that represents strong made hands.

I have top pair with Kings, but the Ace on the river is a definite scare card that likely improves a portion of Minni's button-opening range that might have floated the flop. After she checked back the turn, she could be pot-controlling a medium-strength hand or waiting to see if she hits her draws. Since the Ace completes some straights (QT) and gives any Ace-high floats the top pair, betting into her doesn't offer much value as I'm unlikely to get called by worse and might get raised by better. Checking allows me to reach a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff from her missed diamond draws.

Check. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop (J♦ K♦ 4♣) and got a call; both of us checked the turn and Flash checks the river as well. I have Q♦9♠ — just queen‑high with no pair — and the A♥ on the river makes many opponent holdings (A, Kx, Jx, small pairs) beat me. Flash’s line looks like a wide, medium‑strength checking/calling range that will call small river bets, so I lack reliable fold equity and have no value to extract. Betting here is a marginal bluff with poor odds of success; pot control and seeing showdown for free is the +EV line. If he bets into me I’ll fold to a meaningful sizing; if he checks through we show down.

In a heads-up situation, Jack-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the Button/Small Blind. A standard 3x open (300 chips) allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on favorable boards. My goal is to rebuild my stack against Minni, who currently holds a significant lead.

I’m in the big blind with A♥2♦ facing a 300 open from the button (pot 400, 200 to call). Heads-up A2o is worth defending: the Ace blocker and decent equity vs a wide opening range make a flat call profitable. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% required equity, which A2o usually meets against a button steal. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3‑bet; I’ll reserve 3‑bets for stronger/suited aces or pairs. Call keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and lets me play postflop with extraction or controlled aggression depending on the board.

Flop: A♣ 2♠ 5♣ — I hold A♥2♦, two pair (Aces and Twos). Heads‑up vs a button opener this is a very strong hand and I want to extract value while keeping the pot controllable. Villain’s opening range contains lots of worse A‑x, Kx and medium pocket pairs that will call a modest flop bet; there are few realistic draws (only backdoor clubs) and only rare combos (55, 22, A5) that beat me. Betting denies free cards, charges backdoor equity, and gets called by worse hands. A half‑pot bet (300 into 600) is the right balance — gets value from worse aces and pairs, keeps the pot manageable vs sets, and prevents villain from comfortably taking the initiative. Action: value bet 300.

Minni led out with a donk bet (betting into the pre-flop raiser) for half the pot on an Ace-high board. I have Jack-high with no pair and no significant draws. While Minni could be testing me or on a flush draw, I have no equity to continue, and trying to bluff her off an Ace or a draw in this position is high-risk. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 9♦4♦ — a marginal but suited hand with some playability. In heads-up I should be opening a wide range to pressure the big blind and avoid giving them correct pot odds to see a flop. I’ll raise a standard 3bb sizing to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so add 250) — this is big enough to pick up the blind often, keeps the pot manageable, preserves my deep stack, and gives me position postflop. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold this hand; if called I’ll play postflop with positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-3 offsuit. Minni (the Button/Small Blind) has raised to 3 times the big blind. While the pot odds are 2:1 to call, 8-3 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a player who has a significantly larger stack and is playing aggressively. Defending such a weak range usually leads to difficult post-flop decisions and chip bleed. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to contest the pot.

Nine-deuce offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although playing heads-up generally requires a wide range of hands from the button, this hand lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. Given Minni's active playstyle and the significant stack deficit, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Ac3h — this is a hand I want to play aggressively. I have positional advantage postflop and an ace which performs well heads-up, so calling 50 is too passive and lets the BB see cheap flops with a wide range. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative, get fold equity, and build a pot I can play postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (fold to very large 3-bets, call smaller 3-bets to see the flop and use position), and on most flops I’ll c-bet favorable textures or control the pot when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. Minni has raised to 3x from the Button/Small Blind. In a heads-up scenario, T8o is a hand with decent playability and enough raw equity to defend against a standard open, especially with the deep stacks we currently have. I only need to call 200 to win a total pot of 600 (offering 2:1 odds). Closing the action pre-flop is standard here to see a flop and realize my equity.

I have middle pair (8s) with a Ten kicker. On a board of K-8-5 rainbow, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard and most balanced play. My hand has decent showdown value and can comfortably call a continuation bet from Minni, but leading out here would likely only get called by better hands or fold out hands I currently beat.

Heads-up on the button after I opened preflop; board is 8s 5h Kc and the BB checked to me. I have Ac3h — Ace-high with virtually no draw, but I have position and the initiative. Flash’s calling range from the big blind is wide (broadways, pocket pairs, some Kx), so a continuation bet will fold a lot of unpaired hands and extract value from worse aces/clean up small pairs. I’ll bet a moderate 50% pot (300) to balance fold equity and pot control — big enough to deny correct odds to many drawing/calling hands but not so big I overcommit with a marginal Ace. If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (blank → decide on a second barrel or pot control; big raise → fold to respect strong holdings).

I have middle pair (Eights) with a Ten kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni's half-pot continuation bet is standard for a pre-flop aggressor. Folding middle pair to a single bet would be too tight, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands like a King or a stronger Eight. Calling allows me to keep the pot small and realize my equity, or reach a showdown with a hand that still has significant value.

The Jack of Diamonds is another overcard to my pair of Eights. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to Minni. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value against a range that includes many Kings and Jacks. Checking allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to call based on Minni's action.

I opened preflop and c-bet the flop (300) and Flash called; pot is 1,200. The turn Jd is a blank for my Ac3h — I still only have Ace-high with no draw. Flash’s check is consistent with a wide calling range (Kx, pocket pairs, some A‑x and broadways) that often continues versus a turn bet, so a second barrel would be a thin value bet and risks bloating the pot when I’m frequently behind. I’m in position with plenty of fold equity on later streets, so I’ll control the pot now and check back the turn to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the river (call small river bets vs bluffs/weak aces, fold to big river aggression).

The board now shows three overcards (King, Jack, and Ace) to my pair of Eights. While Minni's check on the turn might suggest weakness, the Ace on the river is a card that hits a pre-flop raiser's range significantly. My pair of Eights has some showdown value against missed straight draws or lower pocket pairs, but it cannot realistically bet for value or expect to fold out a better hand. Checking is the most prudent play to attempt to reach showdown cheaply.

The river Ad pairs my Ac so I’ve improved to top pair (A3) but with a very weak kicker. Flash checked the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn — a line consistent with Kx, medium pocket pairs, or slow-played marginal hands. Those hands will often call a modest value bet; stronger Ax or trips/full houses are possible but less likely given Flash’s passive line (no preflop 3‑bet, no raises on later streets).

I’ll bet for value but keep the size controlled so I: 1) extract calls from Kx, pocket pairs and weaker aces that I beat, 2) don’t grossly overcommit and induce calls from the rarer Ax combinations that dominate my Kicker, and 3) fold out pure air. A half‑pot bet (600 into 1200) accomplishes that balance. If I get raised very large I’ll fold (My kicker is exploitable); if I get called I expect to win at showdown often enough to make this +EV.

On the river, the board has completed with three overcards (Ace, King, Jack) to my pair of Eights. While Minni checked the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a surrender, the Ace on the river is an ideal card for her to represent as the pre-flop aggressor. My hand has been reduced to fourth pair, and it loses to any Ace, King, Jack, or the straight (QT). Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds, Minni's bet of 600 into 1200 is a polarized action that forces me to have a much stronger bluff-catcher than a pair of eights on this specific board texture. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a heads-up situation, Q4 offsuit is a playable hand from the button. To maintain initiative and exert pressure on the big blind, I will put in a standard open raise of 3x. This allows me to take down the pot immediately or play the rest of the hand in position.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button, I'm in the big blind with 9♦2♣ — a very weak, poorly connected offsuit hand. To continue costs 200 into a 400 pot (≈33% break-even), and out of position my realized equity and postflop playability are both very poor. Flash's open from the button is likely wide, but 9-2o is still dominated too often and I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding. I fold and wait for a better spot (would defend or 3-bet with suited connectors, broadways or pairs).

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should open a very wide range. T♠2♦ is marginal but playable in position and has enough post‑flop playability to justify an open. I want to seize the initiative, gain fold equity, and avoid letting the BB see a cheap flop, so I’ll make a standard 2x BB open (min‑raise to 200 total). This size keeps the pot controllable with a marginal hand while still putting pressure on Flash. If he 3‑bets large I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet or control the pot depending on the flop texture.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Five of Spades. The action is heads-up against Minni, who has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. This requires me to call an additional 100 chips into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. While the price is relatively cheap, T5o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a significantly larger stack. Given the long-term goal of maximizing my stack over 150 hands, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand rather than bleeding chips with marginal holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s-7h, which is a very weak starting hand. While the cost to call is only 50 chips, this hand plays poorly post-flop and doesn't offer much potential for improvement. Minni has a significant stack advantage and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. Given the trashy nature of these cards, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the small blind). J♦6♠ is marginal but entirely playable in heads-up with position — I can open a wide range and leverage post‑flop initiative. The table’s standard open sizing has been ~300; I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 so I’ll put in 250 more) to get fold equity, isolate the big blind and play pots in position. If Flash 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate (usually fold this hand vs large 3‑bets, call small 3‑bets occasionally); if called I’ll use position to C‑bet selectively and control pot size.

I have pocket Eights (8c 8s), which is a very strong hand in a heads-up scenario. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand. While calling is an option to keep the pot small, a 3-bet allows me to define the strength of her range and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I'll raise to 900.

Flash 3‑bet to 900 is big and represents a fairly strong/polarized heads‑up range (strong broadways, Ax, pairs and some bluffs). The pot is 1,200; calling 600 would make it 1,800 so I need ~33% equity. J♦6♠ is frequently dominated and has poor post‑flop playability against that range; effective stack behind (8,850) is not large enough to justify speculative calls for implied odds. A 4‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and risky. I planned to fold to large 3‑bets, so I’ll fold and look for a better spot in position.

I am on the button (small blind) with Ace-Five offsuit. In heads-up play, this is a strong opening hand. I have position for the rest of the hand. Based on my previous play style in this tournament, a standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is consistent and puts pressure on the big blind while allowing me to take control of the pot.

Facing a 300 open from the button with 9♥5♣ in the big blind. Pot is 400, I must call 200 (total committed 300). In heads-up the opener’s range is wide, and my hand has reasonable showdown/draw equity vs a wide opening range; pot odds (~33%) justify a defend. I don’t want to 3-bet light with a marginal hand and invite a shove or large pot OOP — I’d rather keep the pot manageable and play postflop with my stack advantage (29,850 vs his 9,750). Plan: call to see a flop, continue aggressively if I hit a pair or good draw, otherwise control pot and fold to heavy pressure on dry, high-card boards.

Check. I have 9♣5♣? (9♥5♣) and completely missed this flop (A♣ A♦ 3♠) — no pair and no realistic draw. Flash opened wide preflop, but a double-A flop heavily favors any Ax in his range (he has trips) and also gives good turn/river equity to many broadway hands; I don’t want to inflate a pot out of position with a marginal hand. My preflop plan was to defend but keep pots under control on dry, high-card boards — so I’ll check and see how he behaves. If he checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate; if he fires a small probe (~≤1/3 pot) I might call once to realize equity, but I will fold to substantial pressure (pot-sized bets or shove) because my showdown chances are very poor. With a big stack advantage there will be better spots to apply pressure later, so avoid getting committed here.

I have flopped trips (three of a kind) with Ace-Five on a very dry board of Ac-Ad-3s. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a bet here often looks like a standard continuation bet and will likely force a fold from almost all of Minni's range that didn't connect with the board. By checking behind, I keep her range wide, disguise the strength of my hand, and hope to induce a bluff or get value on the turn or river if she catches a small piece of the board.

Board: A♣ A♦ 3♠ J♣. My hand: 9♥5♣. I have missed entirely — no pair, no realistic draw. Pot is 600 and there is no bet to face (to call 0). Flash checked the flop and shows the same passive line on the turn, which can be a wide range (many non-A holdings or slow-played Ax). Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity versus his calling range and would risk building a pot OOP where I have almost no showdown equity. With a large stack advantage there will be better spots to pressure him; I should avoid getting committed with junk. Plan: check and concede the initiative — fold to meaningful aggression on the turn/river, call only small probes if they offer good odds to realize any tiny equity.

I have trip Aces with a Five kicker. Checking the flop was a good move to disguise my hand strength and keep Minni's range wide. Now that she has checked a second time on the turn, I need to start extracting value. The Jack of clubs is a card that might have connected with some of her defending range (like QJ, JT, or KJ), or she might be holding a pocket pair that she's willing to bluff-catch with. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to build value and set up a potential river bet.

Flash checked the flop and then made a small, 400-chip probe on the turn into a 1,000-chip pot (≈40% pot). My hand (9♥5♣) has no pair and virtually no draw — I’m behind almost any Ax or Jx and only beat pure air/bluffs. Calling 400 to see the river costs very little relative to my 29.6k stack and requires only ≈22% equity to break even, so folding would be too tight. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: it’s unlikely to fold enough of Flash’s continuing range and would bloating the pot out of position with no equity. Plan: call the small turn bet to realize equity and reassess on the river — fold to a sizable river shove or big value bet, call tiny river probes only if the pot odds justify it, and extract value only if I improve to a pair on the river.

I have completely missed (9♥5♣) on A♣ A♦ 3♠ J♣ 4♦. Flash opened wide preflop, checked the flop, then made a small turn probe (400) that I called — his line includes Ax, some Jx, and lots of medium/weak holdings. Bluffing out of position on the river with no showdown equity is unlikely to be profitable (betting would need unrealistically high fold equity and simply builds a pot I’m a big underdog in if called). With a big stack advantage I should avoid marginal, high-variance spots; I’ll check to see showdown and fold to meaningful aggression (call only very small probes if the pot odds make it correct).

I have trip Aces with a Five kicker on a board of Ac-Ad-3s-Jc-4d. After I checked the flop and bet the turn, Minni called, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board, most likely a Jack or a mid-to-high pocket pair. The river 4d is a blank that doesn't change much. Since she checked to me again, I need to go for value. A bet of 800 into the 1400 pot is a standard value-sized bet that looks like it could be a missed draw or a thin value bet, making it likely for her to call with a Jack or a marginal pair.

I missed the board completely with 9♥5♣. Flash checked the flop, made a small turn probe (400) that I called, and now fires another small probe of 800 on the river into a 2,200 pot. Calling 800 to win the 3,000 pot after his bet requires ~26.7% equity (800 / (2200+800) ≈ 26.7%). Against a wide heads‑up opening range his river betting line is consistent with a mix of thin value (Ax, Jx, some pair combos) and bluffs/missed broadways — so there are enough bluffs that calling a small probe is reasonable. Raising as a bluff is unattractive (I have almost no fold equity and no showdown equity if called), and folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my large stack. I will call the 800 to see showdown, but I have no intention of raising and would fold to meaningful further aggression if it appeared.

Heads‑up on the button (small blind) I should be opening extremely wide — 3♠2♦ is weak but playable in position because I get post‑flop advantage and plenty of fold equity. Flash (BB) has shown frequent preflop activity but also folds to pressure, and I have a large stack edge. I’ll raise to seize the initiative and deny him cheap equity. Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB) is a standard, robust size: it reduces his cheap defenses and deters light 3‑bets while keeping post‑flop play manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet with this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet dry flops and check/control on coordinated boards, folding to significant aggression.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind with a suited hand like 9-5 diamonds against a standard 3x Button open is standard. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to win 400), and my hand has decent playability post-flop. I have a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, so I can afford to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay the opponent or hit a strong draw.

I am in the Big Blind and have called a button raise from Minni. The flop is 8d-Tc-Kc, and I hold 9d-5d. This board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as it contains several high cards (King, Ten). My hand currently has only 9-high with a backdoor flush draw and no direct straight draw. I will check to the aggressor to see how they play the board. If Minni bets, I will likely fold given the lack of immediate equity and the coordination of the board.

Heads‑up on the button I opened wide preflop and Flash checked the K‑T‑8 (two clubs) flop — a very standard spot to continuation‑bet. My 3♠2♦ has no pair, no club, and almost no showdown equity, so checking hands back surrenders initiative and lets him realize equity for free. A half‑pot c‑bet (~300 into 600) hits a wide portion of his calling range and preserves pot control if he calls. Plan: bet 300 to pick up the pot now or see a cheap turn; fold to a substantial check‑raise since I have essentially no fold equity or raw equity to continue, and if called I’ll continue only with improved equity or clear turn reads.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5d and the flop is 8d Tc Kc. This board is heavily coordinated and significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni), as it includes a King, a Ten, and an Eight. While I have a backdoor flush draw and a very weak backdoor straight possibility, I have no immediate pair or strong draw. Minni has bet half the pot (300 into 600), and calling here with 9-high on such a wet board is not profitable. I have no significant equity to continue, and a bluff would be ill-advised against an opponent with a large stack who likely has at least a piece of this board. Therefore, I will fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300, maintaining my standard opening size, to take control of the pot and capitalize on my card strength.

Facing a button open to 300, I’ll flat-call. The pot is 400 and I only need to commit 200 more (300 total), giving me ~25% equity needed — 3♦5♦ suited has decent heads-up equity vs a wide opening range and valuable flush/straight potential. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloating the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting; calling keeps pot control and preserves stack depth to realize implied odds when I hit. Plan: see the flop, continue on diamond or connected boards, and fold or proceed cautiously to heavy aggression on dry, high-card boards.

Check. I have middle pair (3♦ with 3♥ on board) plus backdoor diamond possibilities (Ad on board with my 3♦5♦) and a gutshot to the wheel (a 4). The flop contains an Ace which heavily intersects the button‑raiser’s continuing range, and I’m out of position, so I want pot control rather than barreling with a marginal hand. I have ~9 clean outs to improve to two‑pair/trips/straight (~35% to hit by river) and decent showdown equity vs missed broadway/Kx hands, so checking lets me call reasonable c‑bets to realize equity or take it down cheap if Flash checks back. Plan: check and evaluate — call small/medium bets to see a turn, fold only to large, polarized aggression unless I improve.

I have hit top pair on the flop with an Ace. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet on this board to extract value from weaker hands like smaller pairs or draws (straight or flush). A half-pot bet of 300 is standard for maintaining pressure and building the pot.

Flash opened to 300 and c‑bet the A♦ 2♥ 3♥ flop for 300 into a ~600 pot (now 900). I have middle pair (3♦) plus a backdoor diamond and a gutshot to the wheel (4) — roughly 9 clean outs to make two‑pair/trips/straight by the river (~35% to improve). Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives me ~25% pot odds, so a call is +EV. I’m out of position and prefer pot control with a single pair and runner‑runner possibilities; raising would bloat the pot and commit me with a marginal hand. My stack is deep enough to realize implied odds, so I’ll call the 300, see a turn, and fold to heavy, polarized aggression if I miss or become aggressive only when I improve.

The turn 4♦ gives me the wheel: A-2-3-4-5 (I hold 5♦ & 3♦). That’s a very strong, often-nut made hand heads‑up — and I also have two diamonds in hand with Ad/4♦ on board (I block some strong diamond combos). Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop, which shows weakness or a float; his continuing range will include Ax, medium pairs, some bluffs and some single-card draws. Checking lets him off the hook and surrenders value; betting extracts from Ax, pocket pairs and charging any hearts/diamond draws. I’ll make a value bet about half the pot (600 into 1200) — big enough to get called by worse hands and to charge draws, but small enough not to scare off thin callers. If he raises big, I’ll re‑evaluate for a possible chop or a better straight/flush but against his wide range I’m happy to continue; if he calls I’ll re‑assess river for a second value bet or to pot-control if a scary card arrives.

The turn card 4d is a significant card as it completes a straight for any 5 (A-2-3-4-5). Minni's lead (donk bet) of 600 into 1200 suggests she either has the straight, a set, two pair, or is testing my Ace. Since I have top pair with an 8 kicker, I cannot fold yet, but raising would be overplaying my hand against a range that now contains many straights. I will call to see the river and evaluate the strength of my hand then.

I hold 3♦5♦ and the final board is A♦ 2♥ 3♥ 4♦ 6♠. With my 5 and the board 2-3-4-6 I make a 2-6 straight (2-3-4-5-6) — essentially the nut on this river; the only way I don’t win is if Flash also holds a 5 (which only produces a chop). Flash’s line (button open, flop c-bet, check-call the turn raise) is consistent with Ax, medium pairs and floats — hands that will pay off a value bet. There’s virtually nothing that can beat me, so I should extract value rather than check. I’ll bet pot to maximize calls from Ax, pocket pairs and missed draws (and to deny correct odds to any bluff-equities). If he raises very large I’ll call (or re-evaluate) because he can’t have a better made hand aside from a 5 which would split.

On the river, the board is [Ad 2h 3h 4d 6s]. Minni has led with a pot-sized bet of 2400 after donk-betting the turn. This line (Call Pre-flop, Call Flop, Lead Turn, Pot-bet River) is extremely strong and typically represents a made hand, likely a straight (any 5) or two-pair (A2, A3, A4, A6). While I have top pair with my Ace, the 4 and 6 on the turn and river have significantly coordinated the board, making my hand little more than a bluff-catcher. Given the tournament context and the fact that Minni is the chip leader, calling a pot-sized bet with a marginal one-pair hand is too risky. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦6♥ — this is well inside a standard opening range. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I prefer to take the initiative: raising keeps my range wide, gains fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position. Flash (BB) has a decent stack (9,950) and will defend a lot, but a min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-variance way to apply pressure while preserving postflop playability. Plan: if called, c-bet favorable textures and pot-control on bad boards; if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold Q6o to heavy 3-bets).

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni, who has been active and has a significant chip lead, has min-raised to 200 from the button. This is a standard spot to defend. A3o is well ahead of a button opening range. While I could 3-bet here, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity given the excellent price (100 to call into a 300 pot).

On this monotone flop of 4h-Th-2h, I hold the 3 of hearts and an overcard Ace. While the 3 of hearts provides a low flush draw and I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), I am out of position against the chip leader. Checking is the most prudent play to see how Minni reacts to this board texture before committing more chips.

I raised preflop and hold Qd6h on Th 4h 2h. I have an overcard (Q) plus a four‑card heart draw (9 outs, ~35% to hit by river), so this is a good semi‑bluff. Heads‑up the BB defends a very wide range, but a medium c‑bet can fold many unpaired hands while building the pot when I hit. I’ll continue the initiative with a roughly half‑pot c‑bet (200) — enough fold equity without bloating the pot. Plan: if Flash folds I scoop; if called I can press on a heart or if I pair my Q/6, and pot‑control on blanks. I’ll re‑evaluate (and likely fold) to a strong check‑raise or large flop raise since my showdown value is limited.

I hold an overcard (Ace), a gutshot straight draw, and a low flush draw (3 of hearts). On a monotone flop, Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet. I have significant equity with multiple ways to improve, and the pot odds (3:1) are favorable for a call. Raising is risky against the chip leader on this specific board texture, as it could lead to being forced out of my equity by a shove. Calling allows me to see the turn and re-evaluate my hand strength as the board develops.

On the turn, the Ace of Hearts completes a four-flush board. While I have made a flush with my Trey of Hearts, it is the lowest possible flush (the 3-high flush) since the 4, 10, 2, and Ace of Hearts are on the board. I also have top pair with my Ace of Clubs, but the flush texture dominates this board. Against a chip leader like Minni, who could easily be holding any heart higher than a 3, leading out is risky. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see if Minni attempts to represent a higher flush or checks behind. I am out of position and my hand is essentially a marginal bluff-catcher or a very thin value hand at this point.

Turn analysis: the A♥ completes a heart flush for me (6♥) — I now have a made flush (A,10,6,4,2 hearts) but it is not the nut because any heart higher than 6 in Flash’s hand (K♥/Q♥/J♥/9♥/8♥/7♥) beats me. Flash defended very wide preflop and called my half‑pot c‑bet on the flop, so his calling range contains hearts, T‑x, and pairs. I want to extract value from worse hearts, tens and pocket pairs while denying cheap river cards and not giving free cards to weaker holdings. A value/protection bet of roughly 3/4 pot is appropriate here. I will bet 600 into the 800 pot to charge worse hands and fold out some non‑heart overcards. If Flash check‑raises strongly or jams, that will usually indicate a higher heart and I will fold; if he simply calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

On a board with four hearts, I have the lowest possible flush (3-high) because the Ace, Ten, Four, and Deuce of hearts are on the board. Minni's bet of 600 into an 800-chip pot (75% pot) is a strong lead on a very coordinated board texture. Since any heart in her hand higher than a Trey (such as the 5h, 6h, up to the Kh) would result in a superior flush, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher. While Minni is aggressive and could be attempting to push me off a weak Ace, calling here out of position against the chip leader is high-risk. If I call and she continues her aggression on the river, I will be forced into a difficult decision with almost zero chance of having the best hand if she is value betting. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 4-6 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Given my significant stack disadvantage relative to Minni and the fact that I am playing against a very active and aggressive opponent, there is little value in entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button with 6s7s (a suited connector) I want initiative and a wide opening range. Limping is too passive — raising isolates the BB, builds the pot when I have position, and gives me many flops to C-bet or realize equity. Opponent has ~9.5k effective stack so a standard min-raise keeps the pot manageable while applying pressure. I’ll raise to the min (200) to open the pot and play post-flop in position; I’ll reevaluate if I face a sizeable 3-bet.

I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) to defend my Big Blind against a 2x open from the button. While 86o is a weak hand to play out of position, the price is too good to fold. I will call and see if I can connect with the flop.

I defended my big blind with 8h6c against a small open. The flop is K-T-2 rainbow, which misses my hand completely. Minni has checked back or I am first to act (the log says Minni raised pre-flop, I called, and now the flop is out).
