Since the board is quite dry but favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Kx and Tx hands), and I have no pair and no real draw, I will check. If Minni bets, I will likely fold unless the bet is extremely small, but for now, checking is the standard play.

Flop Kc Tc 2c. I hold 6s7s — no pair, only backdoor spade and very remote straight equity. As the preflop raiser in heads‑up position I should continuation-bet a large portion of my range to fold equityless hands and extract value from worse holdings that called preflop. Flash (BB) has shown a tendency to call wide but also to be passive; a half-pot c-bet (200 into the 400 pot) balances fold equity with pot control. Plan: bet 200 to take down many pots immediately; if he check-raises big I will fold (very little equity), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel selectively on favorable cards.

I defended the big blind with 8h6c and the flop is Kc-Ts-2c. I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, and Minni bet 200 into a 400 pot. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kings, Tens, and Broadway draws. I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity. Continuing even for a small bet would be unprofitable in the long run given the lack of potential for my hand to improve. I will fold.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with 5d 3d. This is a suited one-gapper, which is a reasonable hand to open with in position. My goal is to apply some pressure and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop in position. I'll make a standard raise to 300.

I’m in the big blind with AdTd facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). A‑T suited is strong heads‑up and I don’t want to play a marginal Ace out of position postflop. The button’s 3x open looks wide from history, so I want to take the initiative and either isolate him or win the pot preflop. I’ll 3‑bet for value and fold equity — raise to 900 total (about 3x his open). That sizing pressures his wide opens, keeps worse hands and many pairs in, and still leaves room to fold to a large 4‑bet if necessary.

I am in position with 5d 3d, a suited one-gapper. While this hand has some speculative value, Minni has shown a high level of aggression and a willingness to play large pots, often with unexpected hands. Minni's 3-bet to 900 puts me in a difficult spot. I am significantly out-chipped, and calling 600 more with a hand as weak as 5-high, even suited, is generally a losing play against a range that likely dominates me or will force me off the hand post-flop. Since I need to preserve my stack for better opportunities and avoid high-variance spots against the chip leader, folding is the most disciplined move here.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should open-raise wide to take the initiative and use position postflop. 3♠4♣ is marginal but entirely playable in position, so I’ll make the minimum standard open to 2xBB (raise to 200 total). This is a small, low-risk investment to steal the blind or play the flop last; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this weak offsuit, if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously with draws/weak pairs. With deep stacks and many hands left, this balanced, aggressive line maximizes fold equity while keeping pots controllable.

83o is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While the pot odds are attractive to call a min-raise (100 chips to win a total pot of 400), this hand has very poor playability post-flop, especially when out of position. Minni holds a significant chip lead and has been active, so there is no reason to volunteer chips with a bottom-tier hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot to defend or take the initiative.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens, the third-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. In a heads-up situation, this hand is extremely strong. My goal is to build a significant pot early while I have the advantage. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) is a solid opening move from the button to extract value and narrow the field, although here it is just one opponent. Given Minni's stack size and previous aggression, I want to play this hand strongly.

Call to 300. Facing a 3x open from the button/SB in heads-up, Flash's opening range is wide and 4♠5♣ has decent postflop playability (straight and two‑pair potential). Calling costs me 200 more into a ~400 pot (making it ~600), keeps the pot manageable, and lets me realize equity with deep stacks — 3-betting this weak hand would be too thin and folding is overly nitty heads-up. Postflop I'll proceed cautiously: check/call on dry high-card boards, and continue or turn aggression on connected/drawy flops or when I pair up.

Check. On Ad-2c-9s my 4♠5♣ has no pair and only a one-card straight draw (any 3 completes A-2-3-4-5) — 4 outs (~8.5% to hit the turn, ~16.5% by the river). This is a dry Ace-high board where my hand has little showdown value, so I prefer pot control heads‑up. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and allows me to call small-to-medium continuation bets to see the turn while folding to large overbets/raises. If Flash checks back I get a free card; if he fires a small c-bet I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn.

I started with Pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. The flop comes with an Ace, which is a dangerous card since it's well within the Big Blind's calling range. However, Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of 300 chips (half the pot) to maintain the initiative, test the strength of her hand, and potentially get value from weaker hands like a 9 or smaller pocket pairs. If she calls or raises, I'll need to proceed with caution on the later streets.

Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop and now fires a small c-bet (300 into this pot). My 4♠5♣ has no pair and only a one-card straight draw (any 3 completes A‑2‑3‑4‑5 — 4 outs). Raising here as a semi-bluff is too thin (limited fold equity and I’ll often be called by better hands); folding is too nitpicky heads‑up. The bet is small enough that calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity against a wide range, and preserves my large stack versus Flash’s 8,400. I’ll call 300 and reevaluate on the turn — continue only if I improve or get good odds/reads, otherwise fold to large turn aggression.

Check. I hold 4♠5♣ with no pair and only a one-card wheel draw (four 3s = ~8.7% to hit the river). The turn pairing an Ace (Ad->As) significantly improves Flash’s preflop-raiser range (many Ax combos and hands that now beat me), so bluffing or raising here is thin — I have very limited fold equity and would often be called or crushed if raised. Betting to bloat the pot with ~8.7% equity is poor value; checking controls the pot and lets me realize my draw for free or see a cheap river. Practical plan: check now, call small turn/river bets only (rough break-even call on the river is about a ~100 chip bet given the 1,200 pot), and fold to large bets. If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and consider a small bluff only if he shows clear weakness or I hit.

With the second Ace appearing on the turn, the board is now Ad-2c-9s-As. While this reduces the likelihood of Minni holding an Ace, her call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a Nine, or a mid-range pocket pair. By checking back, I am exercising pot control. My Queens are strong, but I don't want to over-commit if she is trapping with trips or a full house. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially reach showdown or evaluate her action on the final street without facing a check-raise now.

I check. I hold 4♠5♣ and missed my one-card wheel draw — essentially no showdown value. The river pairing the board into Ad-2c-9s-As-Ah heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Ax combos and some turned/rivered value hands). Flash bet the flop but then checked the turn, a line that can be either medium strength hands or slow-played Aces; regardless, on this runout he’s unlikely to fold many Ax or even strong pairs to a river bluff. Betting here has very little fold equity and would be spewing chips into a 1,200 pot with almost no chance of improvement. Better to control the pot size: check and concede unless he gives a clear fold signal by checking back; fold to any meaningful river raise.

The board has provided three Aces (Ad, As, Ah), giving me a very strong hand: Aces full of Queens (Ah As Ad Qh Qs). My opponent called a flop bet on an Ace-high board, which typically suggests an Ace, a 9, or a pocket pair. Since the turn and river brought two more Aces, if Minni has an Ace, she now has Quads. However, she has checked both the turn and the river. If she has a 9 or a pocket pair like Tens or Jacks, she has a full house but a weaker one than mine. I am only losing to the case Ace (Ac) for Quads or pocket Kings (KK) for a higher full house. Given the action, it is highly likely I have the best hand, and I should bet for value to extract chips from a 9 or a mid-pair that might feel compelled to call a half-pot bet in a heads-up situation.

Fold. The river (Ad 2c 9s As Ah) leaves my 4♠5♣ with zero showdown value — my best five-card hand is just the board (AAA92). Flash opened pre, c-bet the flop, checked the turn and now fires a small river bet. That line is exactly what a lot of value hands (A‑x, pocket pairs -> full houses/quads) will do and will beat me; the only hands I beat are pure missed hands that choose to bluff this river. Calling 600 into the final pot (~3,000 if called) requires ~20% equity, but the frequency Flash is bluffing here is well below that. Raising as a bluff is too thin (little fold equity vs. an opener’s range and would risk a big portion of my stack). Negative EV to call or raise — fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with 4♦5♣ — a marginal but perfectly playable hand in position. Versus a single big blind I want initiative and to leverage position post‑flop, so I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size rather than just completing. Raising to 300 is a table‑standard sizing for me: it gives fold equity, isolates the BB, and keeps postflop decisions flexible. Plan: if called I’ll c‑bet favorable low/connected boards and check/control on high-card/heavy boards; fold to a large, valuey 3‑bet (or re‑evaluate if the 3‑bet is small and I have pot odds). With my much larger stack this raise is +EV heads‑up and maximizes opportunities to win the pot outright or play postflop in position.

I am in the big blind with 72 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised from the small blind/button to 3x the big blind. Calling here would put me out of position with a hand that has very little post-flop potential or equity. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack for better opportunities, there is no reason to defend this specific hand.

In heads-up play, Queen-Seven offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button. My opponent, Minni, has a significant stack advantage, but I need to maintain pressure and play a balanced range. A 3x raise is a standard opening size to attempt to take down the blinds or define the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up, I'm in the big blind with 3♠6♥. Flash (button/SB) opened to 300; pot is 400 and I would need to call 200 (break-even equity ≈ 200/(400+200) = 33%). 3-6 offsuit is a very weak, often-dominated hand and I'm out of position postflop against an opener who raises frequently from the button. A light 3-bet to 500 would be a poor bluff with little fold equity and creates a difficult spot if called. Calling for position-disadvantaged postflop play with <33% equity is suboptimal, so I preserve my deep stack and fold, conceding the BB.

Heads-up on the button with 8♦ T♣ — a playable, reasonably connected hand. I want to take the initiative, use my positional advantage, and apply fold equity rather than letting the BB see a cheap flop for free. A standard open to ~3x the BB is textbook here: it builds the pot when I have decent equity, narrows the BB’s calling range, and keeps future decisions manageable. If Flash flats, I’ll play postflop in position; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (most 3-bets will have me folding or proceeding cautiously). Raise to 300 (total) to open the pot and seize initiative.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand out of position against a large stack, even in a heads-up situation. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 2h 6s. This is a very weak hand. The pot is 150 and it is 50 more to call. Given that my opponent, Minni, has a significantly larger stack (30,400 to my 9,450) and has been active, I see no reason to play this hand out of position or invest further chips into a pot with such a low equity hand. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) I can open-raise extremely wide — 2♦3♥ is weak in a full-ring spot but perfectly playable heads-up in position. Calling 50 to complete to 100 is cheap, but raising takes the initiative, denies correct odds to the BB, and folds out a lot of marginal hands. I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size to apply pressure and keep the lead; if Flash 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate given their sizing, and if he calls I’ll have position to c-bet many flops. Total commit = 300 chips.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been quite active from the button, and a 3-bet here is the standard play to extract value and gain the initiative. I will raise to 900.

I opened very wide HU with 2♦3♥ and Flash just 3-bet to 900. With position and deep effective stacks I don’t want to 4-bet a clear bluff with 2‑3 off, and folding after already investing 300 is too nitty given the cheap price to see a flop. Calling 600 more (total commitment 900) keeps the pot controllable, lets me use positional advantage to realize equity, and preserves flexibility — I’ll fold to heavy turn/river aggression when missed, and press when I pair or pick up a draw. I won’t 4‑bet this holding; the correct line is to call and play the flop in position.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Queens) on a very coordinated Broadway board. While I am ahead of many Ax, Kx, and Qx hands, I must be cautious of JT (straight), KK (set), and AK (better two pair). Betting here is the best way to extract value from hands like AJ, AT, or KJ while also gaining information. If I check, I risk giving a free card to a straight draw or allowing a check-back from a hand I dominate. I will lead for half the pot to build the pot against her range.

Preflop I opened wide and already invested 900 total. The flop K♦ A♣ Q♠ absolutely misses my 2♦3♥ — no pair, no real draw. That said the additional call is cheap relative to my stack (900 vs ~29.6k) and the pot odds are ~25% (900 to win 3600). Flash 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands that connect with this board, but he will also c‑bet some non‑made hands heads‑up. With position I can float and try to take the pot away on a favourable turn or fold to clear strength, so I’ll call one more street and keep the pot controlled. Plan: call now, evaluate turn — fold to heavy continued aggression unless I pair or pick up a legitimate draw; if he checks turn I can apply pressure and try to exploit his capped barrels.

I have a very strong hand with top and bottom two pair on a highly coordinated board. The 8c is a relatively blank card, though it does open some backdoor flush possibilities. However, the main concern is the straight (J-T), which is well within Minni's calling range. By checking, I am controlling the size of the pot and providing Minni an opportunity to bluff with weaker hands or missed draws. If she bets, I have a comfortable call given the strength of my hand, but I want to avoid bloating the pot further in case she has already completed a straight or has a superior two pair like A-K or a set like K-K.

I opened very wide preflop and called Flash’s 3‑bet; the flop K♦ A♣ Q♠ completely misses my 2♦3♥ but Flash’s line (c‑bet the flop, then check the turn) looks like pot‑control or weakness. I have position and a very wide range here, so a credible turn barrel can represent an Ace/King/Queen or a slowplayed strong hand and will fold out a lot of his medium/weak holdings (floats, single‑paired K/Q hands, some Ax that are pot‑controlling). Pot is ~3600 and Flash has ~7650 behind — I want enough size to generate real fold equity but not overcommit. I’ll apply pressure with a half‑to‑two‑thirds pot sized bluff: bet 2000. If he raises/jams I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (look to bluff again on a safe card or shut down if he shows strength).

I have top and bottom two pair (Aces and Queens) on a highly coordinated Broadway board. While my hand is very strong, the board is dangerous as any Jack-Ten makes a straight, and I am also losing to Ace-King and pocket Kings. By checking the turn, I intended to control the pot size and induce action from weaker hands or draws. Minni's bet of 2,000 into a 3,600 pot is a substantial lead, but folding two pair here would be too exploitable and tight. However, raising would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten (like a straight or a set). Therefore, the most prudent course of action is to call, keeping her range wide and allowing me to evaluate the river. I have 7,650 chips remaining, so calling 2,000 still leaves me with a manageable stack for the final street.

On the river, the board has become increasingly dangerous. The five of clubs completes a backdoor flush, and the straight (Jack-Ten) has been possible since the flop. I have top and bottom two pair (Aces and Queens), which is a very strong hand, but it is effectively a 'bluff catcher' now against this specific board texture. I checked the turn to control the pot size and Minni bet 2,000, which I called. Now on the river, I will check again to see Minni's reaction. If she checks back, I expect to win a significant portion of the time against hands like A-J, A-T, or K-J. If she bets, I will have to decide if she is representing the straight or flush, or if she is turning a hand with showdown value into a bluff.

I raised wide preflop and c‑bet called on the flop, then barreled the turn and Flash called — his line is weighted to real made hands (Ax, Kx, Qx, AK/AQ/KQ, sets) as well as a few floats. The river 5♣ is a blank for my 2♦3♥ (no pair, no club), so I have essentially zero showdown equity and I’m purely a bluff. To make a river bluff +EV I need a high fold frequency: if I bet B into the P=7600 pot I need folds > B/(P+B). Example: B=3000 needs >28% folds; an all‑in of 5650 needs >42.6%. Given he cold‑3‑bet pre and called a sizable turn raise, his call range on the river will include many one‑pair/two‑pair hands that will call a reasonable bluff, so I don’t expect enough fold equity. Betting here risks a large chunk of my stack with very little chance of success. I’ll check and take the safer line — conceding this pot preserves stack to pick better spots later.
I have been dealt pocket sixes on the button. This is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. I will make a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and define my opponent's range.

Heads-up the button (Flash) opened to 300 — a standard 3bb open. I’m in the big blind with K♠5♣: a marginal but defensible hand against a wide button opening range. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (making the total I’ll have committed 300), so I’m getting 200-to-win-600 pot odds (~33%). Calling keeps the pot controlled out of position, preserves my deep stack, and lets me realize equity and play the flop (I’ll proceed aggressively if I hit a king or good draws, fold to heavy continued aggression when completely missed). 3-betting with K5o here is too thin OOP — I don’t want to inflate the pot or face a 4-bet with a weak holding. Therefore I will call the 200 and see a flop.

I flat-called the button’s 3bb open preflop to keep the pot controlled OOP. Flop Js 8h 2s leaves me with K♠5♣ — only K‑high and a backdoor (runner‑runner) spade draw. I have very little showdown value and minimal fold equity out of position, so bluffing here is unattractive. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack. Plan: if the button checks I’ll either take a free turn or look for a favorable card (K or spade) to apply pressure; if he bets a small c‑bet (≈≤200, ~1/3 pot) I can call and float, but I will fold to medium/large bets (≈>200–300). Checking keeps options open and avoids getting committed with a weak hand OOP.

I have pocket sixes on a board with two overcards (8 and Jack). This is a marginal made hand. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to take the initiative and see where I stand. This bet can fold out many of Minni's air hands and potentially get value from draws or smaller pairs.

I checked the flop OOP with K♠5♣ and now face a 300 bet into a 900 pot (300 to call). Calling 300 to win 1200 requires ≈25% equity. My hand has very little showdown value: realistic chance to pair a K or 5 by river is ≈24% (6 outs by river) and the runner‑runner spade flush is ≈4%, but a large portion of those hit scenarios will still be behind the opponent’s likely c‑bet range (Jx, pairs, many Ax/Kx, and strong broadways). I have almost no fold equity OOP, raising is thin and unnecessarily bloats the pot with a weak holding, and calling here is roughly break‑even or -EV versus a competent c‑betting range. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button with 5♣8♣ — a playable, suited connector that fares well postflop and benefits from initiative. I want to open-raise rather than just complete to gain fold equity and build a pot when I hit. With deep effective stacks (I'm ~264 BBs, he ~134 BBs) a modest raise is fine: raise to 200 (2x BB) keeps the pot controllable while applying pressure. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (hand is marginal), call smaller aggression and play postflop aggressively with club/connected boards and pot-control on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4c, a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While the price to call is small (only 100 more to see a flop in a 300 pot), 64o is extremely poor and I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve chips against an aggressive opponent, I will choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.
King-six suited is a strong hand in a heads-up or small blind vs big blind situation. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the big blind.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button, 8♠5♥ is a weak, unsuited hand but heads-up I need to defend a relatively wide range. Calling the extra 200 (total 300 committed) gives roughly 3:1 pot odds (~25% equity required) so a call is reasonable vs a likely wide opening range. I'm OOP post-flop, so plan is to proceed cautiously: fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss, continue with any pair or decent draw, and avoid 3-betting with this junk hand. A 3-bet shove or large 3-bet is too loose given the hand and deep stacks, so I will call and see the flop.

I have 8♠5♥ on a Q♦ T♥ T♦ flop — I completely missed and have no real draw (only a backdoor heart). Villain opened wide preflop and will c-bet a high frequency, but I’m out of position and should control the pot with this junk. Checking preserves chips, lets me fold to heavy aggression, and allows calling a small probe bet to realize equity against bluffs or Q‑high. Plan: check now; call small C‑bets (≈≤1/3 pot) but fold to larger pressure unless I pick up a pair or a real draw.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with King-high and a backdoor flush draw. The flop is paired (Qd-Th-Td). Minni has checked to me. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a good play here to represent a strong hand like a Queen or a Ten and to take down the pot immediately if Minni has missed this board.

I call 300. I’m OOP with 8♠5♥ and completely missed the Q♦ T♥ T♦ flop (only a backdoor heart). Flash opened wide preflop and has c-bet a small 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot), which is a very common size for a wide continuation range. Calling is cheap: the pot after the bet is 1,200 so calling 300 gives ~4:1 (≈20%) pot odds. Although my raw equity is low, folding here would be too tight and raising is unjustified with no pair or real draw. I’ll call to see the turn, then fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve to a pair or a real draw; if the turn checks I can re-evaluate for a delayed bluff or a free card.

I have 5♥8♠ on Q♦ T♥ T♦ 3♥. I completely missed — no pair and no realistic draw (even with the hearts on board a 5-card heart flush is impossible from here). Flash opened wide pre and c-bet a small flop size, then checked the turn: that check shows either weakness or pot-control with a made hand. I’m out of position and deep-stacked, so turning this into a bluff is thin (villain will call lots of hands and can easily raise). Best play is pot control and to see a river for free: check and evaluate the river. Plan: fold to any sizable river bet, call only very small bets if I think he’s bluffing, and if I pair or pick up a meaningful equity on the river I’ll switch to value/bluff accordingly.

On this turn, the 3 of hearts provides me with a flush draw to go along with my King-high. Minni has checked for the second time. While a semi-bluff could be effective here to push her off marginal hands like pocket pairs or weak Jacks, checking back is a safer way to realize my equity. It allows me to see the river for free and potentially hit my flush or a King, while also keeping the pot manageable against a chip leader who has shown a tendency to be sticky.

River: Q♦ T♥ T♦ 3♥ 3♦. I hold 8♠5♥ — no pair, no real draw. Pot is 1,200 and Flash opened wide preflop, c‑bet a small flop and then checked the turn. That line can represent a wide range but critically it also contains lots of Ts and 3s that now make trips/full houses or hands that will call a river bluff. With essentially zero showdown equity, deep stacks, and the board pairing on the river (which increases Villain’s calling frequency), a bluff here is very thin and likely unprofitable. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid throwing good chips away; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet and only consider calling a very small probe (roughly ≤1/4 pot ≈300) if I have a specific read that Flash bluffs that size extremely often. Otherwise check and fold to pressure.

The board has double-paired (Tens and Threes) with a Queen. My King-high has marginal showdown value in this heads-up pot. Minni called pre-flop and check-called the flop, then checked the turn and river. This passive line often indicates a hand with some value that is afraid of the board (like a small pocket pair or Ace-high) or a completely missed draw. Since any pocket pair (44-99) would have me beat by forming a better two-pair (10s and their pair vs 10s and 3s), and Ace-high also beats me, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and potentially win against her missed draws (like 4-5, 6-7, 8-9) that don't contain an Ace.

Heads-up on the button with K♥3♥ — suited king has decent playability and good postflop potential in position. With deep stacks and the initiative, I want to open-raise rather than limp/call to deny the BB free equity and to build pots when I have the positional advantage. I’ll use a standard sizing (3x BB to 300 total) — large enough to get folds and extract value from weaker hands, but small enough to keep pots manageable vs aggressive 3-bets. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold K3s; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position.

In this head-up match against Minni, I have Q2 suited in the big blind. Minni has opened to 3x (300). Q2s is a hand with some playability and flush potential, but it's relatively weak against a standard 3x opening range from a chip leader. However, folding would give up my blind too easily. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay Minni or hit a strong draw/pair. Given the current stack sizes (I'm at ~140bb and Minni is at ~250bb), I can afford to see a flop for 200 more chips in a pot that will be 600.

I have missed this flop completely with my Qc 2c. The board is 6s-9s-4h, and I have no pair, no draw, and my overcards are relatively weak. Being out of position against the chip leader, my standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I will likely fold given the lack of equity in this hand.

I opened preflop and took the initiative; Flash just checked the dry 9♠-6♠-4♥ flop. I hold K♥3♥ — no pair but a useful K blocker and backdoor heart equity. In heads-up this board misses a large portion of the BB calling range (broadways, many weak pairs and floats), so a standard continuation bet should pick up a lot of pots and deny free cards. I’ll bet ~50% pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with a weak hand. If he raises big I’ll fold (no pair, limited equity); if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and continue only when I pick up real equity (K or heart) or pot-control on blanks.

I have completely missed the flop with Qc 2c on a 6s 9s 4h board. I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor potential. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a continuation bet of 300 into the 600 pot. Given I am out of position and have no equity in this hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

In heads-up play, Queen-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I have consistently been raising to 300 in this position to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early. Given the current stack sizes and the hand strength relative to a 2-player range, a raise is the standard and most effective play.

Facing a button raise to 300, I'm in the big blind with 2♦6♠ — a very weak, unconnected offsuit hand that's often dominated. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires roughly 33% equity to break even, which this hand won't have versus a typical button opening range, and I'd be out of position postflop facing frequent continuation bets. With a healthy stack, it's better to conserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with A♠ T♥ (Ace‑Ten offsuit). This is a strong opening hand in heads‑up play and I have post‑flop position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want to seize the initiative, build a pot with a hand that plays well in position, and fold out some of BB’s weaker holdings. Flash (BB) has defended widely in prior hands, so a standard 3x BB open should get value and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 total — keeps stacks deep enough to play postflop, consistent with table tendencies, and gives me control of the hand. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops (A/T/low‑rainbow) for value and protection; slow down or check/call on very coordinated boards; re‑evaluate if I face a large 3‑bet (call small 3‑bets, fold to huge 4‑bets/shoves unless ranges justify calling).

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of sixes (6h6d). Minni, the only other player remaining in the tournament, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. In a heads-up context, pocket sixes are a strong hand. Looking at our history, I have successfully 3-bet Minni in Hand #39 with pocket eights and Hand #53 with Ace-Queen. 3-betting here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a hand that can flop a set or remain an overpair on low boards. I will raise to 900, which is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizing.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♥T♠ and Flash (BB) 3‑bet to 900. In heads‑up this is a very playable hand and I have post‑flop position. Flash has shown a wide, aggressive defending range and the 3‑bet sizing is a standard, not huge, one. Rather than 4‑bet bluffing with a marginal hand, I prefer to call and realize equity in position, keep his bluffs in the range, and play the flop. Calling 600 more (900 total committed) gives ≈2:1 immediate pot odds and plenty of implied odds with our deeper stacks. Plan: use position to c‑bet favorable A/T/low‑rainbow flops for value/protection, check/call or pot control on coordinated boards, and fold to large, polarized 4‑bets/overbets unless the sizing and reads justify continuing.

I 3-bet pre-flop with pocket sixes to take the initiative. The flop comes Ace-high with a Ten and an Eight, which is quite unfavorable for my specific hand as it contains two overcards to my pair and hits much of a typical 3-bet calling range. While the Ace is good for my perceived range as the 3-bettor, I have very little equity if called. I will check here to see how Minni responds; if she checks back, I can see a free turn. If she bets, I will likely have to fold given the board texture and the strength she showed by calling the 3-bet pre-flop.

I have A♥T♠ on A♦‑8♣‑T♥ — top two pair. This flop is exactly the type I planned to c‑bet: it's a low‑coordinated, rainbow A/T board that hits my perceived value range (Ax, Tx, broadways) and there are very few dangerous draws. Flash has shown wide 3‑betting, and his check on the flop is ambiguous (weakness or a trap), so I want to extract value and deny free cards. I’ll bet ~50% pot to charge one‑pair A/T hands, worse tens, and speculative hands while protecting against the small number of sets (AA/TT/88) that beat me. If he check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (call reasonable-sized raises, but fold to an extreme polarized shove if the math/reads don’t justify it).

On this Ace-high board (8c Ad Th), my pocket sixes have become a weak underpair. I 3-bet pre-flop to take control, but after I checked the flop, Minni's half-pot bet of 900 into 1800 indicates either a made hand (like an Ace or a Ten) or a standard probe to take down the pot. Given that the board hits much of her 3-bet calling range and I have very little equity to improve (only two outs for a set), calling or raising here is too risky against the current chip leader. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable board texture.

Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in heads-up play, particularly from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain aggression, as I have position for the rest of the hand.

Opponent opened to 300 from the button (SB) heads-up. I’m in the big blind with T♠7♥ — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. With deep stacks and a large chip advantage I prefer to defend rather than 3‑bet out of position with an offsuit ten‑seven. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity (we can make top pair with tens or sevens and have backdoor possibilities), and lets me gather info on his post‑flop tendencies. A min 3‑bet to 500 is too small to be particularly effective and a larger 3‑bet would be committing with a marginal holding. I’ll call to see the flop and play position/stack leverage on later streets.

I have top pair (T♠) with a weak kicker (7♥) against a wide button open. The flop J♥-2♥-T♦ is fairly dry besides the two hearts — I beat many of his unpaired broadways and weaker tens, and I deny equity to heart draws and random overcards by betting. Out of position I want value and to define his range without bloating the pot with a mediocre kicker, so I’ll make a standard half‑pot c‑bet (~300 into 600). If he raises big I’ll give up or proceed cautiously depending on sizing; if he calls I can continue extracting thin value on later streets.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a board of Jack-Deuce-Ten. Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot (a donk bet). Folding here would be too tight given I have a pair and position. Raising is an option to test her range, but it might isolate me against better hands like a Jack or a stronger Ten. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, utilize my position on future streets, and see how the turn develops. I have enough showdown value to continue.

Check. The 6♥ on the turn completes a three‑heart board (J♥‑2♥‑T♦‑6♥) and turns many of Flash’s flop calls into either made flushes (if he started with two hearts) or strong draws/paired hands. I only have top pair (T♠7♥) with a weak kicker and a single heart — a marginal hand to build a bigger pot with out of position. Betting here invites a check‑raise or a large value bet from made hearts/Jx combos and unnecessarily bloats the pot when I’m likely behind against those hands. Checking keeps the pot under control, preserves fold equity on the river, and allows me to call small turn bets (to keep bluffs and single‑heart draws in) while folding to large aggression. If Flash checks back I see a cheap river; if he bets small–medium I’ll call and reevaluate on the river; if he raises or jams I’ll fold to avoid committing with a vulnerable top pair.

On the turn, the 6 of hearts completes a potential flush. Minni led the flop but has now checked. I have middle pair (Tens) and the Ten of hearts, giving me a flush draw and some showdown value. Checking back is the most prudent play here; it allows me to realize my equity and avoid a check-raise on a dangerous board. If a heart or a Queen/Ten comes on the river, I improve significantly, and if not, I still have a pair that might win at showdown if the river goes check-check.

I hold T♠7♥ — top pair with a weak kicker on J♥-2♥-T♦-6♥-K♦. I bet the flop for thin value and was called, then checked the turn to control the pot when a third heart appeared; Flash checked back the turn, which lowers the probability he has a turned flush or a confident K/J that would polarize into a river bet. Flash’s flop call and passive turn check are consistent with a wide, medium-strength calling range (Qx, 9x, underpairs, some Tx/Jx, single-heart backdoors), many of which will call a small-to-medium river value bet. A half‑pot value bet (~600 into 1200) targets those worse hands and denies a free showdown while keeping the pot manageable against stronger hands; if he raises I’ll give up since my kicker is weak and I’m often behind. Bet half pot to extract thin value and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On the river, the King of diamonds is a significant scare card. My pair of Tens, which was a marginal holding on the flop, has further depreciated in value. Minni's line - donking the flop, checking the turn, and betting half-pot on the river - often indicates a hand that was strong enough to lead early but cautious on the turn, or a hand that improved on the river (like a King-high hand). Given that the board now contains a Jack, a King, and three hearts, many hands in Minni's range now beat my middle pair. While the pot odds are 3:1, my pair of Tens is effectively a bluff-catcher on this texture, and I have seen Minni show strength in previous hands when betting. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦3♣: it's a weak offsuit hand but perfectly playable in position. I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure to the big blind's wide defending range, and keep the pot in position where I can control it postflop. I choose a standard 3x BB open (300) which is consistent with my image at the table and gives good fold equity; if Flash 3-bets big I'll fold Q3o, if he flats I'll play the flop aggressively from position and look to realize my equity or take the pot down with continuation bets. Total committed to the pot after this action will be 300 chips.

I am holding 9c 3h in the big blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. 93o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a stack leader who has been playing aggressively. Calling 200 more to see a flop with such a low-equity hand is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I'm holding a small pocket pair (3d 3s) on the button in a heads-up situation. While 33 is vulnerable to overcards, it is often ahead of a random hand in the big blind. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a post-flop play if called. I will raise to 300, which is my standard opening size.

Heads-up in the big blind with 6h10h facing a standard SB open to 300. This is a playable, suited hand heads-up with decent equity vs a wide button opening range (backdoor flush/straight potential and some overcard showdown value). Calling costs me 200 more (300 total including my 100 posted) into a 400 pot — roughly 2:1 immediate pot odds — and with deep stacks the implied odds are attractive. A min‑raise to 500 would bloat the pot and put me OOP on later streets with a marginal hand, so I prefer to see the flop and play based on texture (aggressively when I improve, or pot‑control/fold to heavy action when I don’t). Call 300 total.

I called preflop to see a flop and planned to pot‑control or fold if I didn’t improve. The Q♣ K♠ A♦ flop completely misses 10♥6♥ — no pair and only an extremely unlikely runner‑runner heart flush. This texture heavily favors the button’s opening range (many A/K/Q and broadways), and I’m out of position. Leading or bluff‑raising OOP with no equity is a losing line long‑term, so I’ll check and concede the initiative. If Flash bets I will fold to meaningful pressure (re‑evaluate only on a turn that gives real equity such as an A/K/10/heart).

I am holding a small pocket pair (3d 3s) on a very coordinated Broadway flop of Qc-Ks-Ad. This board is extremely likely to have hit Minni's calling range, and my 33 has very little equity to improve. While I represented strength pre-flop, betting here against a check is risky because Minni is unlikely to fold any pair or even a straight draw (like Jx or Tx). Checking back allows me to control the pot and see a turn card for free without inflating the pot with a hand that is currently very weak.

I have 10♥6♥ on Q♣ K♠ A♦ 7♣. I completely missed — there are no hearts on board so a flush is impossible and I only have six outs (three 10s + three 6s) to make a single pair on the river (~13% equity). I'm out of position against a button who opened preflop and then checked the flop, so his range contains many A/K/Q pairs and strong broadway hands that dominate me. Betting OOP with pure air is a marginal bluff and will get called or raised by hands that beat me. With deep stacks there’s no need to force a marginal spot — I’ll check and concede the initiative. If Flash bets I will fold to meaningful pressure and only continue if the river gives a real, clear improvement (pairing my 6 or 10) or if I’m offered very favorable pot odds to call a tiny probe. If he checks back I’ll take a free river and re-evaluate then.

The turn 7c is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni has checked for the second time, which might indicate weakness, but this Broadway-heavy board (Ace, King, Queen) is very likely to have hit her calling range or provided her with a straight draw. My pocket threes have almost no showdown value, but they are also not a great candidate to turn into a bluff here because it's difficult to represent a hand that would check the flop but bet the turn on this specific texture. I will check back to see a free river and keep the pot small.

I just rivered the Broadway straight: A-K-Q-J-10 (I hold T♥). That’s a very strong, often-best hand heads‑up, but the board has three clubs (Qc, Jc, 7c) so the main hands that beat me are a made club flush (opponent holding two clubs). Flash has been passive (opened preflop then checked flop/turn), so his range includes lots of single-pair broadways and Ax/Kx/Qx hands that will pay a reasonable river bet. I should extract value rather than checking back. I’ll make a medium value bet (~2/3 pot = 400) to get called by worse pairs and broadways while minimizing the size of a potential check‑raise by a flush. If he check‑raises big (representing clubs) I’ll re‑evaluate and fold to very large river aggression; I’ll call smaller raises. Bet 400 to maximize thin value while controlling risk vs the flush possibility.

The river card is a Jack of Clubs, which completes a broadway straight for any Ten and potentially a backdoor flush for any two clubs. The board is now Ace-King-Queen-Jack-7. My pocket threes have almost zero showdown value, as almost any hand in Minni's range that called pre-flop and checked through the flop and turn has now paired up or bettered my hand. Minni is leading for 400 into a 600 pot. Given the coordination of the board, it is highly likely she has at least a pair or a Ten. There is no reason to call with 5th pair, and raising as a bluff is far too risky given how many hands in her range will have connected with this board. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening very wide to seize the initiative and use position postflop. 4♣7♥ is marginal but acceptable in a heads-up open-raising range — it has some straight/flush potential and works well as part of a wide steal range. Raising to the 2xBB minimum (200 total) is cheap relative to our 28k stack, creates fold equity, and avoids playing a limp-defense battle out of position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small defenses and play aggressively on favorable flops using my positional advantage.

I hold KTo in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand for defending against a button opening range in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. Minni has made a min-raise to 200. Folding is not an option given the strength of my hand and the excellent pot odds. While I could 3-bet to take control of the pot, KTo is often dominated by the top of an opponent's calling range (like AK, AQ, or KQ) and plays reasonably well in a single-raised pot. I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable against the larger stack.

On a board of Tc-Th-Js, I have flopped trips with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand, especially in a heads-up pot. My opponent, Minni, was the pre-flop aggressor. Standard strategy here is to check to the raiser to allow them to continue with a continuation bet (c-bet). If I lead out, I might force her to fold her air hands, whereas checking gives her the opportunity to bluff or bet for value with hands like AJ, KJ, or pocket pairs. I will check and see how she reacts.

Heads-up and in position on a dry but paired board (Tc Th Js). My 4♣7♥ has virtually no showdown value or meaningful draws, so the best line is a continuation bet to seize the initiative and fold out missed overcards and weak holdings that called preflop. I’ll bet ~60% pot (250 into 400) to apply pressure; if Flash raises significantly I will fold (we have negligible equity vs made tens/jacks), but if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improvement or clear pot-control spots. This keeps me aggressive in position and consistent with my preflop plan to bet favorable flops and fold to strong resistance.

I have flopped trip tens with a King kicker, which is a monster hand in a heads-up pot. My opponent, Minni, has made a continuation bet of 250 into a 400 pot. While the board is somewhat coordinated with straight draws (KQ, Q9, 98) and a Jack, I believe calling is the most profitable line here. Raising now might scare away her bluffs or weaker holdings. By calling, I keep her range wide, disguise the strength of my hand, and allow her to continue firing on the turn with air or marginal value hands like a Jack. I will look to extract more value on the later streets.

I have trip Tens with a King kicker on a board of Tc-Th-Js-6c. This is a very strong hand. On the flop, I check-called Minni's continuation bet to disguise my strength and allow her to continue bluffing or value-betting with a Jack. The turn 6c is mostly a blank. By checking again, I am maintaining the same story—that I might have a Jack or a straight draw—encouraging her to fire a second barrel. If I bet now, I risk folding out her bluffs, whereas checking keeps her entire range involved and allows me to extract more value if she decides to continue her aggression.

Board: Tc Th Js 6c — paired tens with a jack. My 4c7h has no pair and no realistic draws; after the turn I have only 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13%) to make a single low pair, and even then I’m likely behind to any Ten/Jack or better that called my flop bet. Flash called my 250 flop c-bet, which indicates a relatively strong or sticky range, so barreling the turn would be a thin, high-variance bluff with poor fold equity. With deep stacks and a small pot (900), the +EV line is pot control: check the turn and fold to a bet. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and only continue with a real improvement or a highly credible bluff card/blocker.

On a board of Tc-Th-Js-6c-Jd, I have a full house (Tens full of Jacks) with my pocket Td and Kh. While this is a very strong hand, the river Jd is a significant scare card. Any Jack in Minni's range (which is wide given her button open) now makes Jacks full of Tens, which beats my Tens full of Jacks. Minni bet the flop but checked the turn, which could indicate a Jack looking for pot control or a missed hand. By checking the river, I can control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff from her air. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, as only a Jack or the last remaining Ten would call, and the Jack would beat me while the Ten would likely result in a chop or a win based on my King kicker. Checking is the most prudent play to see how she evaluates the river.

Board is Tc Th Js 6c Jd; my 4c7h has no pair, no relevant blockers and essentially zero showdown value vs the range that called my flop c-bet. Flash called my flop bet (range: any J, any T, Tx/Jx combos, strong Ax/Kx occasionally) and then checked the turn and river — a passive line that usually means medium-strong hands or showdown-control, not hands that will fold readily to a river bluff. The river J actually improves many of the hands that called flop (making trips/full houses if they hold a J or a T), so my fold equity is very low. Pot is only 900 while I still have a big stack, but bluffing here is high-variance and +EV only in very narrow reads I don’t have. Best line is pot-control: check and go to showdown; if Flash bets I’ll fold to any meaningful sizing. Therefore: check.

In heads-up play, Ace-high hands like Ace-Six offsuit are well above the average starting hand strength and should be played aggressively from the button (small blind). Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop play with a range advantage.

Facing a standard button raise to 300 (pot 400). I must call 200 more (total commitment 300). Pot odds: 200 to win 600 -> need ~25% equity. 4♥5♣ is a weak but playable heads-up hand with reasonable equity vs a wide SB/BTN opening range (it can make straights, two-pair, and occasional disguised wins). With deep effective stacks the price to see a flop is cheap and calling to realize equity is correct. I won't 3‑bet — 4‑5o is a poor bluff/iso hand (few blockers, and I'd be out of position postflop), and a min 3‑bet to 500 doesn't buy much fold equity for the extra risk. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to see flop, play cautiously OOP — continue with pairs/draws and fold to heavy aggression on dry good-for-opener boards.

Check. Board is 2c-3c-4c and my 4h5c gives me a pair of fours plus a four‑card club draw and a double‑gutshot straight (I need an A or a 6). That’s a lot of turn/river outs (9 club outs + 8 straight outs with 2 overlaps ≈ 15 distinct cards), so I have strong equity to improve by the river (~50%+). However, Flash was the preflop raiser and his button raising range contains a lot of clubs — he could already have a flush. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand and allow him to raise/price me out when he has a club. Better to keep the pot under control and realize equity: check and call reasonable turn bets to see if I improve or get a better read. If he checks behind I get a free card; if he bets small I’ll often call given the strong draw/paired board; if he barrels big or jams, I’ll fold unless I’ve improved.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high and significant equity on this 2c-3c-4c monotone board. I hold the 6 of clubs, giving me a flush draw, and I also have an open-ended straight draw (any 5 makes a 6-high straight). Since Minni checked, I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or build value for my draws.

Call 300. I'm being asked to call 300 into a 900 pot (pot will be ~1200 after my call) so I only need ~20% equity to break even. My hand (4♥5♣) is top pair (pair of fours) plus a four‑card club draw and straight outs (A or 6). Counting distinct outs (9 clubs + 6 non‑club A/6 + 2 remaining fours ≈ 17) gives well over the break‑even threshold (roughly ~36% to hit on the turn, ~60% to improve by river). Flash’s button raising range is wide and contains plenty of bluffs and single‑club hands, but only a subset have a made flush; raising OOP here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and is unnecessary given the cheap price. Plan: call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable OOP, then re‑evaluate the turn — call small/medium bets if I still have strong equity and fold to large overbets/shoves unless I’ve improved to a clear winner.

Board 2c-3c-4c-9c with my 4h-5c: I currently have a made club flush (5-high) but it’s a medium-strength flush — any club higher than a 5 (6c,7c,8c, Tc,Jc,Qc,Kc,Ac) beats me. Flash (button) opened preflop and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn. That check widens his checking range (missed hands, one-pair/overs, some turned/failed draws) and makes it profitable to take the initiative. Betting now extracts value from worse hands (non-club pairs, single overcards, straight draws) and charges any clubs/backs that might draw or call; it also denies a free river card. I’ll make a value bet around half-pot to induce calls from worse and to avoid overcommitting to a turn raise. If he check-raises big or jams I’ll give him credit for a higher club and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river.

On the turn, the 9 of clubs puts four clubs on the board. I have the 6 of clubs, giving me a 6-high flush. While this is a made hand, it is extremely vulnerable as any club higher than the 6 (7c, 8c, Tc, Jc, Qc, Kc, Ac) currently beats me. Minni's lead of 600 into the 1200 pot represents strength, likely a flush or at least a high club blocker. However, with pot odds of 3:1, folding a made flush is too conservative, as she could be betting a straight, two pair, or a total bluff. Raising is unwise because I am unlikely to get called by worse and will be in a terrible spot if she reraises. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see the river.

Check. The river 2d radically worsens my situation relative to the turn: my 5-high club flush (5c) is still a made hand but is extremely vulnerable — any club in Flash’s hand that isn’t on the board is higher than my 5c, and the paired deuce on the river allows full-house/quads combos (pocket 3s/4s/9s, or 2+3/2+4/2+9 combos) that now beat me. Flash’s line (raise pre, c-bet flop, check‑call my turn raise) is club‑heavy and contains many hands that either already beat me or can now trap; his river check could be passive or a trap. There are very few worse hands that will reliably call a river bet (there are effectively no lower flushes to extract value from), so betting mainly bloats the pot and gives a good hand (higher flush/full house) a chance to raise me off my equity. Checking gains showdown for my vulnerable but sometimes‑winning flush while avoiding value‑losing check‑raises or large bluffs from full houses/quads. I’ll check and evaluate any river bet (call small/medium bets if they look like thin value or bluffs, fold to large, polarized raises).

I have a 6-high flush on a board with four clubs and a paired deuce. Minni led into me on the turn when the fourth club arrived, which often indicates she has a flush or at least a very strong club blocker. Her check on the river could be a sign of weakness, but given that my flush is very low (nearly any other club in her range beats me), there is no value in betting. If I bet, she will likely only call or raise with hands that beat me (higher flushes or a full house) and fold hands I already beat (straights or bluffs). Checking back allows me to reach showdown and potentially win against a missed draw or a smaller hand that she played aggressively on the turn.

Heads-up on the button with 5♥3♣ — a weak hand but fine to open in heads-up because of position and large fold equity. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is to 200; I’ll raise to 200 (total committed) to take the initiative and try to steal the big blind. I’m using a small, standard button open to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/A-high boards, folding to strong resistance.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c 2c. Minni, who has been aggressive from the button, has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds (or 4:1 to see the flop). In heads-up play, 92s is a marginal but acceptable hand to defend the big blind against a small raise, especially given the suited potential. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply without bloating the pot out of position.

I have 9-high on a connected board of 7s-6h-4d. This flop does not interact with my 9c 2c at all, as I have no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin backdoor straight possibilities. In heads-up play, the standard approach from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I have very little equity to continue and will likely fold, but for now, checking is the correct fundamental play.

Flop 7s 6h 4d with my 5h3c gives me a completed 3-4-5-6-7 straight — a very strong made hand. Flash called preflop from the big blind, so his continuing range is wide (many top/second pairs, pocket pairs, some 5x/one-card draws). Very few hands currently beat me (primarily 5+8 combos); most of his range is behind and will pay to see more. I should bet for value and to charge any draws while building the pot with a hand that wants action. Bet ~75% pot (300 into 400) to get called by 7x/6x/pocket pairs and charge draws. If he flats I’ll continue extracting on safe turns and re-evaluate versus a check-raise (which would often indicate a rare higher straight or a big bluff).

On a board of 7s-6h-4d, my 9c 2c has completely missed. I have no pair, no flush draw, and the board is highly connected, favoring a range that might include many middle cards or overpairs. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 300 bet into a 400 pot. With only 9-high and negligible equity (no real backdoor potential or gutshots), calling or raising out of position would be a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with J7o. In a heads-up situation, J7o is a playable hand but not particularly strong. Minni, in the Big Blind, has a significant chip lead. I have 13,350 chips, which is over 130 big blinds. A standard raise to 3x (300 total) is generally appropriate from the button to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand. If Minni raises, I can consider folding, but for now, I will open the pot.

Face-up 3bb open to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with Qc6s — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Flash has shown wide opening ranges to 300 frequently, and effective stacks (13,100 vs my 26,500) are deep enough to realize implied odds. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is a cheap way to see a flop and keep the pot manageable out of position. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with Q6o (not enough value or fold equity vs a wide button range), and a small min‑raise would be unattractive. Plan: call, evaluate the flop; fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, but continue with top pair, good draws, or favourable runouts.

I have Q♣6♠ on a 3♥6♣9♠ flop — second pair with a decent queen kicker in a heads‑up spot. Flash has opened very wide from the button and c‑bets frequently, so I’m often ahead of a large portion of his range but my hand is vulnerable OOP to 9x, 6x, sets, and two‑pairs. The board is relatively dry (no flush draws, limited straight outs), so pot control is preferable. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable and induce the button to c‑bet with his wide range. Plan: check‑call small/medium continuation bets to realize value against bluffs and worse made hands, re‑evaluate on the turn (become more aggressive if I improve to two pair/trips or face a check), and fold to heavy raises or very large bets that represent strong made hands. This line maximizes my chance to extract value while avoiding committing OOP with a marginal holding.

On a dry flop of 9s-6c-3h, my J7o hasn't improved, but it remains a decent candidate for a continuation bet. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a standard sized bet of 300 (half pot) is likely to fold out many of her non-pairing hands like overcards that missed. This board doesn't heavily favor her Big Blind defending range more than my Button opening range, so applying pressure now is the most profitable play to take down the 600 chip pot immediately.

I have Q♣6♠ on 3♥-6♣-9♠ — second pair with a decent kicker in a heads‑up spot. Flash (button/SB) opens very wide and c‑bets frequently; the flop bet is small (300 into 900, ~1/3 pot) so a large portion of his range is bluffs or worse single‑pair/overcard hands. Calling here is the right, low‑risk line: pot control OOP, realize equity and extract value from worse hands while folding to heavy aggression. Pot odds are ~25% to call (300 to win ~1,200), and Q6 on this dry board should have that equity vs a wide c‑betting range. Plan: call the 300, re‑evaluate on the turn — call small turn bets or fold to a very large raise; if he checks the turn I can consider a small value bet or take the showdown.

I flopped second pair (6s) and the turn paired my queen, giving me two pair (Q6). Heads‑up against Flash — who opens very wide and c‑bets a lot — this is a strong made hand and I should be extracting value while denying a free river card to floats. Betting half the pot (~600 into 1200) gets value from worse pairs, one‑pair floats and bluffs, keeps the pot controllable against rare sets or Q9 combos, and prevents giving a free card that could outdraw me. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises I’ll judge by sizing but will be willing to continue against small/medium aggression because two pair is ahead of a large portion of his range.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds appears, and Minni leads for 600 (a half-pot bet). My Jack-high has very little equity and no real draw to improve. Minni's lead suggests she either hit the Queen, has a middle pair, or is attempting a play. Since I have no showdown value and would be floating with a very weak hand against a chip leader, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Ac4d — an ace is a strong open in heads-up because of blocker value and good postflop playability. Flash (BB) is an active opponent who often opens to 300, so a standard 3x open from the button both fits the table dynamics and builds fold equity. I’ll raise to seize the initiative: if he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I plan to c-bet on ace/high or dry boards and play carefully on coordinated/paired boards. If he 3-bets big and polarizes (very large sizing), I’ll fold this weak ace; I may defend/call small 3-bets depending on sizing and postflop plan.

I am holding Deuce of Spades and Six of Hearts in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni, who currently has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 200 more to see a flop, 2-6 offsuit has very poor playability and is frequently dominated by the opponent's raising range. Defending with such a weak hand out of position often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am forced to fold to further aggression. Conserving my stack for better spots is the more prudent play here.

I hold Tc 9c on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. This is a strong suited connector well within my opening range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot or take down the blinds immediately.

Flash (button) opened to 300. In heads-up this is a very wide opening range and A♦J♥ is a top-tier hand vs that range. With deep effective stacks I want both value and initiative — 3-betting isolates and gets worse Ax and broadways to fold or call. I’ll raise above the min to give myself fold equity but keep stacks deep enough to play postflop if called. Raise to 1200 total (well above the 500 min) is a clean size: big enough to punish speculative holdings, small enough to continue postflop. Postflop plan: continuation-bet dry textures, value-bet A-high boards (top pair), and be cautious on coordinated/flushy boards; re-evaluate vs large 4-bets (fold to extreme shove sizing if he polarizes).

I have Ten-Nine suited on the button, which is a premium suited connector in heads-up play. Minni has 3-bet to 1200, which is a fairly large sizing, but with my position and the playability of this hand, a call is standard. 4-betting would likely overplay the hand against a potential monster, while folding would be too tight. I will call the additional 900 to see a flop in position.

I 3‑bet preflop to take the initiative and am still the aggressor on this flop. The board (3♠ 8♣ 3♦) is low and paired — a fairly dry texture that misses a lot of Flash’s flatting/calling range (broadways, A‑x, many small suited connectors). I have A♢J♡: two overcards and no made hand, but I block some A‑3 combos and have decent fold equity as the preflop raiser. I want to continue representing a strong range and force many worse hands to fold while keeping the pot manageable if called. A small-to-medium c‑bet is best: ~1/3 pot gives good fold equity and keeps decision trees simple on the turn. Bet 800 into 2400 (~33% pot). Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (if I hit an Ace/Jack I’ll value bet; if a blank and he shows aggression I’ll give up to large polarized raises / check‑raises; I may call small turn bets to probe but fold to strong lines that polarize).

On a paired board of 3s-8c-3d, Minni leads with a small continuation bet of 800 into a 2400 pot (1/3 pot sizing). I have Ten-high with a backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the 8. Given the excellent pot odds (4:1) and the fact that I am in position, calling is the most appropriate move. A fold would be too exploitable against a standard c-bet on this dry board, and a raise would unnecessarily bloat the pot with a weak hand. Calling allows me to see how the turn develops and potentially outplay the opponent on later streets.

Turn pairs the 8 and makes the board 3♠‑8♣‑3♦‑8♦ — a double‑paired, highly coordinated texture that improves many hands in Flash’s flop‑call range (any 8x or 3x becomes trips/full house; some pocket pairs and A‑x also remain). I only have A♦J♥, which at best is Ace‑kicker on a two‑pair board (i.e. a weak showdown hand vulnerable to any 3/8 in his hand). My flop c‑bet was small and was called, so villain’s calling range is wide and contains enough hands that now crush me to make a turn bluff risky. Betting here would fold out some weaker A‑highs but will also get raised or jammed by the hands that beat me; with Flash’s effective stack (~10.7k) I don’t want to bloat the pot and face a polarized raise. I’ll check to control the pot and get to showdown cheaply: if he checks back I can show down my Ace‑kicker; if he bets a small/medium size I can call as a bluff‑catcher; if he jams big I will fold to preserve a large stack. Check.

The board has double-paired with 3s-8c-3d-8d. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, has now checked the turn. This check could represent a trap with a high overpair (AA-JJ) or a full house, or it could be a surrender with Ace-high hands like AK or AQ. My Ten-high has very little showdown value, but betting here is risky as Minni's range remains strong on this board texture. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, potentially hitting a Ten or Nine to improve, or simply reaching a cheap showdown if the river goes check-check. Bluffing into a 3-bet range on a double-paired board is often ineffective, so pot control is the priority.

I hold A♦J♥ on 3♠‑8♣‑3♦‑8♦‑J♠ — that’s two pair (Jacks and Eights). It’s a very strong showdown hand versus a lot of Flash’s calling range, but the board is double‑paired so any 3 or 8 (or pocket J’s) crush me (trips/full house). Flash’s line (open, flat my 3‑bet, check‑call my small flop c‑bet, check the turn) is ambiguous — it contains many A/x and medium pairs I beat, but also some 3/8 combos that now dominate me after the turn. Pot is only 4,000 and Flash still has ~10.7k behind; I don’t want to bloat the pot and get committed vs a polarized raise. I’ll check to control the pot and get to showdown cheaply: if he checks back I win the pot; if he bets a small/medium size I’ll call as a strong value/bluff‑catcher; if he jams big I’ll fold to avoid risking a large chunk of my stack to what is most likely a full house. Check.

The board has double-paired with 3s-8c-3d-8d and a Jack on the river. My Ten-Nine is effectively playing the board's two pairs (8s and 3s) with a Jack kicker, as my Ten is lower than the Jack on the board. In a 3-bet pot, Minni's range is heavily weighted toward Ace-high, King-high, and pocket pairs. Even if Minni has 'given up' with a hand like AK or AQ, she still wins with a higher kicker than the Jack on the board. While a bluff could theoretically push out Ace-high, it is highly risky given the pot size and the likelihood that Minni might call with any Ace or better on such a dry, double-paired board. I will check back to reach a showdown, acknowledging that I likely lose to most of Minni's range, but preserving my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with J♦4♠: this is a marginal but playable hand in position. I want to take the initiative, deny a free flop and use positional advantage postflop, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 2BB (to 200 total). Current pot is 150; raising to 200 means I add 150 more now and if called the pot will be ~400 — small relative to my 29k stack. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold and preserve chips; if he calls I can play the flop in position and leverage fold equity and postflop playability of a Jack.

In a heads-up situation, Q5o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind against a min-raise. I am getting 3-to-1 odds on a call (100 to win a total pot of 300), which means I only need about 25% equity to make this call profitable. Folding would be overly tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength. I will call to see the flop.

In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I have Queen-high with no draws on a dry 9-6-2 rainbow board. There is no reason to lead out with a bet here, as I have no equity and want to see what the pre-flop aggressor does. I will check.

Heads-up with position on a very dry flop (9s‑2c‑6d). I was the preflop aggressor and Flash’s check is noncommittal — he called a wide range preflop that contains many missed hands and weak pairs that will fold to pressure. A standard half‑pot c‑bet builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, applies fold equity (calling 200 to win ~800 gives only ~25% odds), and keeps the pot manageable if called. If he check‑raises big I can fold J4 and preserve my deep stack; if he calls I can re‑evaluate on the turn. Action: bet half the pot.

I have Queen-high with no draws on a dry 9-6-2 rainbow board. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, Q5o has very little playability and equity on this texture. I lack any significant backdoor draws, and calling here would essentially be a 'float' with the intention of taking the pot away later or hitting a lucky pair. Given Minni's active betting style, I prefer to fold my weakest hands like this and wait for better opportunities to defend my big blind.

In heads-up play, Q6o is a hand well within the standard opening range from the button (Small Blind). I have 104.5 big blinds, and while Minni has a significant chip lead, a standard 3x open (300 total) allows me to maintain pressure and take control of the hand. Calling would be too passive, and folding is too tight for this spot.

Facing a 300 open from the SB/button with Js-2c in the big blind. This is a weak hand but in heads-up I should defend a wide range — calling 200 (300 total) gives correct pot odds to see a flop vs a wide open. I don't want to 3-bet with this garbage hand: a min-raise to 500 is just a weak bluff that may get called or re-raised and commits little equity. With deep stacks I can call, see a flop cheaply, and fold to heavy action if I completely miss; if I pair my Jack or pick up backdoor draws I can proceed. Call to 300 and proceed cautiously post-flop.

I defended a wide heads-up range preflop, but on Kc‑4d‑7s my Js2c has missed — no pair and no meaningful draw. The preflop raiser’s range contains lots of kings and broadways that connect with this flop, so leading as a bluff is low EV and risky. I’ll control the pot by checking and be prepared to fold to any significant bet; if the button checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a dry, King-high board that favors my opening range. This is a standard continuation bet spot to put pressure on the opponent's missed hands or weak holdings. A half-pot bet should be sufficient to take the pot down immediately or define the opponent's range.

I defended a very wide heads‑up range preflop, but the Kc‑4d‑7s flop completely misses Js2c. The pot is 900 and the bet is 300, so calling would require ~25% equity to be break‑even (300 to win 1,200). My realistic equity to improve to a winning hand by river is only ~12–13% (about 3 outs to hit a Jack; there are no realistic backdoor flush/straight draws), so calling is +EV negative. A raise here would be a thin bluff into a range that hits this board well (lots of Kx and broadways) and would bloat the pot out of position. Preserve the 29k stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button, T♠3♦ is a playable hand and we should be aggressive. With deep stacks and position, opening a wide range is standard — raising maximizes fold equity and lets us play postflop in position when called. Min-raise to 200 (2x BB) is a small, efficient steal that keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold to heavy pressure; call smaller 3-bets and play for position; if called see flop and use position to c-bet or control pot size depending on texture. This raise maximizes EV vs a relatively passive BB.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 6h. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 200 from the button. I only need to call 100 more chips to see the flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While my hand is weak, the price is too good to fold in a heads-up situation against an aggressive opponent. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate my position then.

I have 6-high on an Ace-high board with a pair of 9s. This board is very likely to have connected with Minni's pre-flop raising range. I have no pair and no real draw. I will check and evaluate her action, though I'm prepared to fold to most bets given my lack of equity.

Heads-up on the button I opened wide preflop, so I have a strong continuation-bet range here. The flop A♥9♥9♦ is paired with an ace — my T♠3♦ has no showdown value and no real draw. Flash checked, which often indicates a wide calling or medium-strength range rather than a monster. I want to apply pressure and take down the pot now vs hands that missed (Kx, Qx, Jx, small pairs, and some Ax weaker than top pair) while keeping the sizing manageable vs his 10.6k stack. Bet half-pot (200 into 400) keeps fold equity high, preserves chips if called, and allows me to fold to strong resistance (check-raise or large raise). Plan: c-bet 200; fold to a large raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on his action.

I have 6-high on an Ace-high board with paired 9s. Minni bet 200 into a 400 pot. This board is excellent for her pre-flop range and provides me with no equity or draws. Continuing with 6-high here would be a waste of chips.

I have pocket sixes on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot, as I am likely ahead of Minni's random big blind range.

I’m on the big blind with T8o facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the button (SB). T8o is marginal but perfectly defendable heads-up — it has showdown value, some straight/boat potential, and blocks some broadway combos. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position-independent fold equity, and gets me good pot odds (I need to call 200 more into a 400 pot → ~3:1). I don’t want to 3-bet light here with a mediocre offsuit hand and allow the shorter-stacked opponent to commit or isolate; calling lets me see a flop and play post-flop (check/call or float on favorable boards, fold to heavy aggression on dry misses, play aggressively with top pair or strong draws). Action: call to 300 (I’ve already posted 100 BB, so this is 200 more).

Check. I’m out of position with Ts 8c on 3d 9c 6c — a very marginal hand: only a 4‑out gutshot to a 7 (≈8.5% to hit on the turn, ≈16.4% to hit by the river) and a runner‑runner club backdoor (~4.2% by the river). Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with little equity and put me in difficult spots if Flash calls or raises. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets Flash blockbuster his wide opening range so I can respond based on bet size and read: call very small probes (≈100) because immediate pot odds become reasonable, but fold to medium/large c‑bets that demand ~30%+ equity (which my draw doesn’t have). If he checks back I get a free turn to continue or barrel selectively when I improve (7 or a club) or pick up a read.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a 3d-9c-6c board, which is an extremely strong hand. The board features a flush draw (two clubs), so I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. Minni checked to me, so I will make a standard value bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot to build the pot and charge any draws or top-pair hands she might be holding.

Fold. I’m out of position with T♠8♣ on 3♦9♣6♣ and I only have a 4‑out gutshot to a 7 (≈8.5% on the turn, ≈16.4% by the river) plus a tiny runner‑runner club chance (~4.2% by the river).
