The pot was 1,000 and Flash bet 400 (pot becomes 1,400); calling 400 to see the turn would make the final pot 1,800 so I need ≈22.2% equity to break even. My realistic equity vs a sensible c‑bet range (lots of pairs, some clubs and overcards, and many thin value hands) is below that threshold. Raising is unattractive — I have weak blockers, poor equity and would be OOP against a short stack that can call or shove. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening very wide. 6♣2♠ is a poor hand, but folding 50 into 150 is too passive and misses easy steal opportunities. I’ll make the min-raise to take the initiative and apply pressure to the BB — commit a total of 200 (I’ve already posted 50, so I’m adding 150). This keeps the pot small while giving me fold equity; if Flash 3-bets big I can fold cheaply, and if he flats I have positional advantage to play postflop and try to win the pot with aggression or well-timed bluffing.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has made a min-raise to 200. In heads-up play, I am getting 3:1 odds to call (100 more into a 300 pot). King-high has significant defensive value against a wide button opening range. Folding would be overly tight given the pot odds and the nature of heads-up play. I will call to see a flop and play post-flop.

The flop is 8c-Qd-Qs. I have King-high (Kh-3d). Minni has checked, and as the Big Blind who defended pre-flop, I am now first to act (in heads-up the BB is out of position on the flop, but the log says Minni raised and I called). Wait, the log shows 'Minni raised to 200, Flash checked/called 100'. In standard heads-up, the SB/Button acts first pre-flop and the BB acts first post-flop. The board is paired (QQ). I will check to the raiser to see how they react to this board, as it is a board that likely missed both of us or hit a Queen in their range.

I opened wide from the button and have position. Board is Q♦ Q♠ 8♣ and my 6♣2♠ has virtually no showdown value, so this is a pure continuation-bluff spot. Flash checked from the BB, which often indicates weakness or a wide calling range (missed broadways, small pairs, weak A/K hands). I want to take the initiative and force folds from those holdings while preserving pot control if called. I’ll bet a meaningful half-to-two-thirds-pot size to present a believable queen and generate fold equity. With the pot at 400 I’ll bet 250 on the flop — that means my total committed in the hand will be 200 (preflop) + 250 = 450. If he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check-raises large I can fold since I have almost no equity.

Minni has made an overbet (450 into 400) on a paired board (8c-Qd-Qs). While King-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, calling a bet of this size with no draw and only one overcard to the board's non-paired card (8) is generally unprofitable. Minni's large sizing is designed to put maximum pressure on my range. Given the stack sizes and the polarizing nature of the bet, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot rather than potentially bleeding chips with a marginal high-card hand.

I am on the button with 76s, which is a very strong hand for heads-up play. My standard strategy has been to raise most buttons to 300 to take control of the hand and apply pressure. Against Minni, who has a significant chip lead, I want to continue being aggressive when I have playable suited connectors like this.

Opponent opened to 300 from the button (standard heads-up open). I’m in the big blind with 3♦2♠ — a very weak, low-card hand that has some backdoor possibilities but poor playability OOP. Pot is ~400 and it costs me 200 more to call (total commitment = 300), so my immediate pot odds are ~200/(400+200)=33%. The chance to connect with at least one pair on the flop is ~32%, so a flat call is marginally justified and gives correct pot odds and implied odds with deep stacks. I don’t want to 3‑bet and build a big pot with junk out of position; I’ll call to see a flop and plan to fold to significant aggression if I miss, or extract value/continue as a semi‑bluff only if I pick up a strong draw or pair.

I completely missed the flop with 3♦2♠ — no pair, no real straight draw (only a very remote backdoor wheel requiring both A and 4), and there are two hearts on board so I have no flush equity. I’m out of position with a ~600‑chip pot and the opponent has a wide opening range. My priority is pot control: I don’t want to build a multi‑street pot with junk or inflate the pot OOP via a bluff. Checking keeps my range wide, lets me fold to aggression, and allows me to see a free turn to reassess if a genuinely relevant card (A or 4 or a paired board) appears. I will check and fold to most continuation bets; only consider calling a very tiny c‑bet that gives correct pot odds to chase the remote backdoors, otherwise re‑evaluate if the action checks through.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 7s 6s on a Jh 5h 8c board. As the pre-flop aggressor, after Minni checks to me on the flop, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This allows me to take the pot down immediately if she missed, or build the pot for when I potentially hit my straight on the turn or river. I'll bet 300, which is half the current pot.

I completely missed this flop with 3♦2♠ — no pair, no real straight draw and no heart. Villain opened preflop and now bets 300 into the ~600 pot (bet makes pot ~900), so calling 300 would have me invest 300 to win 1200 (I need ~25% equity). My hand has essentially zero equity vs a reasonable heads‑up c‑bet range (Jx, 8x, 5x, heart draws, some overcards and semi‑bluffs) and I won’t get good implied odds OOP with junk. With a large healthy stack I should avoid spewing chips in marginal spots; fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 6h7s — a marginal, connected offsuit hand. The pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to complete, which gives ~3:1 pot odds (enough to see a flop). Raising to the minimum (200) is an option, but Flash has shown an inclination to reraise preflop (large 3-bets), and I don’t want to inflate the pot or face big isolation raises with this hand. With positional advantage postflop I prefer to complete and see a flop, use position to realize equity, and fold to heavy preflop/strong postflop aggression if I miss. Call to 100 total.

I have pocket threes in the big blind. Minni has limped from the small blind. Pocket pairs are strong in heads-up play, and I want to take the initiative and define the hand. A raise here will likely take the pot down immediately or allow me to lead the betting on the flop. If she calls, I have a clear plan for various board textures.

Call to 300 total. 6h7s is marginal but playable heads-up with the button (positional) advantage and deep stacks — I can realize equity and get good implied odds. Facing a raise to 300, calling keeps the pot manageable and avoids getting 3-bet or bloating the pot with a weak hand against an aggressive opponent. Plan: see the flop, fold to heavy strength on dry boards, and continue/raise only with a pair, two pair, strong draws, or clear equity realizations.

I have pocket threes on a dry King-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture favors my perceived range of high cards and pairs. A continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) is standard here to take down the pot immediately, protect my small pair from overcards, and maintain the initiative. If called, I will need to be cautious on future streets as my hand is relatively weak, but for now, the lead is the most profitable play.

Flash has bet 300 into a 900 pot (I must call 300). Calling requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)=25%). I have no pair and essentially no realistic flush or straight draw on K♦4♠10♦ (6♥7♠): the single heart on the flop with one in my hand can't make a flush by the river, and straights need double-runouts. However Flash has been raising and c-betting wide; his flop bet size is small, so his range is likely broad enough that a float is reasonable. I'm on the button (positional advantage) and can call cheaply to see the turn, plan to stab at pots when he shows weakness, and fold to significant turn aggression. I won't raise — the board is dry, my hand has almost no equity/value, and a bluff-raise will either get called too often or bloat the pot unnecessarily. Call 300 to float and proceed based on turn action.

The Ace on the turn is a very polarizing card. Having called my flop bet on a King-high board, Minni's range likely includes Kings, Tens, or straight draws (like QJ). The Ace improves many of those hands or provides a strong holding for her to call with. My pocket threes have very little showdown value now and are unlikely to force a fold from a better hand if I bet again. Checking back is the most sensible play to control the pot and potentially reach a cheap showdown or see how she reacts to the river.

Flop I floated a small c-bet with 6♥7♠ to use position and stab when Flash showed weakness. The turn A♠ is a scary card, but Flash checked — a sign of pot control/weakness (he could have Kx/Tx or wide c‑bet range now worried about the Ace). My hand has essentially no showdown value and almost no backdoor equity, so I should try to take the pot now rather than punt to showdown. A half‑pot bet (~600 into 1200) credibly represents an Ace/King and gives Flash ~33% pot odds to call, folding a lot of his missed/medium hands. I will not overcommit: if he check‑raises I fold; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the river and fold to significant aggression unless I improve. Bet 600 to take it down now.

My pocket threes are in a very difficult spot. After I checked the turn, which featured an overcard (the Ace) to the already dangerous King and Ten on the flop, Minni led out with a half-pot bet. My hand has almost no showdown value, as it is beaten by any King, Ten, Ace, or any pocket pair higher than threes. While the Ace is a card Minni might bluff on, it is also a card that very likely improved her range if she was floating the flop with an Ace or a gutshot. Given the board texture and her stack lead, calling is unprofitable as I would be 'guessing' on the river with a very weak hand. Folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding T7o on the button. While it's a marginal hand, in heads-up play, the button should be raising a wide range to maintain pressure and capitalize on position. I have been consistently opening for 300 in previous orbits, and continuing this pattern keeps my range balanced and forces the opponent to defend or fold. My goal is to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand going into the flop.

Call. Against a Button open to 300 heads-up their range is wide; J♦2♣ is a weak hand but it’s cheap to defend from the BB. The pot is 400 and the additional cost is 200, so after calling the pot will be 600 — I need roughly 33% equity to justify the call. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a 3-bet with a marginal hand. Plan: see the flop and continue only with a jack, a backdoor or made/strong draw or a favorable board; fold to heavy aggression when missed.

Check. I defended preflop with J♦2♣ and this board (Th 8s 7s) is very connected and favors the preflop raiser. I have no pair but I have a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs) plus three jacks = 7 outs (~28% to improve by river). Out of position I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand — check to control the pot and see a free turn. Plan: call small continuation bets (roughly up to ~1/3 pot ≈ 200) to realize equity, fold to pot-sized or polarizing aggression, and only continue aggressively if I pair or complete the straight/draw or pick up a very favorable turn.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair (Tens and Sevens) on a coordinated board of Th-8s-7s. While my hand is very strong in a heads-up context, the board is quite 'wet' with both straight and flush draws available (any 9 makes a straight, and there are two spades). Since Minni checked to me, I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against these draws. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard sizing that allows me to get called by top pair (10x), middle pair (8x), or various draws while building the pot.

Call. I have J♦2♣ on T♥ 8♠ 7♠ — no pair but a gutshot to 9 (4 outs) plus three jacks = 7 outs (~28% to improve by river). Flash’s flop stab is 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 gives pot odds of 300/(900+300)=25%, so my ~28% equity justifies a call. I’m out of position and the board is connected with two spades (flush possibilities), so I don’t want to inflate the pot or build a big pot with a marginal hand — folding to pot-sized or polarizing turn/river aggression is fine. Plan: call to realize equity; if I pair a jack or hit the 9 I can proceed aggressively, otherwise be willing to check/fold to large bets or barrels.

Board T♥ 8♠ 7♠ 4♠, my hand J♦2♣ — just jack-high with only ~7 outs (three jacks + four 9s ≈ 28% to improve). The turn completed a three‑spade board so any single spade in Flash’s hand now makes a flush. Flash has shown preflop and flop aggression, so I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand. There’s no bet to call on the turn (pot 1200), so the disciplined line is to check and see a free river. If Flash bets the river I’ll fold to significant pressure unless I pair or make the straight; if he checks back I can consider a small delayed bluff or value bet only if I improve.

On the turn, the 4 of spades completes a potential flush. While I have a strong two pair (Tens and Sevens), the board is now quite dangerous. I do hold the Ten of spades, which is a valuable blocker to the flush, reducing the number of spade combinations Minni can hold. However, given that Minni has a massive stack and has checked to me, checking back is the safest and most strategic play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my showdown value, and see what the river brings without risking a check-raise on a wet board.

River paired the 8s (T♥ 8♠ 7♠ 4♠ 8♥). I never improved — J♦2♣ has no showdown value here against a BTN raiser who c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn. Checking guarantees a near‑certain loss of the 1,200 pot. A small delayed bluff is the highest EV line: the pot is 1,200, so a bet of 400 (total commit = 400) makes the pot 1,600 and only needs ~25% fold equity to be +EV (400/1600). Flash’s passive turn check narrows his range toward many missed hands and medium pairs that can fold a small river stab. If he calls I lose a modest amount; if he raises I can fold and preserve most of my stack. I size small to maximize fold equity while keeping the risk limited — bet 400 to try to steal the pot.

On the river, the board is Th-8s-7s-4s-8h. My original two pair (Tens and Sevens) has been counterfeited by the pair of Eights on the board. My best five-card hand is now Two Pair, Tens and Eights, with a Seven kicker (Ts-Th-8s-8h-7h). Minni has led out for 400 into a 1200 pot, giving me 5:1 pot odds. While the 4s on the turn completed a potential flush and the 8h on the river is a dangerous card that improves any 8 to trips, the small bet size from Minni could indicate a variety of hands, including a weaker pair, a missed straight draw, or a Ten (which would likely result in a split pot). Given the strength of my hand in a heads-up context and the excellent price, I must call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with Q♣J♥ (offsuit) is a strong, playable hand. I want initiative and to play postflop in position, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call. A raise to 300 (3x BB) balances between getting folds/value and not bloating the pot unnecessarily against a wide defending BB. It gives fold equity, builds a pot with a hand that plays well postflop, and still lets me fold to a very large 3-bet or call smaller 3-bets comfortably given deep stacks. Plan: if called, use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and extract value; if faced with heavy aggression (big 3-bet) I’ll re-evaluate and protect the stack.

I am holding a solid pocket pair (6d, 6h) in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the button, has been raising frequently to apply pressure. Pocket sixes are well ahead of a wide button-opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 to take initiative, define the hand, and potentially end the hand right here or set up a post-flop play where I have represented significant strength.

Call to 900. Heads-up on the button QhJc is a strong, playable hand and I have position — I prefer to call the 3-bet and take advantage of postflop play rather than folding or bloating the pot with a light 4-bet. I need to call 600 into a 1200 pot (~33% immediate pot odds) and against a likely fairly wide 3-bet range my equity is comfortably above that, plus I get important positional and implied odds. A 4-bet bluff is an option but unnecessary here and risks facing a shove from the shorter-stacked opponent; if Flash 5-bets all-in preflop I will fold QJo. Postflop plan: in position I will c-bet favorable Q/J/high-card or drawy flops for value/pressure, control the pot on dry low boards, and fold to heavy, decisive aggression when I have little equity.

I have flopped bottom set (sixes) on a highly coordinated board of Ace-King-Six. This board texture is excellent for my perceived 3-betting range, which includes many strong Aces and Kings. Since Minni called my pre-flop 3-bet, she likely holds cards that connected with this flop, such as Ax or Kx. I will lead with a continuation bet of half the pot to extract value from her marginal made hands and to charge the club flush draw.

I’m on the button with Qh Jc on a Kd–6c–Ac flop after I opened and Villain 3‑bet preflop. Villain bets 900 into the 1800 pre‑bet pot (pot now 2700); calling 900 requires ~25% equity (900/(2700+900)). My hand has no pair but I have position, a backdoor club possibility (Jc) and two broadway cards that can improve on later streets. Flash has shown a fairly wide, aggressive 3‑betting/c‑betting tendency, so his range contains a mix of Ax/Kx/pairs and some bluffs — there’s enough equity and post‑flop playability to continue. Raising here as a bluff is too risky because many hands in his range (Ax/Kx) will continue; folding surrenders too much with decent pot odds and position. Plan: call 900, see the turn, and then fold to large, decisive aggression unless I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; if I improve (Q/J or club) I’ll apply pressure or extract value.

I have a full house (Sixes full of Aces) on a board of Kd-6c-Ac-Ah. This is an exceptionally strong hand. The turn pairing the Ace is a great development because it makes it more likely that Minni will call a bet if she holds an Ace (giving her trip Aces) or a King (giving her Aces and Kings), both of which I beat. Since Minni called both my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of this board. I will bet 1,800 (half the pot) to continue building the pot and extract value from her range, setting up a potential shove on the river.

Pot is 5,400 and Flash has bet 1,800; calling requires ~25% equity (1,800/(5,400+1,800)). My Q♥J♣ is Q-high with no pair and the backdoor club draw is dead after the turn. The only clean outs are pairing my Q or J (≈6 outs), and many of those river pairs still lose to the hands Flash is representing (any A = trips, any K = two pair, many pocket pairs). Flash 3‑bet preflop, fired the flop and barrels the turn — a line heavily weighted to Ax/Kx/value hands with fewer bluffs. Given the pot odds, my very limited effective outs, and the opponent’s line and stack depth (he can shove over a call), this is a clear fold. Raising as a bluff is too thin here because strong parts of his range simply call or shove. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position.

I am holding 2s 4d in the small blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While I have the button, Minni has a significant chip lead and has shown a willingness to defend her big blind and play aggressively post-flop. There is no strategic advantage to completing the blind or attempting a bluff raise with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♦4♥. In heads-up play the button should open very wide — K4o is perfectly fine to raise from here because we get position post-flop and can apply pressure to a wide BB defend range. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; calling gives the BB good odds and surrenders initiative. I’ll make the standard 2x BB/min-raise to 200 to take the lead, pick up fold equity, and play post-flop in position if called. If Flash 3-bets to a substantial size I will fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet/fold or realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce of spades. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given the 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300) and the suited nature of my King, this is a standard defense in a heads-up match. I have enough playability to see a flop and decide my next move based on the texture. Raising would likely be overplaying the hand out of position, and folding would be too conservative for these odds.

I have flopped middle pair (Kings) on a very coordinated board (Kh Ah Jc). In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and this board hits a wide range of Broadway cards, checking to the raiser is the most prudent move. I want to see how Minni reacts to this texture before committing more chips with a weak kicker.

Flop K♥ A♥ J♣ — I hold K♦4♥: a pair of kings (second pair) with a very weak kicker and only a backdoor heart runner-runner possibility. Flash called my preflop raise, so his continuing range is wide and contains a lot of A‑x and broadway hands that dominate me. C‑betting here will often be called by the better part of his range (A‑x, Kx, Jx) and won’t fold enough to make a value bet plus protection profitable. I want pot control and to use position to gain information. Plan: check and evaluate — if he fires a small C‑bet (~≤½ pot, ≲200) I’ll call to realize equity and keep worse Kx/Jx in, but if he overbets or check‑raises I’ll concede this flop (fold) since he’s likely to have an Ace or a very strong hand. If he checks back I’ll take a controlled stab on later streets only on favourable cards. This minimizes getting stacked by A‑x while preserving post‑flop options from position.

On a board of Ah-Kh-Jc-Ts, the texture is extremely coordinated. Any Queen now has a straight (Broadway). While I have a pair of Kings, my kicker (2s) is irrelevant, and I am losing to any Ace or any Queen. Since the flop went check-check, Minni's range is somewhat capped, but she could easily be checking back middle-range hands or draws that have now improved. Betting into this board serves little purpose as I won't get called by many worse hands, and I certainly don't want to get raised. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot and see how Minni responds to the straight possibility.

I opened wide preflop with K♦4♥ and intentionally checked the flop to control the pot — on K♥ A♥ J♣ I only have second pair with a very weak kicker and Flash’s call/call/ check line represents a lot of A‑x and broadway combinations that dominate me. The T♠ on the turn doesn't improve my kicker and actually brings more scary rivers (Q completes broadway straights). Flash checked the turn again, which is consistent with either a weak hand/draw or a cautious Ace. Betting here has very little fold equity and will get called by most better hands and many draws, so it’s a negative‑EV line. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, preserve showdown equity, and re‑evaluate on the river: if Flash leads into me I’ll fold to a large bet (or call small bets to see the river); if checked through I’ll consider a small delayed value bet on safe rivers (non‑A/non‑Q) but otherwise exercise pot control.

The board is very connected with a four-to-a-straight texture (A-K-J-T). Any Queen makes a straight. I have a pair of Kings with a weak kicker. Since the flop and turn both went check-check, it's likely neither of us has a monster, but Minni could easily have a Queen or even an Ace that she's playing cautiously. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't want to be raised off my showdown value. Checking is the best option to try and see a cheap showdown.

I hold K♦4♥ on K♥ A♥ J♣ T♠ 8♣ — second pair with a very weak kicker. Flash called preflop and then checked flop and turn, so his continuing range contains a lot of A‑x and broadway hands that dominate me, plus possible two‑pair/straight/flush combos (Q makes the Broadway straight; three clubs on board mean any two clubs in his hand are a made flush). Betting here has very little fold equity and will be called by most hands that beat me (and fold out many worse hands), so a value bet is thin and high variance. I’ll exercise pot control and take showdown: check to see his card and avoid losing more chips to better hands.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Five offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button (small blind). Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or play the pot with positional advantage if called.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♥9♥ facing a button open to 300 (I need to call 200). Pot is 400, so calling 200 gets me ~33% pot odds (200 / 600). A9s has better than ~33% equity vs a wide button opening range and is very playable OOP: good top-pair potential, backdoor hearts and straight possibilities. With deep stacks, I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop rather than make a tiny, non-committal 3-bet to 500 that won’t buy me much fold equity. I can still 3-bet larger as a polarized play in other spots, but here a call preserves flexibility and balance.
Plan if I call: play aggressively when I connect (A or heart cards) to extract value or get to a comfortable stack control line on dry boards; fold or control pot size when I miss.
Action chosen: call to 300 total.

I have A♥9♥ on J♣ A♠ 5♣ — top pair but a medium/weak kicker. Villain was the button open-raiser (wide range) and the flop contains two clubs so there are some club draws in their checking range. The line I planned preflop was to be aggressive when I connect; this is one of those spots. Betting ~1/2 pot (300) achieves three things: extracts value from worse hands (Jx, Kx, Qx, small pairs and many non‑ace overcards), charges and prices out club draws, and keeps the pot at a controllable size with deep effective stacks. If called I’ll proceed cautiously on turn (value-bet safe cards, check to control on scary cards); if faced with a large raise I’ll give credit and fold unless the sizing/read indicates a bluff.

In this heads-up situation, I opened with a standard raise from the button and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes Ace-Jack-Five with two clubs, which is a very coordinated and high-card heavy board. Minni has led out (donk-bet) for half the pot. While I have bottom pair, this is a very weak hand on this specific board texture. The Ace and Jack are heavily represented in the Big Blind's defending range, and there are numerous turn cards (any club, King, Ten, or Queen) that would further complicate the board or improve an opponent's range. Given that the board favors the caller's range more than the pre-flop aggressor's on a donk-lead here, and my hand has very little potential for improvement, I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button with Q♣? (Qh Tc = QTo). I’ve already posted the SB and calling 50 is just a limp; raising is standard here. Raise to 300 (3x the BB) achieves: (1) takes the initiative and uses position, (2) denies the BB good odds to see a flop cheaply, (3) extracts value from worse hands and applies pressure to a player who frequently defends/raises preflop. QTo is a strong, playable heads-up hand with good equity and postflop playability; the sizing is consistent with my table image. If Flash 3-bets big (e.g. ~900), I will fold QTo; vs a call or a small 3-bet I can continue with position and c-bet on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d2s. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. This is a standard 3x open. Looking at my hand, 92o is very weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player with a significant chip lead. While the cost to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, the lack of playability and the risk of being dominated or bluffed out on later streets makes this an easy fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit. While I have position, this hand is extremely weak and has poor playability. My opponent, Minni, has been active and has a significant chip lead. Attempting to steal with such a low-equity hand against a capable opponent is unnecessary at this stage. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with J♦5♥. In heads-up you should open very wide — J5o is marginal but perfectly fine to play in position. Raising takes the initiative, creates fold equity, and lets me play the flop last. I’ll open to the standard 2x BB (200 total) to keep the pot manageable versus a deep-stacked, somewhat aggressive opponent. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this weak hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or control pot size and extract value when I hit. Limp/calling is too passive here.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6c 2h. Minni, who is currently the chip leader with a significant advantage, has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (Button). This is a min-raise (to 2bb). While the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a pot of 300), 62o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing it out of position against a dominant stack is generally a losing strategy over time, even with a small call. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Q9s is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. As the button/small blind, I should be raising a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and capitalize on my positional advantage post-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Facing a 300 open from the button with Qc2d in the big blind. I only need to put in 200 more (total committed 300 including my posted blind). The pot is 400, so the call gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity) — versus a wide heads-up opening range Q2o has enough showdown equity to justify a cheap defend. I am out of position postflop, which is a drawback, but with deep stacks and a small immediate price it's better to see a flop and fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards than to surrender the blind or make a marginal 3-bet bluff. I’ll call and play cautiously postflop: pot-control with unimproved Q-high, fold to strong action on dry boards, and proceed more aggressively when I pick up meaningful equity.

I defended preflop for price against a wide heads‑up open, but on A♠ K♣ 6♥ my Q♣2♦ is pure air with no backdoor heart/straight help. I’m out of position and have no reason to build the pot — I want to control the size and see how Flash acts. Checking keeps the pot small, preserves stack, and lets me fold to meaningful aggression. Plan: check and fold to a large c‑bet (≈≥1/2 pot); consider calling a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) to see a turn if the price is right; if Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn for a possible small bluff or another check for showdown control.

The flop of As-Kc-6h is very favorable for my pre-flop raising range, as I am more likely to hold an Ace or a King than the big blind defender. Since Minni checked, I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent strength and force folds from her non-pairing hands. This board is dry enough that a single bet often ends the hand right here.

Call 300. The flop bet is a small ~1/3-pot probe on A♠ K♣ 6♥; calling 300 into the 900 pot gives a break‑even equity of roughly 25% and is cheap enough to see a turn. Qc2d is currently air and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to build the pot without improvement — but vs a wide button raising range this small c‑bet is worth one card. Plan: call the flop to keep the pot controllable, fold to a large, committed turn shove or big barrel unless I pair my Q or pick up a useful club/backdoor improvement; if Flash checks the turn I’ll re‑evaluate (can attempt a small bluff on a favorable card or take it to showdown). Calling preserves stack flexibility and exploits the small sizing while avoiding bloating the pot OOP with a weak hand.

Board is A♠ K♣ 6♥ 8♣, I hold Qc2d and the pot is 1,200. I have no pair and very little showdown value — I can only improve to a pair of Queens or twos, and with one card to come I cannot make a 5‑card club flush (opponent could if he holds two clubs). Flash c‑bet the flop (small) then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or a pot‑control line. Betting here as a small bluff gives me fold equity without bloating a pot OOP with a marginal hand. A bet of 400 (≈1/3 pot) requires Flash to call 400 to win 1,600, so I only need about 25% fold frequency for this to be +EV. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I will not over‑commit on the river unless I improve (Q or 2) or the river card gives a very clear bluffing opportunity and he shows further weakness. If he raises turn I will fold my air and preserve my deep stack. Bet small to exploit his check and pick up the pot now.

On a board of As-Kc-6h-8c, I currently hold Queen-high with no draws. I was the pre-flop aggressor and continuation-bet the flop, but Minni called and is now leading into me on the turn for a small amount (400 into 1200). This 'donk lead' often suggests a hand that has improved or a marginal made hand that wants to set the price. Given that I have zero equity and no path to winning the pot without a significant bluff against a larger stack that has shown stickiness in previous hands, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chips.

I’m on the button (small blind) heads-up with J♥8♣. In heads-up play this is a playable hand and my position post-flop is a large advantage — I want to take the initiative. The table’s standard open size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 total to maintain a consistent sizing, gain fold equity against Flash’s wide big-blind defending range, and isolate him to play the flop in position. The size is small relative to stacks (I have ~27.9k, Flash ~11.9k) and maximizes EV by forcing decisions. Post-flop plan: continuation bet favorable boards, proceed cautiously on A/K-high boards, and fold or re-evaluate against large, polarized 3-bets depending on sizing and reads.

In this heads-up situation, I'm in the Big Blind with Q♣ 2♣. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Suited hands with a Queen have reasonable equity and post-flop playability. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 odds. This is a standard defend in heads-up play to prevent the opponent from opening too wide and to capitalize on potential flush or top-pair draws.

I have flopped top pair with Queen of Clubs and Two of Clubs on a 3s-8h-Qs board. While my kicker is the weakest possible, top pair is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Being out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. This allows her to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands, including bluffs or weaker pairs that might fold if I were to lead out. I plan to check-call most standard bet sizes to keep the pot manageable while extracting value from her air.

I raised preflop to take the initiative heads‑up and I’m in position on this flop. Board: 3♠ 8♥ Q♠ — I have middle pair (8♣) with a Jack kicker. Flash’s defend range from the BB vs my 300 open is wide (Qx, overcards, small pairs, spade draws, suited connectors), so I’m ahead of many hands (weaker pairs, bluffs, many draws) but behind Qx/sets and vulnerable to spade runouts. I should continue to take the initiative: a half‑pot c‑bet (~300 into 600) extracts value from worse pairs and draws and denies equity to spade draws and unpaired overcards, while not overcommitting vs a strong check‑raise. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot control vs Q/spade, continue vs passive calls). If he shows strong aggression I’ll respect that and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing. Bet 300.

I have flopped top pair with Queen-Deuce suited. While my kicker is very weak, top pair is a premium hand in a heads-up pot. Minni's half-pot continuation bet of 300 into 600 is standard. I will call here to keep her range wide and avoid bloating the pot against a potentially better Queen. Raising would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or strong draws. Calling allows me to extract value from her bluffs on future streets or reach a relatively cheap showdown.

The Ace of Spades is a highly significant card that changes the board texture considerably. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop in this heads-up match, I need to be cautious. My pair of Queens, which was top pair on the flop, is now middle pair. Furthermore, the Ace is a card that fits well into Minni's pre-flop raising range, and the third spade completes potential flush draws. Leading out here into the pre-flop aggressor on a board that arguably favors her range is risky. By checking, I can control the size of the pot and evaluate Minni's reaction. If she checks behind, it might indicate she doesn't have the Ace or a flush; if she bets, I will have to decide whether my pair of Queens is still good enough to call based on the bet sizing.

Check. The turn A♠ changes the dynamic: the board is 3♠-8♥-Q♠-A♠ and I hold J♥8♣ — only middle pair with a mediocre kicker. Flash’s line (call pre, call flop, check turn) is consistent with Qx, small pairs, single-spade holdings or some A‑x; the Ace and the third spade on board significantly increase the number of hands that now beat me (A‑x, Qx, sets, and two‑spade combos that already have a flush). Betting here would be thin value (I’ll get called by many better hands) and unnecessarily bloats the pot where I’m likely behind or face a big raise. With my stack advantage I prefer pot control: check and evaluate the river — call small river bets, fold to large aggression, or take a stab on a safe river card. So I check to keep the pot manageable and preserve fold equity for later streets.

I have two pair, Aces and Queens, with an Eight kicker (A-A-Q-Q-8). On the turn, when the Ace of Spades arrived, Minni checked behind after her flop continuation bet. This check significantly caps her range, making it highly unlikely she has an Ace, a flush, or a better Queen. The river pairing the Ace makes it even less likely she holds one of the remaining Aces. While my hand is very strong in a heads-up context, it is unlikely she will call a bet with a worse hand than a Queen. By checking, I induce potential bluffs from her missed draws or low pairs that she might think can no longer win at showdown. I plan to check-call a reasonable bet to maximize value from her air.

Board: 3♠ 8♥ Q♠ A♠ A♥. My hand: 8♣ J♥ — only a pair of eights. Villain’s line (call pre, call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with Qx, Ax, small pairs, or missed spade draws. The turn/river Aces drastically increase combinations that beat me (any Ace = trips/full house, Qx and many pocket pairs >8 beat my pair, and two‑spade hands already have a flush). Betting here is thin value — I’ll get called by better hands and fold out very few worse hands, while bloating the pot when I’m likely behind. With a big stack advantage I prefer pot control: check and treat a small river bet as a bluff‑catch (call small bets), but fold to larger aggression. Action: check now to keep the pot manageable and avoid committing when I’m frequently behind.

I am holding King-Ten of clubs, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button, I have the positional advantage for the rest of the hand. My standard opening raise has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB and I’m in the BB with pocket 3s (3h3s). This is a small pocket pair out of position but with deep effective stacks (Flash ~12,300) it has good set-mining value. Calling 200 more (total 300) into the 400 pot gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds and excellent implied odds if I hit a set. A minimum re-raise to 500 would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand and isn’t necessary; folding is too tight heads-up. Plan: call to see the flop — if I hit a set or very favorable texture I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (check/fold to heavy pressure or float selectively).

I hold 3h3s on a 7h-8d-6s flop. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep (I ~27,100; Flash ~12,300). I called preflop as a set-mining play, but I missed the flop and only have two outs (~8–9% to hit a set by river). Out of position vs the preflop raiser, I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I will check to control the pot and see Flash's action: call a small C-bet (roughly ≤1/2 pot) to realize implied odds if I hit, fold to large pressure, and if Flash checks behind take a free turn to reevaluate. Checking preserves flexibility and minimizes risk with a low-equity hand.

On a board like 6-7-8, the texture is highly coordinated and favors the big blind's defending range, which contains many more low pairs, two-pair combinations, and straight draws than my pre-flop raising range. Holding King-high with overcards and a gutshot to the nut straight (a 9 gives me the Ten-high straight), I have significant equity but do not want to face a check-raise on such a 'wet' board. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and potentially induce bluffs or see a favorable turn card like a King, Ten, or Nine.

I have pocket 3s (3h3s) on a 7h-8d-6s-Qh board. Pot is 600 and I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser who checked the flop. I only have two outs to hit a set on the river (~2/46 ≈ 4.3%), so my raw equity is very low. With deep effective stacks and a marginal hand, I want pot control and flexibility rather than bloating the pot OOP. I’ll check to see Flash’s action — call a small turn bet to realize my tiny equity, fold to heavy pressure, and if he checks back take a free river to reevaluate (possible bluff or thin value depending on the runout).

Minni has checked for the second time on a board of 6-7-8-Q. While the Queen is an overcard that might favor my pre-flop range, the board remains highly coordinated and dangerous. Holding King-high with a gutshot to the straight, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity for free, control the size of the pot, and potentially win at showdown if the river is a blank and Minni is on a missed draw or weak pair.

I started this hand as a set‑mining call and missed. On the river I still only have a pair of threes on a 7‑8‑6‑Q‑9 board: I lose to any pair (Q,9,7,8,6, or any pocket pair), any two‑card straight combos that connected earlier, and most single‑pair/value hands in the raiser’s preflop range. Both players checked flop and turn, so betting here would be a thin bluff OOP with limited fold equity and a real risk of getting called or raised by hands that beat me. With deep stacks and many hands left in the tournament, I prefer pot control — check and fold to meaningful river aggression rather than bloating the pot with a very weak hand out of position.

On a board of 6-7-8-Q-9, I have successfully made a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). While this is a very strong hand, the board is extremely coordinated. Any Jack in Minni's range gives her a higher Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J), and a Jack-Ten would give her a Queen-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q). Given that Minni has checked the river, I could consider a value bet, but the risk of being check-raised by a Jack is significant on such a 'wet' board. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and realize the equity of my straight without exposing myself to a potential bluff or a superior hand. Checking is the most disciplined play to protect my stack while still likely winning the pot.

Heads-up on the button with 2♥5♥ — a very weak but suited hand. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 more to see the flop (total commit 100). Flash in the BB has shown aggression; open-raising would often invite 3-bets or bigger confrontations I don’t want with this holding. Calling keeps position postflop, lets me see a cheap flop and then fold or bluff depending on texture. I’ll call the 50 (100 total) and re-evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Five of Spades. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has chosen to limp. While Ts 5s has some potential for flushes or straights, it is not a strong enough hand to justify raising for value or as a bluff against a deep-stacked opponent who may be trapping or simply seeing a cheap flop. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and preserve my stack.

On this flop of 8d-Qc-7c, I have 10-high with no significant draws. Checking is the most standard play here as I am out of position and have very little equity against a range that could easily contain a pair of Queens, Eights, Sevens, or various straight draws. I will check and see how Minni reacts.

Heads‑up on a Q‑8‑7 rainbow flop with 2♥5♥ — I have almost no showdown value and no realistic draw (flush impossible, only a remote two‑card straight). Flash checked, so I have initiative and should c‑bet widely in position to take down pots and fold out missed hands. Pot is 200; I’ll make a modest half‑pot bet (100) to apply pressure without committing too many chips. This size folds out many broadways and overcards, keeps the pot manageable if called, and preserves fold equity. Plan: bet 100; fold to a sizable check‑raise (I have negligible equity), and if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and continue only with improvement or a favorable board texture.

On a board of 8d-Qc-7c, I have Ten-high with no direct draws and very little backdoor equity. Minni has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. Although the price to call is small, my hand is extremely weak and is likely dominated by almost any hand Minni would choose to lead with or bluff with. Continuing out of position with Ten-high and a Five kicker is a losing play over the long term. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with J9o. This is a standard opening hand. I have a stack of 12,750 which is about 127 big blinds, giving me plenty of room to play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind, consistent with my previous betting patterns.

Heads-up, Flash opened to 300 from the button. I hold QcTh — a fairly strong, playable hand heads-up but I'm out of position postflop. 3-betting is an option, but a small reraise to the 500 minimum has little fold equity and a larger 3-bet bloats the pot and risks a 4-bet from a fairly aggressive opponent. With deep effective stacks (opponent ~12.5k), I prefer to keep the pot under control and realize equity. I will call the 200 (300 total) to see a flop and then play based on the texture: continue on favorable boards (top pair, strong draws) and be prepared to fold to heavy aggression on completely missed boards.

I have QcTh on Td-5s-Qh — top two pair (Q+T), which is a very strong made hand heads-up. Flash opened wide preflop, so his continuing range contains many Qx, Tx, Kx and medium pairs that I beat. Very few flop hands currently beat me (pocket Q/TT/55 → sets) and there are minimal backdoor draws, so the best play is to bet for value and to deny equity. I will bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) to get called by worse queens/tens/second pairs and charge any turn cards that could pick up equity. If he check‑raises big I’ll assume a set/very strong hand and re‑evaluate (likely call small/medium raises for pot control, fold to an extreme shove depending on sizing); if he simply calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and extract further value when appropriate.

On a flop of Td 5s Qh, I have an open-ended straight draw with J9o. Minni has led out with a 400-chip bet into a 600-chip pot. This 'donk' bet from the big blind often indicates a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a weak Queen) or a draw. Given the pot odds (calling 400 to win a total pot of 1400) and the potential implied odds if I hit my straight on the turn or river, calling is the most efficient play. Raising here might force out hands I can beat later or bloat the pot unnecessarily when I am currently behind, whereas folding would be too tight given the equity of an eight-out draw.

I have ThQc for two pair (Q+T) on Td‑5s‑Qh‑2d. Opponent opened wide preflop and check‑called my 400 flop bet, so his continuing range contains a lot of worse Qx, Tx and single pairs. The 2♦ is a blank — it doesn't add meaningful equity to his range — so I should be value‑betting rather than slow‑playing. I want a size big enough to charge worse queens/tens and medium pairs but small enough to keep the pot controllable against sets; ~2/3 pot is appropriate. Bet 900 into the 1400 pot. If he check‑raises big I’ll treat it as a likely set and re‑evaluate (call small/medium raises, fold to an extreme shove).

On the turn, the board is Td 5s Qh 2d. I hold 9s Jh for an open-ended straight draw (K or 8). Minni has continued her aggression after leading the flop, betting 900 into a 1,400 pot. To call, I need 900 to win a total pot of 3,200, giving me pot odds of approximately 28%. With 8 outs, my chance of hitting the straight on the river is about 17.4%. While this is below the immediate pot odds, the deep stacks and Minni's lead suggest that if I hit my straight, I have significant implied odds to make up the difference. Folding an open-ended straight draw in a heads-up pot is too tight, and raising is risky given her demonstrated strength. I will call to see if the river completes my draw.

I have QcTh — top two pair (Q+T) heads‑up. Flash opened wide preflop and called both my 400 flop and 900 turn bets, so his continuing range contains many worse Qx, Tx and one‑pair hands; only a few combos beat me (sets: QQ/TT/55, QJ that turned into two‑pair, or rare straights like AK/K9). The river Jc is mildly dangerous because it gives QJ two‑pair and some straight combos, but overall I’m still far ahead of his calling range. Checking risks giving up value to worse hands that will call a reasonable bet. I will bet for value — size ~2/3 pot to extract from worse queens/tens and Jx while keeping the pot controllable if I face resistance. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll assume a very strong hand (set/straight) and re‑evaluate (call small/medium raises for pot control, fold to an extreme shove).

On a board of Td 5s Qh 2d Jc, I have finally improved to a pair of Jacks on the river. However, the board is extremely coordinated. Minni has led out on all three streets (donk bet on the flop, then leading turn and river), which is a line that typically represents significant strength or a well-timed bluff. The Jc on the river is a double-edged sword: it pairs me, but it also completes a possible straight for any King (specifically K9, which would have been an open-ended draw like mine). Furthermore, she could easily have a Queen, which has been beating me since the flop. With her betting 2,100 into a 3,200 pot, she is showing no fear of the board. My pair of Jacks is merely a bluff-catcher here, and given her consistent aggression throughout the hand, it is unlikely she is doing this with a hand weaker than mine (like a Ten or a total air bluff). I will fold and preserve my remaining 112 big blinds.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) I should be opening a wide range. T8s is a strong candidate to raise: suited, connected and plays well postflop with position. The pot is 150 and calling only gives me the initiative back to Flash; I prefer to take the lead, deny free cards, and build fold equity. A standard 3xBB sizing to 300 is in-line with the table norms and balances pressure with risk — if Flash 3-bets big I can fold T8s without committing a large portion of my stack; if he calls I can c-bet favorable flops and leverage backdoor spade/straight equity. Raise to 300 to maximize EV and keep control of the hand.

In a heads-up situation, Q8o is a standard defending hand from the big blind against a button raise. The pot odds are favorable, requiring only 33% equity to continue. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary given the marginal nature of the hand and the stack depth. I will call to see a flop.

On this King-high flop of 2d-9c-Ks, my Q8o has completely missed. I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor. Standard play here is to check to the raiser and see how they react. I have no reason to lead out with a bet given my lack of showdown value or a draw.

I was the preflop aggressor (opened to 300) and this K♠-9♣-2♦ flop is a dry, high-card board that misses a large portion of the BB’s flatting range. My T♠8♠ has no pair but some backdoor equity (spade backdoor with K♠ on board) and a useful blocker to spades. Heads-up I should c-bet frequently here to take advantage of position and fold equity. I’ll make a value/pressure c-bet of about 2/3 pot (400 into 600). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue on favorable cards; if he check-raises large I’ll fold since this hand has little showdown value.

On a board of K-9-2 rainbow, my Q8o has completely missed and lacks any significant draw or showdown value. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage on this King-high board and is betting 400 into a 600 pot. Continuing with only Queen-high against a 2/3 pot bet in this spot is not profitable, as I have very little equity and would be forced to fold to most turn bets anyway. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

King-Ten offsuit is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, significantly above the average range. Holding the button gives me a positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will raise to 300, which has been the standard opening size throughout the tournament, to apply pressure on the big blind and build the pot with a hand that has good high-card value.

Flash opened to 300 from the button/SB — a wide heads‑up raise. My Tc3h is a weak, disconnected hand but calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds, and vs a wide opening range my fold equity and showdown equity are sufficient to justify a defend. I don’t want to 3‑bet as a pure bluff with such a poor hand (too high variance and I’ll be playing a bloated pot out of position). Plan: flat call (total 300 committed). On the flop I’ll proceed only if I connect or pick up a usable draw; otherwise I’ll fold to heavy aggression.

I have Tc3h on Jd 6s 4h — no pair, no real draw and I'm out of position to the preflop raiser. Flash opened wide from the button, so he will continuation-bet often, but my hand has very little showdown or improvement equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see what he does. If he checks back I can see a free turn; if he c-bets I’ll fold to any meaningful aggression (I might entertain a call only to a very small probe — ~≤1/3 pot — otherwise I give up). Betting or bluffing out of position with such a weak holding is unnecessarily high variance.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit on a very dry flop of Jd-6s-4h. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage here, as this board is unlikely to have improved the Big Blind's calling range significantly unless they hold a Jack or a small pair. Minni has checked to me, which is standard. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 chips (half-pot) to apply pressure and fold out her weaker hands (like Q-high or middle-card air). If called, I still have two overcards that could improve my hand on the turn.

Flash opened wide preflop and now makes a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot). My Tc‑3h has virtually no showdown or drawing equity (only remote backdoors and a pair of tens/treys), so ordinarily I wouldn’t chase. However the sizing gives me ~25% pot‑odds (300 to win 1200), and versus a wide button/sb c‑bet my realized equity is close enough to justify a cheap look. I’ll call the small probe to see a turn and preserve the option to fold to any meaningful turn aggression. I will not raise or bluff here — out of position with a garbage hand is too high variance. If I miss the turn and face pressure, I’ll fold; if I pair or pick up a clear draw I can proceed.

I have T♣3♥ on J♦6♠4♥2♠ — no pair and no meaningful draws. Pot is 1,200 and there is currently nothing for me to call. Flash is a wide, aggressive button who c-bet small, so his range includes a lot of air and marginal hands, but I have almost zero equity and I'm out of position. Betting or bluff-raising here is high-variance and unnecessary with a stack this deep; the correct line is to control the pot and see a cheap river. I’ll check and plan to fold to significant river aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear draw/improvement.

On the turn 2s, which is a brick, Minni has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. Her call on such a dry flop (J-6-4) suggests she likely has a piece of the board, most likely a Jack or a medium pocket pair. While I have two overcards, my King-high still has some showdown value against her potential floats or air. If I bet again, I am unlikely to fold out a Jack, and I risk getting check-raised. By checking back, I control the pot size and give myself a free look at the river where a King or Ten could give me the best hand, or I might simply win at showdown if the action remains passive.

I have T♣3♥ on J♦6♠4♥2♠8♠ — I completely missed. The board now has three spades, I hold no spade and no pair, and I have no meaningful blockers. Flash is a wide, aggressive button raiser who c‑bet small on the flop then checked the turn — a line consistent with marginal one‑pair hands, some floats, or occasional air. With no bet to face on the river, thin bluffing out of position is unnecessary and high variance: most hands that will continue (Jx, 8x, any two spades, better pairs) beat me, and a small probe is likely to be called. Best line is to preserve chips and check; I’ll fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling a tiny, highly suspicious probe). Therefore: check.

On the river, the board is J-6-4-2-8 with no completed flushes or obvious straights (except the unlikely 5-7). Minni called my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests she has a piece of the board, such as a Jack, a 6, or a 4. Her subsequent checks on the turn and river indicate she is likely looking to go to a cheap showdown with a marginal hand. While my King-high has very little showdown value, it is unlikely that a bet will fold out a Jack or even a middle pair like a 6 on such a dry board. Since Minni has a significantly larger stack and has shown some 'stickiness' in previous hands, I will check back to see the showdown. This preserves my stack and avoids a potential check-raise bluff or losing additional chips to a better hand that was never folding.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 9♦4♦ — a suited hand with decent post‑flop playability in heads‑up. In heads‑up play the button should be opening a wide range: position and fold equity make raising preferable to a passive call. I’ll open to a standard size (300 total) to take the initiative, build a pot when I have position, and deny the BB a cheap flop. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to play flops for equity and leverage. Raising to 300 is a balanced, aggressive play that maximizes long‑term EV here.

I am holding T8o in the Big Blind and facing a standard 3x raise from Minni on the Button. In heads-up play, T8o is well within the range of hands that should be played defensively from the Big Blind, especially when receiving 3:1 pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 400). I will call to see the flop and evaluate the board texture before committing more chips against the chip leader.

I have flopped top two pair (Tens and Eights) on a board of 10-8-6. This is an exceptionally strong hand in a heads-up situation. My standard play here from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue her lead with her wide range, including overcards or draws. If she bets, I will most likely check-raise to extract value and protect my hand from various straight draws like 9-7, J-9, or 7-5 that are possible on this board.

I opened wide from the button preflop, and on this dry, rainbow 8♠‑T♥‑6♦ flop Flash checked. I hold 9♦4♦ — no pair, only a backdoor diamond and a gutshot to a 7 — so my hand has low showdown value but some turn/river equity. heads‑up the preflop raiser should c‑bet frequently on a board this unconnected to fold out many of the BB’s missed hands and seize the initiative. A ~50% pot c‑bet (300 into 600) gives good fold equity without overcommitting; if he calls I retain position and can continue on a diamond/7 or control pot size on bricks, while a check‑raise would be met with a fold most of the time given my weak equity. Betting here is standard and exploitative, so I’ll bet 300.

I have flopped top two pair on a semi-coordinated board (T-8-6). This is an extremely strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. I should raise here for value and protection. A raise extracts value from her top-pair holdings (like AT, KT, QT, JT) and forces her to pay if she is drawing to a straight with hands like 97 or J9. Raising also helps me build a pot while I likely have the best hand. I will raise to 1000 total.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet this dry T♥‑8♠‑6♦ board to take the initiative. Flash’s large check‑raise to 1,000 (about 3.3× my c‑bet) polarizes his line strongly toward value (top pair, two pair, sets, strong Tx) with some bluffs possible but not enough to pull me in. I would need to call 700 more (1000 total committed on the flop) to win roughly 3,600 — break‑even equity ≈ 19.4%. My hand (9♦4♦) has only a one‑card gutshot to any 7 (~16% to hit by river) and a tiny backdoor diamond (~4% to make a flush by river); combined equity is at best ~20% and in practice lower versus a check‑raise heavy value range (also many of my outs are sometimes blocked). I also have a large effective stack advantage (27,900 vs 10,200) and no need to gamble with a weak draw in a spot I previously flagged to fold to big aggression. Calling is marginal and re‑raising as a bluff is unnecessarily high variance here. Fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.
Ace-seven offsuit is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. Raising to 300 is a standard play to build the pot and take the initiative from the button.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB; I’m in the BB with 4♦2♥ — a very weak, offsuit, unconnected hand with almost no playability out of position. Calling 200 into the 400 pot requires about 33% equity to break even, and 4‑2o will be well below that versus a typical heads‑up opening range and will be dominated by a large portion of his range. 3‑betting to 500 as a light bluff is possible in heads‑up, but with deep effective stacks this hand is a poor candidate for a multi‑street bluff. No reason to gamble — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q♣7♥ — a playable hand in position. Pot is 150 with 50 to call; I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the big blind while keeping the pot manageable. I raise to 200 total (2x BB) to pick up the blind or play a pot in position; if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops (queen-high, heart/straight draws) and use position to fold to heavy action. If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll fold this marginal hand; small defenses I can continue against. This is a standard heads-up open to maximize fold equity and postflop playability.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the big blind with 92o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips, 92o has very poor playability and equity realization, especially when out of position. Given the stack depths and the high-roller tournament context, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a better hand than to commit chips with a bottom-tier starting hand against an aggressive opponent.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a very strong hand in heads-up play, particularly from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent strength, as I have been doing with my opening ranges in previous hands.

3-bet to 900. Q♢J♣ is a strong heads‑up hand vs a button open — it has good equity and plays well post‑flop. Facing a 300 open from the button, calling 200 keeps us out of position and concedes initiative; folding is too passive. I want to take the lead, create fold equity against wide opening hands (small pairs, suited connectors, weaker broadways) and get value from worse Qx/Kx/medium broadways. I size to ~3x the open (900 total) — large enough to apply pressure and isolate, but small enough to keep some dominated hands and speculative callers in with deep stacks. If Flash 4‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate (QJo can fold to heavy 4‑bet pressure), but overall 3‑betting here maximizes expected value.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong holding in a heads-up match. Minni's 3-bet to 900 puts pressure on my opening raise, but folding would be too tight given the wide ranges involved in heads-up play. While a 4-bet is an option, calling allows me to use my positional advantage on the flop without bloating the pot excessively against a range that likely contains better Aces or medium pairs. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

I was the preflop aggressor and this flop (9c 5s 2c) is very dry. QdJc has two overcards and a backdoor club — reasonable equity but no pair. Flash’s cold‑call to my 3‑bet represents a wide range (A‑x, K‑x, small/medium pairs, suited connectors and club combos) many of which miss this board. I want to use my initiative: a compact c‑bet applies pressure, folds out unpaired broadways and small pairs, and charges club draws while keeping worse hands and thin value hands in. I size to ~1/3 pot (600 into 1800) — big enough to generate fold equity but small enough to preserve flexibility OOP. If he raises big I’ll fold (QJo is usually behind vs value); if called I’ll evaluate the turn and continue with equity or top pair, otherwise control the pot.

I have Ace-high on a relatively dry board of 9-5-2. Minni's bet of 600 into a 1,800 pot (offering me 4:1 pot odds) is a standard continuation bet after her pre-flop 3-bet. In a heads-up match, Ace-Eight retains significant equity against a wide range that includes missed broadway cards and various bluffs. Folding here would be too weak given the price and my positional advantage. I will call to see the turn and evaluate the action then.

Board: 9c 5s 2c 6c. I hold Qd Jc — no pair but two overcards and a strong turn draw (I have four clubs including Jc, 9 outs to a club on the river plus extra outs to pair). I was the preflop aggressor and c-bettor on a very dry flop; Flash’s call range is wide (AX/KX, small/medium pairs, suited connectors and some clubs). On this turn a bet is a strong semi-bluff: it folds out lots of non-club pairs and broadways, builds the pot when I hit, and still has respectable equity (~20% to hit a club on the river, more including pair-outs) if called.

Sizing: I want enough fold‑equity to take it down now but not so large that a single raise commits me with only a draw. A ~50% pot bet (~1500 into 3000) strikes that balance — it prices calls from worse hands and keeps my options open. If Flash check‑raises/shoves large (showing heavy strength or a made club) I will respect that and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river for value or bluff. Therefore I continue with a half‑pot semi‑bluff.

On the turn, the 6 of clubs completes a board of 9-5-2-6 with three clubs. Holding the Ace of clubs, I now possess the nut flush draw. Minni's bet of 1500 into the 3000 pot provides me with 3-to-1 pot odds, meaning I need 25% equity to make a profitable call. With 9 outs to the nut flush and 3 potential outs for top pair with an Ace, I have approximately 26% equity, satisfying the requirements for a call on direct odds. Furthermore, being in position provides significant implied odds; if I hit my flush on the river, I can potentially extract maximum value from her likely range of overpairs, sets, or two-pair hands. Folding is too tight given the draw, and raising would likely only isolate me against the very top of her range, so calling is the most strategic play.

Recap: I 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the dry flop and barreled the turn; villain cold‑called both streets. River (9c 5s 2c 6c 2d) pairs the deuce and I don’t improve — I’m Q♦J♣ with a single club. Range read: Flash’s line (call 3‑bet, call flop, call turn) is polarized toward hands that beat me — made clubs (two clubs → flush on the turn), sets (99,55,22), and sturdy single pairs (9x/6x) — plus some thin floats. The river pairing is especially bad for bluffs because any deuce in his hand now makes trips/full house and many strong hands will simply continue. Math: a small bluff needs opponents to fold more than bet/(pot+bet) (e.g. 1,500 into 6,000 needs >20% folds). Given he called my turn bet, I estimate his fold frequency is lower than that for meaningful sizes, so a bluff is unlikely to be +EV. Betting also exposes me to a raise or a call by better hands; checking preserves my stack and allows me to either catch him bluffing or fold to aggression. Plan: check back and go to showdown (or fold to a river stab if he leads big and I judge he has me beat).

On the river, the board pairs with the Deuce of Diamonds (9c-5s-2c-6c-2d). Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop and bet both the flop and turn, has now checked. I have Ace-high with the Ace of clubs. While my flush draw did not materialize, Ace-high retains some showdown value in heads-up play against missed straight draws (like 78) or air bluffs. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (pairs or better), and since I hold the Ace of clubs, I block the most likely nut flush she could be worried about. Furthermore, in a large pot, Minni might be checking to call with a marginal hand or trapping with a flush. Checking back is the safest and most efficient way to see a showdown and potentially win the pot without risking more chips on a bluff that may not be successful against her 3-betting range.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 2♦7♦ is weak but suited and fine as a steal candidate with position. Limping gives the BB a free look and surrenders initiative; a min-raise (to 200 total) applies pressure, often wins the pot preflop, and keeps the pot manageable if called. With deep stacks I’ll fold to a large 3-bet but will proceed postflop with positional advantage if Flash just calls. Raise to the min to pick up the blinds or play a small pot in position.

Defending the big blind with T8o against a min-raise (2x) from the small blind. The pot odds are excellent (3:1), and T8o has sufficient equity against a typical wide button-opening range in heads-up play.

I am in the Big Blind and checked to the pre-flop aggressor on a board of 9c-6c-4h. I hold Td-8c, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and two overcards. Standard play here is to check to the raiser and see how they act before deciding whether to continue with my drawing hand.

I opened very wide on the button with 7♦2♦ as a steal and got the BB to just call. The flop (9♣-6♣-4♥) misses my hand but is low and fairly dry — it also misses a lot of the caller’s overcards. As the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage, so a modest continuation bet can pick up the pot now or fold out many hands (Kx/Qx/Ax, many single overcards) while keeping the pot manageable if called. Flash has shown a tendency to passive check‑call lines, so I keep the size small to maximize fold equity but limit my downside if he shops for a raise or has a club draw/small pair. Bet half the pot (200) — if he raises big I’ll fold this junk; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a 7) on a 9-6-4 board. Minni's continuation bet of 200 into 400 provides me with 3:1 pot odds. Given the price and the potential to improve, calling is the most profitable move to see a turn card.

On the turn, an Ace of Spades arrives, which is a significant card that typically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I currently hold only a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and ten-high. There is no reason to lead out into the aggressor here, especially since the Ace is a scare card. I will check to see how Minni reacts to the Ace and hopefully get a free or cheap look at the river to realize my equity.

I’m heads‑up with a healthy chip lead (24.5k vs 14.7k). After my flop c‑bet (200) Flash called; the A♠ on the turn now hits a large portion of his calling range (Ax, A‑X, some K/Q hands that called) and kills most of my fold equity. I have 7♦2♦ — no pair and no club draw, only two clean outs to pair on the river — so I don’t want to build the pot with junk. With position and the stack advantage I’ll control the pot: check the turn, fold to large aggression, and re‑evaluate on the river (look for a small steal on a safe card or check down if he shows strength).

I have Ten-high and completely missed my gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). The river 5d does not improve my hand. Minni checked back on the turn when the Ace appeared, which suggests she likely doesn't have a strong hand like an Ace or a set, but she could still have a pair of nines, sixes, fours, or a small pocket pair that beats my Ten-high. Since the pot is relatively small (800) and I have no showdown value, I will check. If she bets, I will fold, as bluffing in this spot is unlikely to succeed against her wide range of marginal made hands.

I check. River: 9♣-6♣-4♥-A♠-5♦, I hold 7♦2♦ — no pair and no way to improve. I c-bet the flop and was called, he checked the turn (passive check‑call tendency). A river bluff would need meaningful fold equity to be +EV (e.g. a 200 bet needs ≈20% fold rate to break even) and against this opponent who calls down a lot that fold equity is unlikely. With a big chip advantage and only an 800‑chip pot to win, preserving stack and checking down is the higher EV play; I’ll fold to sizable river aggression but avoid risking chips with a busted hand.

Q8o is a strong starting hand for heads-up play from the button. Raising to 300 is a standard open to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. If called, I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

Call to 300. A♦5♥ is a playable heads-up hand versus a wide SB open: it has an ace blocker and reasonable equity against a typical opening range. The pot is 400 and I must put in 200 more to call (offering roughly 2:1 pot odds; I need ≈33% equity), which A5o usually achieves vs a wide opener. I remain out of position postflop either way (button acts last), so a small 3-bet or raise only bloats the pot with a marginal offsuit ace — unnecessary against a likely wide range. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot under control, see a flop, and proceed aggressively only when I hit top pair or strong draws; fold to heavy postflop aggression if I miss. Therefore I will call and evaluate the flop.

I called preflop to keep the pot under control and see a flop; I hit top pair (A♦5♥ on Q♠ A♣ 9♣). Versus the SB/button’s wide opening range I’m ahead of a lot of hands (weaker Aces, Qx, medium pairs) but vulnerable to A K / A Q and rare sets. The board has two clubs, but a real club flush draw requires the opponent to hold both clubs — not very common. Betting gains value from worse hands and protects against potential draws. I’ll bet for value/protection around 2/3 pot to charge draws and get called by worse Ax and Qx combos while avoiding bloating the pot out of position. If called I’ll reevaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise I’ll proceed cautiously and likely fold to very heavy aggression. Action: bet 400 into a 600 pot (≈2/3 pot).

In this heads-up situation, I raised pre-flop with Queen-Eight offsuit and Minni called from the big blind. The flop comes Ace-Queen-Nine with two clubs, giving me second pair. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into a 600 pot. While the Ace is a scare card, my middle pair is too strong to fold immediately to a single bet in a heads-up game. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (like an Ace) or strong draws. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot, keep her bluffs or weaker pairs in, and see how the turn card changes the board texture before committing more chips.

Board Q♠ A♣ 9♣ J♦, I hold A♦5♥ — top pair with a weak kicker. Flash is the button and opened wide preflop and then called my 2/3 pot flop c-bet; his continuing range is wide (weaker Aces, Qx, 9x, some Kx, and occasional club combos). The turn J improves some of his holdings (Jx) and makes K–T the main made-straight combo, but those straight combos are relatively rare. I want to extract value from worse Aces and Qx, deny cheap rivers to floats/draws, and avoid giving free cards that could make two-pair/straights. Pot is 1400 — a ~2/3-pot turn bet continues the same line and pressures marginal hands while still being small relative to our stacks. If he raises very large I will fold (my kicker is weak and a big raise usually represents two-pair/straight); I may call a small raise depending on sizing, and if called I’ll reevaluate on the river. Action: bet 1000 into the 1400 pot to get value and protect.

On this board of Ace-Queen-Nine-Jack, my pair of Queens with an eight kicker has significantly dropped in relative value. Minni led out on the flop (a donk bet) and is now firing a second, larger bet on the turn. The Jack of Diamonds is a very coordinated card; it completes straights for hands like King-Ten or Ten-Eight and improves many of Minni's likely calling ranges like Queen-Jack or Ace-Jack to two pair. Given the board texture and Minni's shown strength across two streets, my middle pair is likely beaten, and calling here would put me in a very difficult position on most river cards. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with a small pocket pair (2♠2♣) and very deep stacks (~251 BB me, ~147 BB opponent). Calling 50 is overly passive — I want initiative, fold equity and to isolate the BB so I can set-mine or take down the pot. A standard 3x BB size is appropriate here: raise to 300 total. Plan: if Flash 3-bets a small/moderate size I can call for set-mining given deep stacks; if he 4-bets big I’ll likely fold to avoid playing large stacks in a dominated spot. Postflop I’ll proceed aggressively if I hit a set or the board is favorable, otherwise control the pot and use position to realize equity.
