A6o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind in a heads-up match. The pot odds are favorable (2:1), and Minni has been raising a wide range of hands from the button. Calling allows me to see the flop without inflating the pot too much while out of position.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker is weak (6). In a heads-up situation against an aggressive button raiser like Minni, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. This allows her to continuation bet with a wide range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs (like Kings or pocket pairs). Leading out here might drive away her bluffs while only getting called by better Aces or Kings. I plan to check-call most standard bet sizes on this street to keep the pot manageable while holding a hand that is likely ahead but vulnerable to better kickers.

Heads‑up on the button with 2♠2♣. I opened to take the initiative and Flash just checked back on K♠ A♥ 3♣. This flop has two big overcards so my pair is currently vulnerable, but the board is relatively dry (no flush/straight draws) and the BB’s calling range from preflop contains a lot of missed hands (broadways, singletons, some small pairs). I want to use my initiative to pick up the pot or charge those missed hands while keeping the pot controllable if I’m called. A small c‑bet (~1/3 pot = 200) gives fold equity versus unpaired hands, doesn’t commit me with an underpair if I get resistance, and keeps options open for turn play. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check to control size and look to realize equity or fold to strong aggression unless I improve). If he check‑raises big, I’ll relinquish the pot (fold) because I’m likely behind.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a board of A-K-3. Minni's small continuation bet of 200 into a 600-chip pot is very standard for her range, which includes weaker Aces, Kings, pocket pairs, and total bluffs. While my kicker is weak (6), folding is not an option with top pair in a heads-up match. Calling is preferable to raising here because it keeps the pot size manageable and allows Minni to continue bluffing on later streets with hands I currently beat. Raising would likely force out her bluffs and only get called by better Aces or monster hands.

The turn card is a Queen, making the board Ace-King-Three-Queen. This card is quite coordinated as it completes a potential J-10 straight and improves hands like Ace-Queen or King-Queen. Despite this, I still have top pair with my Ace. In a heads-up situation, leading out here doesn't accomplish much as I'll likely only be called by better hands or straights. Checking is the superior play to control the pot size and allow Minni to either check back with her marginal hands or continue betting with her bluffs and value range. I intend to see how she reacts to this board texture.

I have pocket deuces on K♠ A♥ 3♣ Q♥. I led the flop with a small c‑bet (200) and was called, which indicates a wide calling range that contains lots of missed broadways and some Kx/Ax combos that dominate me. The turn Q♥ is another overcard and adds another heart to the board, so my hand is still a clear underpair with little fold equity vs strong holdings. With very deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with just a pair of twos — checking controls the pot, allows me to realize equity on the river, and keeps the option to call small bets or fold to strong aggression. If Flash bets the river I’ll fold to large bets and call or raise only vs small, unblockable sizing or known bluffs; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river card and decide whether a small value/bluff attempt makes sense.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Sixes) on the river. Minni's decision to check back on the turn, which was highly coordinated with a Queen and a potential straight draw (J-10), strongly suggests she does not hold a monster like a straight, A-K, or A-Q, as those hands would typically bet for value and protection. My hand now beats all her one-pair hands, including top-pair Aces with better kickers or any Kings. Leading out with a half-pot value bet is designed to get called by those marginal hands that she might otherwise check back for a free showdown.

Heads‑up river decision with 2♠2♣ on K♠ A♥ 3♣ Q♥ 6♦. I opened preflop and the BB called; I c‑bet 200 on the dry flop and was called, both checked the turn, and now Flash makes a small river bet of 500 into a 1500 pot. Pot odds to call are very good (calling 500 to win 2500 → ~20% equity required). Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small probe on the river) is wide and will contain a mix of thin value (A/K/Qx, occasional JT straights) and many missed broadways/bluffs. I have an underpair with no backdoor improvement and don’t block many bluffs, so a raise would be a marginal, thin bluff that’s unlikely to fold enough better hands and would overcommit me when I’m usually behind. Folding is too tight given the cheap price. Therefore the correct, low-variance line is to call the 500 and take my showdown equity against his mixed river betting range.

I am holding 98o in the Small Blind during a heads-up match. This is a standard opening hand in this position. Given the stack sizes and the flow of the game, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot early and pressure Minni in the Big Blind.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 (3x). K7o is a defendable hand in heads-up — it has decent equity against a wide opening range and plays well postflop when I connect. Calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives ~33% pot odds into the current pot and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. 3-betting is an option, but K7o is marginal as a light 3-bet and I prefer to see a flop and make decisions OOP rather than inflate the pot out of position. Plan: call, proceed cautiously on dry boards (fold to strong pressure), and extract value/turn aggression if I hit a king or strong draw.

Flop: Ac Tc Js. I have Ks7d — no pair, only K-high. This board heavily favors the preflop raiser (lots of Ax, T, J and broadway combos in his opening range). I have ~7 relatively clean outs (3 Ks + 4 Qs) → roughly ~28% to make a pair or Broadway by river, but many of those outcomes will still be second-best vs an Ace or two‑pair. No realistic flush draw. With position against me and deep stacks I should control the pot rather than bluffing here. Plan: check and keep the pot small. If Flash fires a small C‑bet (roughly ≤ ~230 chips) I’ll call to realize equity; fold to larger, committed-sized bets/raises. If he checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate and will only start raising as bluff when I pick up real equity (paired K or Q/straight).

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw on a very high-card-heavy board (Ace, Ten, Jack). This texture significantly favors the defender's calling range, which is likely to contain many Broadway cards, pairs, and straight draws. While a continuation bet could represent the Ace, it is unlikely to generate many folds from Minni given how well this board connects with her likely holdings. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see the turn for free, allowing me to realize the equity of my eight outs (any Seven or Queen) without risking a check-raise.

Check.

I have K♠7♦ on A♣ T♣ J♠ 4♠ — just K-high with no flush and only backdoor/Broadway outs: 3 Kings and 4 Queens = 7 outs to improve on the river (~7/46 ≈ 15%). Flash opened wide preflop but checked both the flop and turn, which narrows his range toward pot-controlled hands (small pairs, some Ax, missed broadways) or a slow-played strong hand. Bluff-betting the turn has thin fold equity here — many hands in his range will call a small bet and I have poor equity if called. With deep stacks and a single card to come, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to keep the pot small and check. If he fires on the river I can fold most large bets, call small probing bets occasionally depending on sizing, and only bluff-raise or bet for value if I hit a King or a Queen/straight. Therefore I check now to control the pot and re-evaluate on the river.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any Seven or Queen) on a very coordinated Broadway board. After checking back the flop, Minni has checked to me again on the turn. While a bet could potentially take the pot down now, the board texture (A-J-T) is very likely to have connected with Minni's big-blind defending range. By checking back, I ensure I see the river for free to realize my equity. If I hit my straight, I can value bet; if I miss, I can evaluate the river based on her action and the final card to determine if a bluff is appropriate.

I have K♠7♦ on A♣ T♣ J♠ 4♠ T♦ — no pair, just K‑high. Flash opened wide preflop but checked both the flop and the turn, which narrows his range toward showdown‑type hands (Ax, small pairs, some broadways) or the occasional slow‑played strong hand. The river pairing the Ten makes many hands in his range (any T, Ax, Jx, pairs) beat me, and bluffing has very thin fold equity — a small probe will be called too often and a large stab is unnecessarily risky with deep stacks. Lowest‑variance, +EV line here is to check and preserve chips. If he fires a small, obvious bluff I can consider a call, but against normal sizing I’ll fold K‑high. Therefore I check to take the safe line and avoid getting exploited.

The board has run out A-T-J-4-T. I started the hand with 9-8 offsuit and an open-ended straight draw on the flop, but I have completely missed. My 9-high has zero showdown value. However, the board texture is extremely heavy with Broadway cards and a pair of Tens. Minni has checked to me for the third time. While this could signal weakness, Minni has shown a high degree of 'stickiness' in previous hands (e.g., Hand #19 and Hand #12), calling large bets with moderate holdings. On a board where she is likely to have at least a pair or an Ace in her defending range, it is unlikely she will fold to a single river bet. Attempting a bluff here to win a relatively small pot of 600 chips risks losing more of my stack against a caller. I will check back and concede the pot, preserving my 15,500 stack for more favorable situations.

Heads-up on the button with Js4s — a marginal but playable hand heads-up with suitedness and a decent chance to realize equity post‑flop. Stacks are deep (≈245bb for me, 154bb for Flash) so I don’t want to nit-fold every marginal hand, and I want to seize the initiative and use positional advantage post‑flop. Opening to 200 (2x BB) is the standard, gives fold equity, keeps the pot controllable, and lets me c‑bet favorable flops (or check dry A‑high boards). Plan: raise to 200 now; fold to a large 3‑bet (don’t commit with J4s), and if called play aggressively on J/spade/drawy flops and pot control on uncooperative boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 8c. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). I need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot odds are 3:1 (I need 25% equity), and 98o is more than strong enough to defend against a standard opening range from the Button. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, calling is more standard to keep the pot manageable out of position against the chip leader.

On a flop of Jc-8s-3h, I have middle pair with a 9 kicker. This is a solid marginal hand that is best played by checking to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or fold out hands I currently beat. By checking, I can gauge Minni's strength and likely check-call to see a turn card, keeping the pot under control while out of position.

Flop: 3h 8s Jc. I have Js4s — top pair (jack) with a weak kicker and a backdoor spade. Heads‑up, preflop caller’s range is wide (Ax, Kx, Qx, small pairs, suited connectors and some Jx). Flash checked the flop, so as the preflop aggressor I should c‑bet for value and protection: get value from worse Jx/8x/3x and charge any straight/flush draws. I don’t want to give free cards with deep stacks, but I also don’t need to overcommit. I’ll bet a constructive size (~60–65% pot) to fold out pure overcards and price draws correctly, then re‑evaluate on the turn. If he raises I’ll respect a strong line given my weak kicker and react based on sizing.

On a flop of Jc-8s-3h, I have middle pair with a 9 kicker. Minni's bet of 250 into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet that she could make with a wide range of hands, including total air, straight draws (like T9 or QT), or small pairs. Folding middle pair to a single bet on a relatively dry board would be too exploitable. Raising is unnecessary as it would likely only be called by better hands (Jacks or overpairs) and would fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Calling is the best option to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot manageable.

On the turn 7s, the board texture remains relatively safe for my middle pair, although it does introduce some straight and flush possibilities. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor and current chip leader, checking is the most sound strategy. This allows me to see what Minni does and keeps the pot size manageable with a marginal hand that has some showdown value.

I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak kicker (4♠) and a two‑spade board after the 7♠ turn. Flash checked the turn after calling my ~62% pot c‑bet on the flop — his range is wide (weaker Jx, 8x, 3x, spade draws, some small pairs). I want to deny free cards to spade draws and extract value from worse pairs and many Jx hands, but I don’t want to overcommit with a poor kicker if he shows heavy resistance. Betting ~60% pot here keeps the initiative, charges draws, and folds out pure overcards. Size: pot is 900 so I’ll bet 550 (≈61%). Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises large I’ll assume strong (set/flush/two‑pair) and concede given my weak kicker.

On a board of Jc-8s-3h-7s, I have middle pair (Eights) with a Nine kicker. Minni, who opened from the button, has followed up her flop continuation bet with a second barrel of 550 into a 900 pot. While the 7s connects with some straight draws (like 56 or T9), it is generally a safe card. My middle pair is a solid hand in a heads-up, button-vs-blind scenario. Folding here would be too tight given the pot odds (roughly 3.6:1) and Minni's likely wide range. Calling allows me to keep her bluffs and weaker semi-bluffs in the pot while reaching a river where I can decide whether to go to showdown. I am not raising as I likely only get called by better hands (Jacks or overpairs) and fold out the hands I am currently beating.

On the river 9h, I have improved to two pair (Nines and Eights). However, the board is now quite coordinated (7-8-9-J), and any Ten (10) in Minni's range has completed a straight. Given that Minni raised from the button and fired two barrels, a Ten is a very plausible part of her range (hands like T9, JT, QT, KT, AT). While my two pair is strong in a heads-up context, checking is the most prudent action. It allows me to see if Minni will check back with a one-pair hand (like a Jack) that might have folded to a bet, or if she will bet for value with a straight or better. If she does bet, I will have to decide whether to call based on the size of the bet, but leading out myself risks running into a superior hand.

I have top pair (J♠4♠) but a very weak kicker on a dynamic board: 3♥ 8♠ J♣ 7♠ 9♥. Pot is 2,000 and stacks are deep (I: 23,500; Flash: 14,500). Flash called my flop (250) and turn (550) bets, which maps to a wide range: worse Jx, 8x, 3x, small pairs, but also hands that make a straight (any Ten) that would already be made on the turn and hands that now pair the river (9x). His river check is ambiguous (weakness or trap). Betting here risks getting called or check‑raised by many hands that beat me (10x straights, two‑pair/sets) while only extracting thin value from worse Jx/9x/8x. With a poor kicker and the river completing dangerous parts of his range, the lower‑variance, long‑term EV line is to check and see showdown rather than commit more chips.

In heads-up play, holding Queen-six offsuit on the button is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to exert pressure and take the initiative in the hand, as I have been doing with most of my playable button hands.

KcQc in the big blind vs a 300 open from the button is a very strong heads-up hand. Calling 200 keeps the pot small and gives the button cheap flops, but I want initiative and fold equity against a likely wide SB opening range. I'll 3-bet to apply pressure, extract value from worse broadways/small pairs, and either take the pot now or play a defined pot size postflop with a playable hand and backdoor club equity. Min raise to 500 is available, but I size up to 1200 total to make calling more costly while still leaving room vs a 4-bet given effective stacks (~16k).

In Hand #100, I opened with Queen-six offsuit (Q6o) from the button, which is a standard play in heads-up to maintain pressure. However, Minni responded with a substantial 4x 3-bet to 1,200. While I am in position, Q6o is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by Minni's 3-betting range. Calling 900 more with such a weak hand often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I have poor equity and playability. Given that we are at hand 100 of 150 and I have a healthy stack of 162 BBs, there is no reason to defend this specific hand against a large raise. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the small blind). K♦6♥ is a marginal holding, but in heads-up play the button should open a wide range — raising takes the initiative and leverages my positional advantage postflop. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to complete, but a raise to the minimum (200 total) achieves fold equity, narrows the field, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (23,750 vs 16,100). If Flash 3-bets I can fold this marginal hand; if he calls I play last postflop and can realize equity or apply further pressure. Raise to 200 (2x BB) is the standard, +EV line here.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Seven offsuit is a strong starting hand. Minni has opened with a minimum raise to 200 from the small blind/button. Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand, calling is the most standard play to see a flop while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A 3-bet is also an option but calling allows me to play a wider range of flops where my Ace-high often holds significant value.

On this flop of Qc-4c-7s, I have flopped middle pair with an Ace kicker (Sevens). In heads-up play, this is a relatively strong hand. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board. If she bets, I intend to call to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the turn, as my hand is too strong to fold but doesn't necessarily want to build a massive pot right away against a potential Queen or a flush draw.

Heads-up I opened preflop and have position. Flop Qc-4c-7s misses my K♦6♥ but the board is relatively dry and the BB’s cold-call range is wide — lots of missed broadways, small pairs and club draws. A standard continuation bet gets value from and folds out many of those hands. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (200) to apply pressure, represent Qx/pairs, and deny equity to club draws. Plan: fold to a large check-raise (I have little equity), call small-to-medium resistance and re-evaluate on the turn (continue only with improvement or favorable runouts).

I have middle pair (Sevens) with an Ace kicker on a Qc-4c-7s board. In a heads-up match, this is a strong hand to continue with against a standard continuation bet. Minni's 200 bet into a 400 pot gives me excellent 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600). Since my hand has value but is not strong enough to raise and potentially face a re-raise, calling is the most balanced line to keep the pot manageable while playing from out of position.

The 3c on the turn completes the flush draw. I hold middle pair (7s) with the Ace of Diamonds as a kicker. Since I am out of position and the board has become more coordinated, checking is the most standard and cautious play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the flush-completing card. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to any Queen or a completed flush. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and can evaluate whether to call a potential bet based on its sizing and my read on Minni's aggression.

I c-bet the flop and was called; the turn (Qc 4c 7s 3c) brings a third club completing obvious club flushes. I hold K♦6♥ — no pair, no club — so I have very little equity versus the caller’s range (which contains many clubs, Qx and small pairs). Bluffing here is low EV because many hands that call the flop now beat me, and a raise or a sizable bet from him is likely to be for value. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid bloating it with a bluff. Plan: check and fold to a medium/large bet; only consider calling a very small probe bet (thin block) to see a cheap river.

On the river, the second Queen arrives, pairing the board. My hand is now two pair, Queens and Sevens, with an Ace kicker. Minni's check on the turn after the flush draw completed suggests she likely does not have a flush. However, the pairing of the Queen is a card she could potentially have in her range. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to either check back with weaker hands or potentially bluff with missed straight or flush draws. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, making a check the most strategic way to reach showdown.

I have K♦6♥ on Qc‑4c‑7s‑3c‑Qd and missed everything; I have no club and no pair. Line: I raised pre, c‑bet the flop and was called, both of us checked the turn (the club completing). Villain’s flop‑call range contains Qx, club draws, and small pairs — many of which now beat me (trip Qx, completed clubs, or pocket pairs). The turn check from villain indicates a lot of medium/strong hands are pot‑controlling rather than barreling, so a river bluff here has low EV: most hands that call the flop will call a reasonable river probe or already beat me. With position and a marginal hand, I control the pot by checking. I will fold to any meaningful bet on the river and only contemplate calling an extremely small probe; therefore I check.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. I want to build the pot for value and take initiative. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the big blind and capitalize on my hand strength.

5♦2♥ is the absolute bottom of my range. Flash opened to 300 from the button. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ≈33% equity — 5-2o will not have that versus a reasonable opening range and is frequently dominated. If I call I'll be out of position post-flop (I'll have to act first), making post-flop play difficult. A 3-bet bluff here is unattractive: I have poor blockers and this hand rarely improves. With deep stacks and many hands left, folding and preserving chips for better spots is the correct +EV play.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. J♦3♥ is a marginal but acceptable raising hand in HU — it plays well postflop with position and gives me fold equity preflop. I’ll raise to 300 (total commitment = 300). Size rationale: 3x the BB is a standard HU open that gets folds, avoids giving a cheap flop, and keeps Flash’s calling range wide; a limp/call is too passive and the min-raise (200) is too small. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet; if Flash calls I’ll use position to c-bet favourable flops and control the pot on dry/J-high boards, extracting value or bluffing selectively.

I am holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has raised from the button to 300. Given the strength of my hand, I should 3-bet for value and to exert pressure. Raising also helps me regain initiative while being out of position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3-bet sizing here is approximately 3x to 3.5x the initial raise. I will raise to 1,000 to build the pot and potentially take it down immediately.

I opened very wide on the button with J♦3♥, but Flash’s 3‑bet to 1000 is a sizeable response. The pot is 1300 and I must call 700 (calling would make the pot 2000), so I need ~35% equity to break even. J3o is largely dominated by a typical 3‑betting range (pairs, broadways, suited A/K combos) and will have well under that equity out of position. A 4‑bet bluff with this hand is too thin and a shove would be unnecessarily committing with a weak holding. With deep stacks and many better spots to exploit later, the highest EV line here is to fold and preserve chips.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium hand in this position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value against Minni's defending range in the big blind.

Facing a standard SB/button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 2♦7♥. To call is 200 into a 400 pot (would make it 600) — roughly 3:1 pot odds — but 7-2 offsuit has almost no equity vs a button opening range and terrible post‑flop playability (dominated, no straight/flush potential). With deep stacks this would put me in messy, losing spots. 3-betting here is unnecessary and weakens my range. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♦6♦s — a suited connector with good playability in position. I want initiative and fold equity rather than just completing to 100, and with deep stacks I can realize equity postflop. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind to 300 total (putting pressure on the BB while keeping the pot manageable). Plan: c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously vs heavy resistance; fold to a large 3-bet but consider calling small 3-bets to see a flop. Raising here balances fold equity, position, and postflop playability.

I'm holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Ten-Nine offsuit is a solid hand to defend in a heads-up situation, and I am getting favorable 2:1 pot odds to see a flop. I will call the additional 200 chips to see if I can connect with the board.

In this heads-up situation, I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker on a board of K-9-7. While I have a piece of the board, it is a relatively vulnerable hand. I am out of position against Minni, who raised pre-flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how she reacts. If she bets, middle pair is usually strong enough to call one street to see a turn, especially in a heads-up pot where ranges are wide. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor often polarizes my hand too much or forces out the bluffs I want her to make.

Flop: 7♥ K♦ 9♦. I hold 4♦6♦ — no pair but a four-card diamond flush draw (9 outs). That's ~19% to hit on the turn and ~35% to hit by the river. I have little showdown value, so checking behind loses fold equity. Flash called my preflop 3x open and then checked, which is consistent with a wide, often passive calling range (weak Kx, Qx, small pairs, suited connectors). C-betting as a semi-bluff is the standard play: it folds many worse hands and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll size half-pot to 1) generate fold equity, 2) keep the pot manageable if called, and 3) allow straightforward fold-to-a-big-check-raise logic. If he calls I can use position and my draw; if he check-raises large I’ll respect strength and fold. Bet 300 into 600 to continue aggression while protecting against big commitment without a made hand.

I have middle pair (Nines) on a board with a King. Minni's half-pot continuation bet is standard for a pre-flop aggressor. In heads-up play, middle pair is too strong to fold to a single bet, especially given the pot odds (300 to win 900). I'll call and see how the turn develops.

The turn brings a second King, paired with the one on the flop. I have a pair of Nines, which now makes two pair with the Kings on the board. While the second King makes it slightly less likely that Minni has a King herself (since there are only two left in the deck), it is still a significant part of her range as the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the most sensible action here to control the pot size and see how Minni proceeds. If she checks back, I am likely ahead of her bluffs or draws; if she bets, I can re-evaluate, but my pair of Nines remains a decent bluff-catcher in a heads-up pot.

Board: 7♥ K♦ 9♦ K♠. I hold 4♦6♦ — no pair but a four-card diamond draw (9 outs, ≈19% to hit the river). Pot is 1,200 and Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c-bet. That check indicates a lot of marginal hands/draws in his calling range (and fewer strong slow-played trips), so a semi-bluff here can pick up the pot now or build it when I have equity. I want fold equity but don’t want to overcommit with a single-card draw, so I’ll size to roughly half-pot to pressure weaker hands while keeping the commitment reasonable if called. Plan: bet 600 as a semi-bluff; if he folds I take down the pot, if he calls I still have ≈19% to hit the flush and can use position on the river. If he check-raises large I’ll respect the strength and fold rather than risking a big portion of my deep stack with a draw-only hand.

On a board of 7h-Kd-9d-Ks, I hold a pair of Nines, which gives me two pair (Kings and Nines) with a Ten kicker. Minni's turn bet of 600 into 1200 (a half-pot bet) indicates strength, but in a heads-up match, a pair of Nines is a very strong bluff-catcher. The pairing of the King on the turn makes it mathematically less likely that she holds a King himself. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, and my hand is ahead of her semi-bluffs (like diamond draws or straight draws) and potential air. Raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a King or AA), so I will call to see the river and keep the pot manageable.

On this river, I have two pair (Kings and Nines) with a Ten kicker. The board is 7h-Kd-9d-Ks-Qh. The Queen on the river is a potentially problematic card, as it completes a possible straight for a hand like J-T and improves any Q-x or pocket Queens. Given the action so far, where Minni has shown aggression on every street, checking is the most appropriate action. My hand has showdown value as a bluff-catcher against missed diamond draws or smaller pairs, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking, I allow Minni to either check back with her medium-strength hands or attempt a bluff with her missed draws, which I can then consider calling.

I missed my diamond flush on the river and currently have no pair (4♦6♦ on 7♥ K♦ 9♦ K♠ Q♥). The opponent cold‑called my flop and turn bets, which is consistent with a sticky calling range (Kx, Qx, 9x, small pairs, some draws). Given they’ve shown willingness to call two streets, my river bluff has very little fold equity — many of their hands will call a bluff. The pot is only 2,400 while effective stacks are deep (my 21,700 / his 15,900), so risking chips on a low‑EV bluff isn’t justified. I’ll check back and avoid unnecessary loss; if he now bets I can fold and preserve a large stack for better spots.

I have Qd Tc on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a solid Broadway hand well within my opening range. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and exert positional pressure.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open from the SB with deep stacks (my stack 21,600 ≈ 216BB, opponent 18,000 ≈ 180BB). I hold a small pocket pair (4♦4♥). This is an ideal set-mining spot: calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me strong implied odds to hit a set and extract a big payout postflop. A 3-bet for thin value would put me in tricky, deep OOP spots against many overcards and range pressure; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to see a flop, look to trap or proceed cautiously if I miss, and only commit further with strong improvement or favorable reads. I will call the 200 additional chips (total committed on the street = 300).

Flop: 6♦ Q♠ K♠. I hold 4♦4♥ — an underpair that missed the flop (no set). Pot is 600 and both stacks are very deep (my stack ~21,400, opponent ~18,000). This is a classic set‑mining spot where I want to keep the pot under control and see another card rather than bloating the pot out of position. There is no bet to call now, so I will check. Plan: if Flash c‑bets small-to-medium (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot ≈ 300) I will call to continue looking for my set; if he fires a large pot‑sized or overbet I will fold most of the time because my hand has very little showdown equity. If I hit a 4 on the turn I will shift to value extraction; without improvement I won’t bluff-raise here.

I have second pair (Queens) on a board that contains both a King and a Queen. In a heads-up situation, this is a strong holding. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to extract value from draws (like the spade flush draw or a straight draw like JT) and to protect my hand against overcards like an Ace. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals while keeping the pot manageable if I am actually behind a King.

Flop (6♦ Q♠ K♠), I hold 4♦4♥ — an underpair that missed. Villain (Flash) opened preflop from the SB with a wide/strong-ish heads‑up range and now fires ~300 into the pot (~1/2 pot). This sizing is in my calling band for a set‑mining spot: I want to keep the pot controllable and see the turn rather than face huge OOP decisions or attempt a useless bluff‑raise. Pure set odds to hit by the river (~8%) don’t justify the call by themselves versus these pot odds, but heads‑up I expect a lot of hands in his range that can be out‑bluffed or give up on later streets, so calling one street for implied odds is correct. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn; if I hit a 4 shift to value extraction. Without improvement I will fold to a large turn shove/overbet or serious pressure, but I will call small turn probes to keep seeing cards. I will not raise — aggression OOP with an underpair is incorrect here.

I check. With 4♦4♥ on 6♦ Q♠ K♠ T♦ I'm an underpair with only two outs to hit a set on the river (~2/46 ≈ 4.3%) and no realistic draw. Pot is 1,200 and both stacks are deep, but I'm out of position — I want pot control and to avoid committing chips with a very weak hand. Checking keeps the pot small; I will fold to meaningful turn aggression (pot-sized or large bets). Only an essentially token probe (very tiny bet, roughly ≤ ~50 chips) would merit a call on pure pot‑odds grounds; anything larger is a losing chase without substantial implied odds. If Flash checks back I'll check the river and re-evaluate only if I hit or the river/line gives a clear reason to bluff or value. I will not lead, raise, or bluff here.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Queens and Tens). The board (Ks Qs 6d Td) is quite coordinated, with AJ completing a straight and KQ holding a superior two pair. However, in a heads-up match, my hand is very strong and likely ahead of a wide range of hands that Minni could be calling with, such as Kx, lower Queens, or draws like spade flushes or open-ended straights. Since Minni checked to me, I will bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. I'll bet 800, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to extract maximum value from a King or a drawing hand.

On the turn I hold 4♦4♥ on 6♦ Q♠ K♠ T♦. Flash bets 800 and the pot shown is 2000, so calling 800 would give me a final pot of 2800 and requires roughly 800/2800 ≈ 28.6% equity to break even. I only have two outs to make a set (2/46 ≈ 4.35% chance), so my raw equity is far below the pot‑odds. Villain’s line (open, flop c‑bet 300, now a sizable 800 on the turn) is weighted to value (top pair/overpairs/sets) and raising out of position with an underpair would be a pure, high‑risk bluff with poor fold equity and strong chance of being jammed or called by better hands. I explicitly planned to fold to meaningful turn aggression; this bet meets that threshold. Fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

We're on the button (small blind) with Qc6d — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call, so calling gives ~200 total (50 into 200) — about 20% equity required to be profitable. In position with deep stacks I prefer seeing a flop cheaply rather than committing extra preflop with a smallish raise that won’t fold out many hands and could invite a 3-bet. Plan: call to use positional advantage; continue with top pair/strong draws or pot-control/fold to heavy aggression when we miss.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h5h. The small blind (Minni) has just completed (called) the extra 50 chips. This gives me the option to check and see a free flop or raise. 3h5h is a speculative hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, but it is not strong enough to warrant a raise for value or as a semi-bluff against a wide completing range in a heads-up scenario. I will take the free flop to minimize risk and see if I can connect.

On the flop of 2s-Kc-4s, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 3h5h (needing an Ace or a Six to complete the straight). Minni, the pre-flop completer, has checked to me. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to see the turn for free and realize the equity of my draw without risking a check-raise from a potential King or a spade draw. In a small pot like this, taking the free card is a high-equity, low-risk play.

Flop is Kc 2s 4s; I completely missed with Qc6d (no pair and no real draw). Heads-up the board is very dry and Flash checked from the big blind, so his range is wide and contains many unpaired hands. This is a good spot to seize the initiative with a small continuation bet: it will fold out lots of worse hands and high-card hands while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll bet half-pot (100). If Flash raises I’ll fold (I have essentially no equity); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 3h5h on a board of 2s-Kc-4s. After checking to Minni, she has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call. With 8 outs to a straight (Aces and Sixes), my equity to hit the straight on the next card is roughly 17%, and about 32% by the river. Given the favorable pot odds and the potential to win a larger pot if I hit my draw, calling is the most standard and mathematically sound play here. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option, but calling allows me to see the turn cheaply while keeping her range wide.

I have an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Six) on a board of 2s-Kc-4s-4h. On the flop, Minni bet 100 into a 200-chip pot and I called to see the turn. The turn paired the board with the 4h. As I am out of position, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board pairing and potentially see a free river card to complete my straight. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but leading out here as a semi-bluff into the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop is risky.

I c-bet the flop with Qc6d and Flash called. The turn (4h) pairs the board, introducing the possibility of trips, and Flash now checks. I have no pair and no realistic draw — this is a pure bluff spot if I bet, and a raise from him would be very costly with almost no equity. With deep stacks relative to the pot and my earlier plan to proceed cautiously on the turn, I’ll check back to control the pot size and avoid folding chips into a stronger holding. Plan: check, and fold to any sizable bet on the river (re-evaluate only if he checks again).

I have 5-high after failing to complete my open-ended straight draw. The board is currently King-high with a paired four, and the river card completed a potential spade flush. Since Minni checked back on the turn, she likely has marginal showdown value or a missed draw that still beats my 5-high. However, the pot is small (400), and I have not shown much strength this hand. Checking is the most sensible play as a bluff would likely need to be large to move her off even a weak pair or Ace-high, and I would rather preserve my stack.

I have Qc6d with no pair or draw on a final board Kc 2s 4s 4h Js. I c‑bet the flop and was called, then both of us checked the turn when the board paired — the river is a pure bluff spot but my folding equity is low versus a wide calling/raising range (Kx, Jx, pocket pairs). With ~20k stacks and only a 400 pot, a river bet invites a costly check‑raise or call and I have essentially no showdown value. I stick to my prior plan to control the pot: check and fold to any bet. If Flash checks back I show; if he bets I fold.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with 7h 8c, which is a decent connector for stealing the blinds or playing a post-flop game. Given the stack depths and the tendency for both players to play relatively wide pre-flop in this tournament, a standard open raise is appropriate to put pressure on Minni and take control of the hand early.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a button open to 300. My hand 9♦4♥ is very weak offsuit, has poor equity and terrible playability out of position. Pot is 400 and calling 200 more (to 300 total) requires ~33% equity; 9-4o is well below that versus a reasonable button opening range. A min-3bet to 500 is possible as a bluff, but against this opponent it will often get called or re-raised and I’ll be out of position with a junk hand. Better EV is to conserve chips and wait for a stronger spot. I fold.

Heads-up on the button with 3♠8♦: very weak hand but huge positional advantage and deep stacks. Folding here is too nitty and limping is passive — I want initiative and to pressure the BB. A small standard heads-up open to 200 (2×BB) keeps the pot small, preserves fold equity, and allows me to play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I can c-bet dry boards or pot-control on coordinated turns/rivers. Raise to 200 to maximize EV while keeping options postflop.

4h 2d is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. While the price to call is relatively cheap (100 chips to win a 300 chip pot), the hand has extremely poor playability and realization of equity, especially when playing out of position for the rest of the hand. I will choose to fold and look for better opportunities to commit chips.

In a heads-up situation, a King-high hand like King-Deuce or King-Five is well within the standard opening range from the button (small blind). I have been consistently raising to 300 in this position to maintain aggression and leverage my position. Minni has shown a mix of folding, calling, and occasionally 3-betting, so a standard raise is the most effective way to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I have J♥9♥ in the big blind — a playable, suited connector with good flop playability and strong implied odds with deep stacks (21200). Calling the additional 200 (total 300 committed including my 100 BB) is the +EV line: I get ~2:1 immediate pot odds and can realize equity on a wide range of flops, while avoiding bloating the pot out-of-position with a marginal 3-bet. Flash opens frequently with a small 300 sizing, so calling keeps my range wide and lets me exploit postflop tendencies (float, check-raise, or extract value when I hit). I’ll call and evaluate on the flop rather than iso‑3betting with a marginal hand OOP.

Flop is 6♥‑8♦‑2♥ and I hold J♥9♥: two overcards plus a very strong heart draw (9 hearts left). That’s ~9 outs to a flush and an additional 3 Js + 3 9s for pair outs (15 total, ~54% to pair or make a flush by river, though some of those outs are less clean vs AX♥ or paired/strong hands). Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains lots of hands with poor flop equity that can fold to pressure. I want fold equity while keeping the pot manageable OOP and preserving implied odds if called. A half‑pot c‑bet (300 into 600) strikes that balance: it pressures marginal hands, builds the pot when I hit, and leaves room to call/raise on later streets with deep stacks. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the turn (value-bet big on a heart, control/critically re-evaluate on blanks, fold or call to raise depending on sizing and reads). Therefore I’ll bet half pot: 300.

On this low, somewhat disconnected board, my King-high still holds significant equity against Minni's wide range. Her lead for half the pot (300 into 600) could represent a weak pair, a draw (like 79 or a heart draw), or air. With the King as an overcard and a backdoor heart flush draw with the Five of Hearts, I have enough equity and position to call and see how the turn develops. Raising here might be unnecessary since I'd prefer to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage on later streets.

Situation: Heads‑up, stacks deep (Minni 20,700 vs Flash 18,100). Board: 6♥‑8♦‑2♥‑3♣. I have J♥9♥ — two overcards + a very strong heart draw. On the flop I c‑bet half pot and got called, so Flash’s calling range is wide (pairs like 6x/8x, weak Ax/Kx, pocket pairs, various backdoor draws). The turn 3♣ is a blank that doesn’t remove my outs: I still have 9 hearts + the non‑heart Js/9s = ~15 outs on the turn (~32% to improve on the river). Flash checked the turn, showing either weakness or pot‑control with a marginal made hand. Plan: continue as a semi‑bluff to (1) take the pot down now vs many of his non‑heart holdings, (2) build the pot when I hit, and (3) retain fold equity while keeping the pot manageable OOP. A half‑pot bet is the right balance here — applies pressure but leaves room to fold to a big raise. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I still have ~32% to improve and can proceed according to the river card (bet big for value on a heart, check‑back blanks and evaluate showdown or a river shove). If he check‑raises large on the turn I will fold this draw (unless the raise is small enough that calling is correct on pot‑odds + implied odds). Action: bet ~1/2 pot (600).

Minni has followed her flop donk-bet with a second barrel on the turn. My King-high has not improved, and the turn 3c has actually reduced my equity by eliminating my backdoor heart flush draw. While I have picked up a gutshot straight draw, the pot odds of 3:1 are not high enough to justify chasing a four-outer. Given Minni's persistent aggression and the weakness of my non-pair hand, I cannot profitably call another bet. I will fold here to protect my stack, as Hand 110 of 150 still leaves plenty of time to find a better spot.

Heads-up preflop with J♣6♠ on the button (SB). In heads-up I should be opening a very wide range — limp/folding is too passive and gives the BB good odds. The min-raise is 200, but I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to get fold equity, define the hand, and play the flop in position. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if called I can c-bet or evaluate based on the flop. Stack depths (21850 vs 18000) are deep enough to play postflop, so an open to 300 is optimal here.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 5s and Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 odds. Against a wide button opening range, Js 5s has sufficient equity and playability to defend. While the hand is not strong enough to 3-bet for value, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my position or hit a strong draw/top pair. I will call the 200.

I hit bottom pair with a Jack kicker on an Ace-high board. This board texture generally favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. I will check to the aggressor to evaluate the strength of her hand based on her betting action. Bottom pair is too weak to lead into the pot but has enough value to potentially check-call a small bet.

Heads‑up I opened wide preflop with J♣6♠ and got called; pot is 600 and I’m in position. Flop A♦ T♣ 5♦ heavily favors the caller’s range — many Ax, Tx, and diamond draws — and my hand has no pair and no meaningful draw. Against a calling station on an A‑high two‑diamond board a continuation bet will get called too often and will be unprofitable as a bluff. I’ll check for pot control and to see what Flash does: if he bets small (≈1/3 pot ≈ 200) I’ll call to realize equity and play the turn in position; if he sizes up to ~1/2 pot or more I’ll fold because my hand has very little equity and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position into a range disadvantage. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the turn and look to take a free card or punish a vulnerable check with a small turn bet on a harmless card.

On the turn 4d, the board now shows three diamonds and several straight possibilities. My pair of fives is quite weak in this context. Minni checked back the flop, which suggests she likely doesn't have a strong Ace or a set, but she could still hold a Ten, a small pocket pair, or a diamond draw. I will check to see how she reacts to the flush card. If she checks behind, I have some showdown value; if she bets, I'll have to evaluate her sizing and the likelihood she's representing the flush or a slow-played Ace.

Heads‑up with J♣6♠ on A♦ T♣ 5♦ 4♦. I have no pair and no diamond, but Flash checked the flop and turned — that line contains lots of missed hands (Kx/Qx/Jx/Tx), some medium pairs, and some backdoor/paired hands. A small, probing bet can fold many of those hands and gains information while keeping the pot small if I’m called. I don’t want to bloat the pot on a board that now has three diamonds (there’s a non‑trivial chance of a made flush), so I’ll make a ~1/3‑pot bluff to pick up the pot or see a cheap river. Bet 200. If he raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

Minni checked the flop (Tc Ad 5d), which significantly reduces the probability that she holds a strong Ace or a Ten. When the 4d arrives on the turn, completing the flush draw, her 1/3 pot bet of 200 looks like a standard 'probe' bet or a semi-bluff. While my pair of fives is weak, the pot odds are 4:1, and my hand still beats her high-card bluffs or smaller pairs if she is turning them into a bluff. Given her previous aggression and the flop check-back, I have sufficient equity to call and see a river card.

On the river, the board now shows four diamonds (Tc Ad 5d 4d Qd). I have a pair of fives with no diamond in my hand. Minni checked the flop, then bet the turn when the third diamond appeared. The fourth diamond on the river makes it extremely likely that any single diamond in Minni's range now has a flush. Since I do not hold a diamond to block any of her possible flushes, and my pair of fives has very little showdown value against her range (which likely contains many high cards or small diamonds), checking is the only logical play. I will check and evaluate her action; if she bets, I will likely fold as I am only beating a pure bluff without a diamond.

Board: Tc Ad 5d 4d Qd. I hold J♣6♠ — no pair, no diamond, essentially a bluff-only hand. I bet 200 on the turn and was called; that line strongly suggests Flash either had a made hand (A/T/pair) or a diamond draw that the Q♦ just completed. Pot is 1,000 and to bluff profitably I’d need a substantial fold frequency (≈23% for a ~300 bet), but Flash’s turn call and his general call-down tendencies in prior hands make that fold equity unlikely. Betting here risks being called by almost every hand that beats me (and by the completed flush), so the +EV play is to avoid committing more chips and check to showdown. I’ll check and concede this pot rather than barrel again into a heavy diamond board with no equity.

I am on the button with 6c 5d. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, but given the 50/100 blind structure and the remaining 38 hands, I want to keep the pot small and avoid over-committing with a marginal connector. Minni has a slightly larger stack and has been playing relatively aggressively pre-flop. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size here to take control of the hand and put pressure on the big blind.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button with Tc8h in the big blind. Stacks are very deep (≈213bb vs 183bb) and the pot is 400; calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives me ~3:1 pot odds (break-even ≈25%). Tc8h is a playable heads-up hand with decent equity vs a wide button opening range and good implied odds to hit two pair/straights. 3-betting OOP with a marginal hand is suboptimal — I’d rather see a flop and play postflop cautiously: continue when I hit or have good draws, control the pot with misses, and fold to excessive aggression. Therefore I will call (putting a total of 300 in).

I have T♣8♥ on Q♠ A♣ 8♦ — second pair but with two overcards (A and Q) and I'm out of position versus a standard 3x button opener. With deep stacks I don't want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal made hand. Checking controls the pot, lets the opener c-bet wide so I can call reasonable-sized continuation bets to realize equity, and avoids getting blown off the hand by a check-raise or large barrel. Plan: check-call small/medium c-bets, fold to a polarized large raise, and if checked to, consider a delayed bet on safe turn cards or proceed cautiously to showdown.

I raised pre-flop and the flop came Ace-Queen high. This board heavily favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Even though I have no actual value with 6-high, a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) is a standard play here to put pressure on Minni and force folds from her hands that missed the flop, such as middle pairs or lower cards.

Call 300. Flash’s flop bet is small (~1/3 pot) which usually indicates a wide continuation range from the button. I have second pair (8s) with a decent Ten kicker — enough showdown value vs a wide c-bet and good implied odds with deep stacks. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise; check‑raising is too thin and will get me into difficult spots versus A/Q/top pairs/sets. Calling controls the pot, realizes equity, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn: fold to a polarized check‑raise or a large turn barrel, call reasonably sized turns, and consider a small protection/value bet only if checked to on a safe turn card.

I have T♣8♥ — second pair (8s) with a ten kicker on Q♠ A♣ 8♦ 6♠. Villain c‑bet the flop small (~1/3 pot) and then checked the turn, which is a safe card. OOP with a marginal made hand I don’t want to bloat the pot, but a small protection/value stab is correct: it denies equity to backdoor/turning gutshots and folds out many overcards and pure air, while getting called by worse pairs and some pocket pairs. Size to ~1/3 pot to keep the pot manageable and retain fold equity. Plan: bet 400; fold to a large raise, and if called re‑evaluate on the river (check to showdown or target a thin value bet depending on runout).

Minni has led into me on the turn with a small bet of 400 into a 1200 pot after I continuation bet the flop. The 6s on the turn improved my hand to bottom pair. While the Ace and Queen on the board are intimidating, Minni's small lead (25% pot) often represents a blocking bet with a marginal hand like an 8, a draw (spades or a straight draw like 79), or even a total bluff. I am getting 4-to-1 odds to call, which is more than enough to justify seeing a river with a pair in a heads-up match. I will call and re-evaluate on the river based on her action and the final card.

I have Tc8h — second pair (eights) on Qs Ac 8d 6s Jd. Villain opened 3x preflop, made a small flop c‑bet (300) that I called, then checked the turn and called my small turn bet (400). That line is consistent with a mostly made‑hand/medium strength calling range (Ax, Qx, Jx, Kx, some Tx/9x and occasional floats). Many of those hands beat my second pair, and a thin river value bet would mostly get called by better hands while folding out only the very weakest parts of his range — so it would be negative EV. Checking controls the pot OOP, preserves my showdown equity, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand when the river J adds additional pairs that beat me. I’ll check to showdown and evaluate opponent’s action; call small river bets but fold to large river raises.

The river is the Jack of Diamonds, completing the board as Ac Qs 8d 6s Jd. I currently hold a pair of sixes, which is bottom pair. Minni led out on the turn with a small bet of 400 and has now checked the river. This sequence—calling a flop bet, leading a turn card that doesn't obviously change the board texture for the pre-flop caller, and then checking the river—often indicates a marginal made hand like an 8 or a weak Jack, or a missed draw. My pair of sixes has some showdown value in a heads-up match, but it is unlikely to be called by any worse hand if I bet, and it is equally unlikely to force a fold from a better hand like an 8 or a Queen. Therefore, the most prudent action is to check back, realize my equity, and see the showdown without risking more chips.

Raise to 300. Heads-up on the button gives me post‑flop position — huge advantage. 7s6s is a very playable suited connector heads‑up: it flops hands and strong draws and has excellent implied odds with our deep stack (22,350 vs 17,500). Limping/calling is too passive and surrenders initiative; a 3x-ish open (to 300) applies pressure, narrows the BB defend range, and lets me play the flop in position. Plan: if called, C‑bet favorable/drawy boards and realize equity; if met with heavy resistance (a large 3‑bet), re‑evaluate and fold versus strong lines.

I am in the Big Blind with T6o. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call. Given the current stack sizes and the 2:1 pot odds offered, this hand is a standard defense in a heads-up situation, although it's on the weaker side of my range. I've observed Minni to be quite active and willing to fold to pressure post-flop (Hand #110, Hand #112). Defending here allows me to see a flop and potentially take the pot away later if the board texture allows, or simply realize my equity.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) on a wet board (Jd 9c 6c). Checking is the standard play as the Big Blind defender to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts. My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out into the raiser.

I opened preflop to 300 from the button and the BB defended. On J♦ 9♣ 6♣ I have 7♠6♠ — middle pair with a decent backdoor and position. Heads‑up my range is wide and this flop is fairly dry (one high card, one medium, two clubs but no made flush yet). C‑betting here accomplishes three things: (1) gets value from worse pairs and many 9x/6x/tx hands that will call a reasonable bet, (2) denies equity to broadway overcards and club draws, and (3) uses my positional advantage to keep control of the pot. I don’t want to overcommit to the nutless board, so I size to about half‑pot to charge draws and thin value hands while leaving room to fold to heavy resistance. Bet 300 into the 600 pot; if he jams or check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold this medium pair, but vs a call I’ll continue to realize equity and play the turn in position.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a wet board (Jd 9c 6c). Minni has continuation bet 300 into a 600 pot. With 3:1 pot odds, bottom pair is a standard call in a heads-up pot. My hand has showdown value and I can improve to two pair or trips, or catch a straight if the board runs out favorably. Raising would turn my hand into a bluff unnecessarily, and folding is too tight given the price and the wide ranges in heads-up play.

I am first to act on the turn after calling a continuation bet on the flop. I have bottom pair (Sixes) with a ten kicker. The board (Jd 9c 6c 5h) is somewhat coordinated but the 5h is mostly a blank, although it does complete a straight for exactly 78. My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value against Minni's range. Checking is the most appropriate action to control the pot size and see how Minni proceeds. If she checks behind, I get to see a free river; if she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, though I likely have the odds to call one more bet given the pot size and my pair.

Turn: J♦ 9♣ 6♣ 5♥. I hold 7♠6♠ — a small pair (sixes) with 7-kicker plus significant equity to improve: 4 eights complete a 5-6-7-8-9 straight, plus three remaining sevens (two pair) and two remaining sixes (trips) — ~9 outs (~36%) to improve on the river. Villain called my flop c-bet, so his range likely contains Jx/9x, some small pairs, and club/straight draws. Checking gives him a free card to improve or realize equity cheaply; betting charges his draws and gets value from worse pairs and some single-pair hands. I’ll make a half‑pot protection/value bet of 600 to deny free cards, charge draws, and keep the pot manageable. If he raises big I’ll give up — my hand is vulnerable to J/9 and big aggression — but if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river for value or pot control.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) on a coordinated board (Jd 9c 6c 5h). Minni is betting 600 into a 1200 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, bottom pair is typically a call against a second barrel, as the opponent's range contains many bluffs and semi-bluffs such as flush draws or straight draws. Folding would be too exploitable given the price, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands while folding out the bluffs I currently beat.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) on a board that just saw an Ace land on the river. Throughout the hand, I have been check-calling Minni's bets. The Ace is a significant card because it likely hits a portion of her opening and double-barreling range (especially missed flush or straight draws that contained an Ace). My hand has very little value now and cannot be bet for value, nor is it a good candidate for a bluff after calling two streets. Checking is the only viable option to see if I can reach showdown cheaply or to evaluate a final bet from Minni, though the Ace makes a call extremely difficult if she continues to show aggression.

Board: J♦ 9♣ 6♣ 5♥ A♦. I hold 7♠6♠ — a single pair of sixes. Flash defended preflop and called both my flop (300) and turn (600) bets, so his continuing range is heavy in Jx/9x, medium pairs, some Tx and a number of missed club/straight draws. The river Ace improves a large portion of that calling range (all Ax and many Jx/9x) while missed draws and 5x are the primary hands I realistically get value from. A river bet would mostly fold out the weaker hands I beat and either get called by better hands or induce a raise I’m likely to fold to — a poor risk/reward trade. With a vulnerable, marginal made hand and deep stacks, pot control is the highest EV line: check to showdown and realize equity. If Flash now barrels into me I’ll evaluate sizing, but I will frequently fold to a large river bet.

I am on the button with 85o, which is a very weak starting hand even for heads-up play. While I want to maintain pressure, Minni has been relatively active and willing to defend or 3-bet. With deep stacks and over 35 hands remaining in the tournament, I can afford to fold the bottom of my range and wait for better spots to utilize my stack. Folding here is a disciplined play to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop against a capable opponent.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a very wide range — K7h is a perfectly fine raising hand: a top-card king plus suited hearts gives good postflop playability and backdoor equity. Flash in the big blind has shown a mix of aggression and frequent 3xx preflop actions, but I have position and deep stacks so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure. Standard heads-up sizing and my table image both support a 2.5–3x BB open; raising to 300 gives good fold equity, denies cheap odds to many dominated hands, and keeps postflop playability intact without committing too much of my 21,200 stack. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to extract value or apply pressure, and re-evaluate if I face a 3-bet. Therefore I raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs3s, a suited gapper that plays reasonably well in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. The cost to call is 200 to win a total pot of 600, giving me 3:1 odds. Given the deep stacks (nearly 200 BBs) and the fact that I am in position for the rest of the hand (as the big blind in a heads-up game actually acts second post-flop), I have the right odds and playability to defend my blind. Fold is too tight; raising is unnecessary. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with Queens on a Qd-2c-Td board. While my kicker (3s) is very weak, top pair is a significant hand in heads-up play. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to allow her to continue with her range, which includes bluffs and draws. I am prepared to call most standard-sized bets on this street.

I opened wide on the button and this flop (Qd 2c Td) is relatively dry for a heads-up pot — it hits more of my calling range than it does a defending-only range. I have K♥7♥ (no pair) so my main goal is to take the pot down now or charge draws/weak pairs that would call a tiny bluff. Flash has shown a lot of check-calling tendencies, so I don’t want to overcommit bluffing, but I still should continue with range pressure from position. I’ll c-bet a standard half-pot (300) to fold out pure air, keep the price reasonable when called, and retain flexibility on the turn: fold to strong raise unless I improve or get a very favorable card, otherwise proceed cautiously and look for value or a better bluffing opportunity.

I have flopped top pair with Queens on a board of Qd-2c-Td. While my kicker is weak (3s), top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. I should not fold such a strong hand, but raising is also risky as it might isolate me against better Queens or sets. By calling, I keep the pot manageable and allow Minni to continue betting with her bluffs or weaker draws. I will call and reassess on the turn.

On the turn, an Ace of Spades has appeared, which is a significant card that likely connects with Minni's pre-flop raising range (Ace-high hands). I currently hold second pair (Queens) with a very weak kicker (3). Minni has checked behind on the turn after I checked to her. This check-back often indicates a hand that has some showdown value but doesn't want to build a large pot, or a draw that is taking a free card. Since I am first to act on the river and the current street is checked around, I will check here to see the river for free and maintain control of the pot size, given that my hand is no longer the top pair and is vulnerable to any Ace or better Queen.

I missed — K♥7♥ has no pair and the turn A♠ is a clear scare card that improves a large portion of the range that called my flop c-bet (Ax, Qx, Tx, some pocket pairs). Flash has shown a tendency to check‑call and to defend wide, so my fold equity on a double barrel is thin and there’s no real value to bet. With position and a medium pot (≈1200) I prefer to control the pot and realize equity rather than bluff into a sticky caller or get check‑raised off a marginal hand. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river — fold to a large bet, call small/medium bets with select Kx or showdown-capable hands, and consider a river bluff only on favorable runouts and frequencies.

I have second pair (Queens) with a weak kicker on a board where an Ace appeared on the turn. My opponent (Minni) checked behind on the turn after I checked, which often indicates she does not have an Ace and is looking for a cheap showdown with a marginal hand like a Ten, a smaller pocket pair, or a weaker Queen, or she is giving up on a bluff. By checking the river, I can either reach a cheap showdown or potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw (like diamonds). Betting myself is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and might force me to fold if she raises with a better Queen or an Ace she was playing cautiously. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to maximize my chances of winning the pot while minimizing risk.

Check. I missed the river with K♥7♥ and have no showdown value. My flop c‑bet was called and the turn A♠ is a real scare — it improves lots of hands that would continue (Ax, Qx, Tx, pocket pairs). Flash repeatedly shows a check‑calling tendency, so my river fold equity is very low and a bluff is unlikely to work. There are no worse hands that will call a value bet, so betting risks chips for little gain. With deep stacks and a small pot (≈1200) I prefer pot control and to realize equity; I will check and fold to a meaningful raise.

J3o is a weak hand at the bottom of my range. Although heads-up strategy suggests raising a wide range from the button, Minni has been active and sticky post-flop. With the tournament nearing its end and stacks relatively balanced, I prefer to fold this junk hand and wait for a more advantageous spot rather than risking chips with a low-equity hand.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♠6♣ — marginal but perfectly playable in this spot. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete; against a single blind I want to take the initiative and open a wide range to steal and play position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) instead of min-raising to 200: it gives more fold equity, sets a reasonable sizing vs a player who raises often, and keeps the pot small relative to our deep 206bb stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, use position to c-bet favorable flops and realize equity on others.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-5 offsuit, a weak hand. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. While I have the chips to defend, 7-5 offsuit performs poorly out of position and doesn't have much straight or flush potential. Given we are deep-stacked, I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Deuce of Clubs and Five of Spades, which is a very weak hand. I am against Minni, who has been relatively active and sticky in recent hands. Given that this is a heads-up situation and the hand has very little potential, I will fold to avoid getting into a difficult post-flop spot with a weak hand, especially since I am slightly behind in the overall chip counts and don't want to bleed chips needlessly.

Heads-up on the button with TsJs — a very playable, suited Broadway with great postflop potential and position. Limping/calling surrenders the initiative to the BB and lets an aggressive player dictate the hand; raising takes the initiative, gets folds from worse hands, and builds a pot when we have a hand that plays well multi-street. A standard 3xBB to 300 (total) is a clean size: it’s small relative to our 20.8k stack so it preserves flexibility, gives solid fold equity, and keeps weaker hands in their calling range. Plan: if called use position to c-bet favorable flops and extract value; if reraised re-evaluate based on sizing. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and maximize EV with this hand.

I have Qd 7s in the Big Blind. Minni raised to 3x from the Small Blind. In a heads-up situation, Q7o is a reasonable defending hand against a standard opening range. The pot odds (2:1) make this a standard call to see the flop and play post-flop, as folding would be too tight and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength.

I am the Big Blind and first to act on the flop. I have missed the flop entirely with Queen-high on a dry King-high board. There is no reason to lead out here against the pre-flop aggressor, so I will check and see how they respond. If they bet, I will most likely fold given the lack of any real equity or draws.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with T♠J♠. Flop K♠ 9♥ 2♦ is a dry, relatively benign board that heavily favors a preflop raiser’s range. I don’t have a pair, but I have meaningful equity: a Q completes a Broadway straight (4 outs) and there are backdoor spade possibilities. Flash has shown a passive, check/call tendency postflop, but a standard c‑bet here will fold out many unpaired overcards and weaker hands while keeping worse hands and floats in. I’ll apply pressure with a half‑pot c‑bet to get value when ahead and maintain initiative. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (pivot to continuation or pot‑control depending on the card); if he raises large I will fold unless the raise is tiny and merits a call.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-high on a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Minni's 1/2 pot c-bet on this dry board (K-9-2 rainbow) is standard. Without a pair, a draw, or any significant backdoor equity, calling is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While heads-up play often requires a wider range, 6-2o is at the very bottom of that range. Minni has shown a willingness to defend the big blind and play post-flop, making a bluff raise with such a poor hand unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T♠6♠ — a playable, suited hand with good postflop playability and implied odds. Facing the big blind, limp/call (putting me to 100 total) is too passive and gives the BB cheap equity. A standard open-raise here both builds the pot and keeps the initiative; with deep stacks (~211 BB) I can comfortably play postflop in position. I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish sizing (300 total) — big enough to fold out very weak hands and deny free cards, but small enough to keep a lot of postflop maneuvering room. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll likely fold T6s; if he calls or 3-bets small I’ll proceed to the flop in position and evaluate.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit in the big blind. This is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. While the pot odds are 3:1, playing such a weak hand out of position is generally unprofitable in the long run. Given the stack depths and the quality of my hand, a fold is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation with a suited connector-style hand like 6h3h, I want to apply pressure from the button, especially since Minni has been folding frequently to pre-flop raises lately. Raising to 300 (a 3x open) is a standard play to take down the blinds or set up a post-flop situation where I have the initiative.

Heads-up the button will be opening a very wide range to 300. Q♥9♠ is a playable hand against that wide range — decent showdown value and some connectivity — and I’m getting good pot odds to see a flop. Pot is 400; I need to call 200 more, which makes my total commitment 300 and gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds. Stacks are deep (~21200, ~106bb), so there are implied odds to realize, but I’m out of position postflop so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting. A small 3-bet OOP would be awkward and unnecessary. I’ll call to see a flop, keep pot control, fold to big aggression on unfavorable runouts, and look to value-bet or bluff-catch when I connect.

Heads-up vs a very wide CO/SB open, Q♥9♠ on 7♣8♦9♥ is top pair with a decent kicker. The flop is coordinated (7-8-9) so there are straight outs (6 or T) but no obvious flush; many of Flash’s opening hands are worse (one-pair hands, broadways, small pairs, single-card straight draws) that will call a c-bet. I want value and to deny equity to those straight draws while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks, so I’ll make a half-pot c-bet to charge worse hands and force defenders to pay to see turns. If Flash raises large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to big check-raises with just top pair), if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn. Bet 300 into the 600 pot.

On this 7c-8d-9h flop, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 6h 3h (needs a 5 or a 10). Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot (a half-pot 'donk' bet). This board texture heavily favors the defender's range, as the Big Blind often has more combinations of 65, T9, 87, and 98. However, given my open-ended straight draw, I have significant equity. Calling is the most prudent play here; it allows me to see a turn card cheaply while keeping the pot manageable. Raising would likely be met with a lot of resistance on this coordinated board, and my hand is a draw that doesn't mind seeing a cheap turn.

Turn 2♠ is a blank — I have top pair (9♠) with a strong Q kicker but I’m out of position and stacks are deep. I c-bet the flop to charge straight draws and weaker one-pair hands and got called, so Flash’s continuing range is heavy with single pairs and open-ended draws (6/T) plus some broadways. Betting again OOP only builds a pot I don’t want to face big aggression in and forces uncomfortable folds/calls; checking keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity, and can induce bluffs on the river. Plan: check and evaluate — call small turn/river bets for value, fold to large aggression I can’t beat, and if Flash checks back we go to a river decision with a reasonably strong showdown hand.

I have an open-ended straight draw (6-high) on a board of 7-8-9-2. Minni led the flop but has now checked the turn. While I could bet to try and take the pot down now, checking back is a safer and more calculated play. It allows me to take a free card to try and hit one of my 8 straight outs (any 5 or 10) without risking a check-raise that would force me off my equity. If I miss on the river, I can still consider a bluff depending on the final board and Minni's action.

The river (7d) paired the board. I have top pair (9s) with a good Q kicker, but the board pairing gives any 7x in Flash’s range trips and reduces my relative hand strength. I bet the flop for value and protection, checked the turn to control the pot and induce action. With deep stacks heads-up Flash has a very wide calling range and checked the turn, so checking the river keeps the pot manageable and avoids inflating into hands that now beat me (7x, straights, or 9x with a better kicker). If Flash bets a small-to-medium size I can call for value; if he raises large I can fold. Prefer pot control and showdown value here rather than thin-value betting and risking a big raise.

I missed my open-ended straight draw on the river. The board paired with a seven, and Minni has checked for the second street in a row after leading the flop. While her checks suggest weakness, she likely holds a marginal hand with some showdown value (like a pair of eights or nines) that would be difficult to fold out with a single bet. Since I have no showdown value with six-high, checking back is the most conservative play to preserve my stack for better opportunities, as I expect to be called by any pair in this spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 7♥5♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Opponent is in the BB and stacks are deep (~22k vs 18k). Facing only 50 to call into a 150 pot, I prefer to take the initiative and open-raise rather than limp. A raise to 200 (2x BB) seizes initiative, gives fold equity, and keeps the investment small if I’m up against a strong 3-bet. Plan: force weaker hands to fold, play post-flop in position if called (c-bet on favorable flops, check/float or pot-control on wet boards), and fold to large, noncommittal 3-bets. Therefore I will raise to 200 total.

I hold King-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, which is a min-raise.
