K8o is well above the average hand in a heads-up situation and is more than strong enough to defend against a 2x raise. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. I will call to see the flop and play from there.

I have King-high on a paired board (9-T-9). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. This board texture is likely to have missed both of us unless someone has a 9 or a 10. Checking back here allows me to see a free turn card and preserve the showdown value of my King-high without building a pot where I might be forced to fold to a check-raise.

I was the preflop raiser and have position, so I should continuation-bet frequently to leverage initiative. The flop 9d Tc 9h is paired and I hold nothing (7h5s), but the BB’s calling range contains many missed hands (A/K/Q highs, small pairs, some suited connectors) that will fold to pressure. I size to about half-pot to get value from worse hands and fold out equity-heavy but non-made hands while keeping the pot controlled if called. Plan: bet 200 into 400; if I face a big raise I’ll concede and fold, otherwise re-evaluate on the turn.

On a board of 9-T-9, Minni has led out with a half-pot continuation bet of 200. I hold King-high (K8o), which has some showdown value in heads-up play and still holds equity against a wide range of bluffs or lower high-cards. With 3-to-1 pot odds, I am getting a fair price to see a turn card. I have one overcard (the King) that could improve my hand significantly if it hits, and King-high may currently even be the best hand. I will call the bet and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am out of position as the Big Blind on the turn. The board is 9d-Tc-9h-Qh, which is a very coordinated board. My King-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable, especially with the Queen hitting. Leading into the pre-flop and flop aggressor doesn't make much sense here, as I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand and I don't want to bloat the pot with just King-high. I will check to see how Minni acts on this turn card.

I check (0). I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop (200 into 400) and was called; on the Qh turn the BB checked. With 7♥5♠ I have no pair and no real draw (there are only three hearts total including my 7♥ so no flush on the river is possible). Flash has shown a very call‑heavy/passive tendency throughout the session, so a turn bluff would have to be large to credibly fold out Qx/Tx/Ax and would often be called — making the EV of a bluff poor. Checking controls the pot (800), avoids putting a large portion of our deep stack at unnecessary risk versus a calling station or a slow‑played strong hand, and allows us to re‑evaluate the river where we can bluff a better card or fold to aggression. Therefore I check to keep the pot manageable and look for a better spot on the river.

I have King-high on a board of 9-T-9-Q-2. Minni raised pre-flop and bet the flop, but checked the turn. This sequence often indicates a marginal hand that is looking for a cheap showdown or a missed draw. My King-high has some showdown value in a heads-up pot, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking, I hope to see a cheap showdown. If Minni bets, I will have to decide if she is bluffing with a missed draw or betting a marginal pair.

I have nothing (7♥5♠) on a 9♦ T♣ 9♥ Q♥ 2♣ board. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the paired flop and got called, but the villain has been very passive/call‑heavy all night and checked the turn and now the river. Pot is only 800 while stacks are very deep (~21.5k/17.7k), so any river bluff that credibly folds Ax/Kx/Qx/Tx (many of which are in his calling range) would have to be large and is thin EV against a calling station. Given my zero showdown value, low fold equity, and the small pot relative to the stacks, the +EV play is to conserve chips and proceed to showdown rather than risking a large portion of my stack on a bluff. I check to control the pot and avoid an unprofitable bluff; if he had bet into me I would fold to sizeable aggression.

I have 98 of hearts, which is a strong suited connector in a heads-up situation. Being on the button (SB), I want to play this hand aggressively to build the pot and take advantage of my position throughout the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to apply pressure and maintain a balanced opening range.

Heads-up vs a standard SB open to 300. I’m in the big blind with Th3d and a deep stack (21,400). Pot is 400 and I must put in 200 more to call — break-even equity is 200/(400+200) = 33.3%. The button’s open is likely very wide heads-up, so T3o has enough showdown equity and some chance to hit a ten or backdoor draws. Calling is cheap and keeps the pot controllable; 3-betting as a bluff with this raggy hand (no useful blockers, OOP postflop) is unnecessarily risky and folding is too tight in heads-up. Plan: call to 300 total, play the flop cautiously (fold to large action on completely missed boards, continue with top pair/strong draws or use position-dependent floats if appropriate).

I have Th3d on Ks 7h Qh — no pair, only a single heart (runner-runner backdoor flush). I’m out of position and the pot is small relative to deep stacks (21,200 / 18,200). Betting OOP with a junk hand is unnecessary and will overcommit me without a plan. Checking controls the pot and lets Flash reveal his intent: if he c-bets small (≈1/3 pot) I can call to realize equity and see a turn; if he bets large or raises I can fold and preserve the stack. If he checks back I can take a free card or apply a delayed bluff on a harmless turn (or barrel if a heart/ten lands). Check now to keep options and avoid bloating the pot with no made hand.

On a board of Ks-7h-Qh, I have a flush draw with my 9h-8h. Minni has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. This is a very good board for a continuation bet because it favors my perceived range (holding high cards like K or Q) and I have significant equity to improve if called. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to apply pressure and build the pot for my draw.

Call 300. Pot is 900 so I’m getting ~25% pot-equity break-even (300/(900+300)=25%). T♥3♦ has no pair and only weak runner-runner/one-card outs (three remaining Tens, backdoor heart/straight possibilities), so raw equity to improve is limited — but Flash opened wide from the button heads-up and his flop bet is relatively small, so his continuation range will include a lot of air and marginal hands I currently beat. 3-betting OOP with this rag is unnecessary and folding is too nitty given the price and implied odds with deep stacks. Calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity, and lets me fold to strong turn aggression or continue if I pick up a Ten or a favorable card (heart/blank enabling a cheap showdown). Plan: call now, fold to big raise/barrel if I miss and face heavy action, and re-evaluate on the turn if I hit or see a probe bet.

Check. The turn (Q♦) pairs the board and effectively kills my backdoor heart possibilities — I hold T♥3♦ with no pair and only 6 weak outs (three Tens + three Treys ≈ 13% to improve on the river). Pot is 1,200 and stacks are deep (I have ~20,900; Flash ~17,900). Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop, but the paired turn makes his range much stronger (Qx, Kx, sets) and reduces the frequency of bluffs. Leading out OOP with no pair and poor blockers would be a thin bluff that can be check-raised or called by many better hands; that would be a bad spot to get uncomfortable. I want to keep the pot small and realize my modest showdown equity for free — check and fold to meaningful river aggression unless I improve or the bet is trivially small and the river texture justifies a call.

The turn pairs the Queen, which is a significant card that likely improves neither player's hand if they don't already have a Queen. Since I am on a flush draw with 9h8h, checking back here allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river. If I hit my flush, I can go for value; if I miss, I have the option to check down or potentially bluff depending on the river card and Minni's action.

Board: Ks 7h Qh Qd 7c. I hold T♥3♦ — I missed. Pot is 1,200 and Flash (BTN) checked to me on the river. With no pair and very little showdown equity against hands that beat me (Qx, 7x, Kx), a fold or passive check both cede the pot when villain has anything. A tiny river probe is the highest-expected-value line here: the minimum bet is only 100 into 1,200. Even if I had zero chance to win at showdown, I only need ~7.7% fold frequency for this bluff to break even (100 / 1,300 ≈ 7.7%); with any real showdown equity vs parts of his calling range the bet becomes clearly +EV. Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop but checked the turn and river — his checked range will contain a lot of marginal hands and pure air that will fold to a small river probe. Betting 100 maximizes fold equity while keeping the downside tiny relative to stacks. Action: make the minimum river bluff to try to take down the 1,200 pot.

The board is Ks 7h Qh Qd 7c. This means the best possible five-card hand using the board is two pair (Queens and Sevens) with a King kicker. My hand is 9h 8h, which means my best five-card hand is exactly the board: QQ77K. Minni has bet 100 into a 1200 pot. This is a very small bet. Unless Minni holds an Ace (to play QQ77A), a Queen (for a full house), or a Seven (for a full house), we will split this pot. Many of Minni's likely hands, such as various connectors or low pairs that missed, result in a split pot. At 13:1 pot odds, calling is the correct mathematical play to realize a potential split, as the cost is minimal compared to the possibility of reclaiming half the pot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♣5♦ — a playable but marginal hand. In heads-up play I want initiative and to use position, so I’ll open-raise rather than just complete. A standard 3x BB to 300 is the right sizing here: it builds the pot when I have position, puts pressure on Flash’s wide defending range, and keeps my postflop options flexible. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and often fold J5o out of position; if he calls I’ll c-bet selectively and use position to press any advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with AQo, a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has opened for 3x (300). I should definitely 3-bet here for value and to take control of the hand. A standard 3-bet size would be around 900-1000 total.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 after my 300 open. I’m on the button (postflop position) with J♣5♦ — a marginal hand but playable in heads-up given position and deep stacks. Calling the extra 700 (1000 total) gives me ~35% break-even pot equity and preserves stack depth so I can use position to outplay him postflop. 4-betting with J5o is too thin and folding surrenders too much initiative. Plan: call, see the flop, c-bet or apply pressure on favorable textures, and control the pot or fold to strong resistance on dry/unfavourable boards.

On this 8s-6c-4c flop, I have two overcards (Ace and Queen) and a backdoor straight draw. While this board texture can hit the caller's range (Minni), as the pre-flop 3-bettor, I maintain a range advantage. A continuation bet is appropriate here to maintain initiative and potentially fold out some of Minni's air. I'll bet roughly half the pot to keep the pressure on while not overcommitting if I face resistance.

I’ll call 1000. Heads‑up on the button with J♣5♦ on 8♠6♣4♣ I’m in position and the BB’s 1,000 into a 3,000 pot gives ~4:1 pot odds (need ≈20% equity). My hand has roughly 6 clean outs to pair by the river (J or 5 → ~24% to hit by river) plus very small runner‑runner club possibilities, and I can use position to realize equity or induce mistakes on later streets. Raising to the 2,000 minimum is too thin — I have no made hand or reliable draw and would be bloating the pot against a 3‑bettor who will call or shove with many hands that dominate me. Plan: call, see the turn, continue (bet/raise) only if I pair or pick up meaningful club equity or villain gives a clear spot to bluff; otherwise fold to heavy pressure.

After my flop continuation bet was called by Minni, the turn 2h is a relative blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. While I still have overcards, this low, connected board (8-6-4-2) likely connects better with Minni's calling range than my 3-betting range. Betting again would be a high-variance play that might not fold out her pairs. By checking, I control the size of the pot, potentially see a free river, or induce a bluff from her missed draws which I still beat with Ace-high.

Turn summary: I have J♣5♦ on 8♠6♣4♣2♥. The turn (2♥) kills the runner‑runner club possibility — I now have only 6 clean outs (three Jacks, three Fives) ≈ 13% to pair on the river. Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop; that line looks like weakness or pot control rather than a shove/raiseable monster, but he still has many hands that will continue to a river. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff that requires substantial fold equity to make profitable (and vs a 3‑bettor who has shown aggression I don’t trust I have enough). With no made hand and limited equity, pot control is the correct, low‑risk line. I’ll check behind to see the river and preserve stack depth; if I pair the river I can extract value or call reasonable bets, and if the river bricks I’ll consider a targeted river bluff only when the card and villain’s action make it believable — otherwise fold to strong action.

On the river 9c, the board is 8s-6c-4c-2h-9c. The 9c is a significant card as it completes any backdoor club flush draws and doesn't improve my Ace-high. My opponent, Minni, called a pre-flop 3-bet and a flop continuation bet, but checked back on the turn. This sequence suggests she may have a medium-strength hand (like a pair of 8s or 6s) or was on a draw. Since Ace-high is unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, and unlikely to fold out better hands like a pair, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to see a showdown for free or potentially catch a bluff from missed straight draws (like 7-5 or 5-3, though some of those already made straights).

Final board 8♠ 6♣ 4♣ 2♥ 9♣. I hold J♣5♦. Pot is 4,000 and Flash checked the turn and again checked the river after c‑betting the flop. I have basically no showdown value (missed my pair, no flush) so checking behind hands many better showdowns I’d lose. Flash’s line (flop c‑bet then turn/river checks) looks like weakness or pot control with a marginal pair or busted equity — a sizable river bluff is believable (representing a club flush or a made 9). A half‑pot river bet (2,000) requires only ~33% fold frequency to be profitable; I estimate Flash will fold that often given the passive turn/river line. Bet 2,000 to apply pressure and try to take down the 4,000 pot; if he raises I’ll strongly consider folding this airball.

The board is 8s-6c-4c-2h-9c. After I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, the turn checked through. Minni's check-back on the turn often signifies a marginal made hand (like a pair of 8s, 6s, or 4s) or a draw. The river 9c completes a potential backdoor flush and doesn't improve my Ace-high. Minni's bet of 2,000 into a 4,000 pot (making the total pot 6,000) is a polarized sizing. While she could be bluffing with missed straight draws (like 5-3), almost all of her 'marginal' hands that she would have checked on the turn now beat my Ace-high (any pair of 8s, 6s, 4s, 2s, or 9s). Given that I have no showdown value against her checking range and the board texture favors the pre-flop caller's range more than the 3-bettor's on these low cards, folding is the most disciplined play.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with Q5o. While not a premium hand, I have position throughout the hand. Given that the blinds are 50/100 and I have a healthy stack, I will use a standard open-raising strategy to exert pressure and take control of the pot early. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and puts Minni in a position where she must have a hand to continue or defend her big blind out of position.

Heads-up vs Flash (SB) who open-raised to 300 — he opens very wide in this game. I have T6o in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total commit 300). With deep stacks and a wide opening range, T6o has enough equity to see a flop and potentially hit top pair or useful draws. 3-betting to 500 with this marginal hand out of position is unnecessary — it bloats the pot and creates difficult postflop decisions against a player who raises a lot. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and proceed cautiously: continue when I hit tens/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

Check. I’m out of position with T6o on K♣-7♦-8♠ — I completely missed: no pair and no realistic flush. My only clean outs are the four 9s to make a 6-7-8-9-10 straight (≈16% to hit by river); tens/sixes are additional but weak outs since a paired ten/six is often still behind a Kx. Flash is a very wide opener and will c-bet a lot, but OOP versus a wide c-bettor I don’t want to bloat the pot or bluff-raise with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot and lets me fold to meaningful aggression. Plan: if he checks back I get to see a free turn; if he bets small I can sometimes call to realize equities, but I will fold to sizable continuation bets — only continue when I pick up a strong pair, the straight, or a very favorable runout.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of King-high is generally favorable for a raiser's range. Although I only have Queen-high with no draw, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet after Minni's check. By betting 300 (half the pot), I represent a King or a strong pair, putting pressure on Minni's medium-strength hands or air. If she calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, but I expect a fold from many of her missed hands here.

Call 300. Flash is opening and c-betting very wide in this game; the flop bet is 300 into a 600 pre-bet pot (900 total) so calling 300 to make the pot 1,200 requires ≈25% equity. My hand missed but has 4 clean 9s to make a 6-7-8-9-10 straight and three tens + three sixes as additional (dirty) outs — ~10 raw outs (~38% to improve by river), but tens/sixes are often second/third pair vs Kx so realistic equity is nearer 20–30% vs a wide c-betting range. Given the price, I’ll flat-call to realise equity and keep the pot manageable OOP; raising is a poor bluff with no fold equity and folding here would be too nitty given pot odds and opponent tendencies. Plan for the turn: fold to a sizable barrel if I don’t improve, call small turn bets when pot odds justify, and proceed aggressively only if I hit a 9, a ten that looks good, or a very favorable free card/runout.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop — a sign of weakness. I have T6o on K♣-7♦-8♠-7♠: no pair, no realistic backdoor flush, only four 9s to make a straight and weak tens/sixes that are often second/third pair. There's essentially no value to a bet and limited fold equity on a paired board versus a wide, sticky opener. Pot is only 1,200 while both stacks are deep, so I should preserve stack and realize equity cheaply. I’ll check and take a free river to try to hit a 9/10/6; plan to fold to a large river bet unless I improve or get favorable odds to call.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 7s. This card is generally better for the Big Blind's calling range than my opening range, as she is more likely to have 7-x hands. Since my Queen-high has no real equity or draws to improve, and she called the flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of the board or a draw. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size, avoid a check-raise, and see a river card for free. I can then decide whether to bluff or give up depending on the river action.

I have T6o on Kc-7d-8s-7s-Kd — I missed completely (no pair, no meaningful blockers). I checked the turn intending to take a free river and fold to significant aggression; the river paired the king which only improves villain’s value combos (Kx, full/2-pair combos) and reduces my fold equity. A bluff here would need a large fold frequency (≈25%+ for a small 1/3‑pot bet, ≈40% for a 2/3‑pot bet) and I have no blockers to reliably generate that against a wide but sticky opener. With deep stacks and many better spots ahead, the correct line is to preserve chips: check and fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling an absurdly tiny bet if pot odds are extreme). Checking is the low-variance +EV choice.

The board has double-paired with Kings and Sevens (Kc-7d-8s-7s-Kd). My best five-card hand is now Kings and Sevens with a Queen kicker (K-K-7-7-Q). This actually beats any hand with a lower kicker, such as an 8 (which would play as K-K-7-7-8) or any pocket pair below Queens. However, I still lose to any Ace-high, any 7 (full house), or any King (full house). Minni called the flop, which suggests she has some connection to the board, likely an 8, a 7, or a King. By checking the river, she might be waving the white flag with an 8 or lower, or she could be trapping. Since I have some marginal showdown value now that the board has double-paired and I beat an 8, checking back is the most sensible play to minimize risk while potentially winning the pot.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 9s (9c9h). This is a very strong hand in position with deep stacks (22850 vs 17000) and good post‑flop playability (set potential, vulnerable only to overcards). I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and isolate the BB rather than limp. The minimum raise is 200, but a standard 3x BB open to 300 gives good value against Flash’s wide defending range while maintaining fold equity and leaving room to maneuver postflop. Plan: if I get a simple call I’ll c‑bet favorable textures and pot‑control on dangerous boards; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll reassess (often call to see a flop given deep stacks and set value, 4‑bet only vs very light 3‑bets).

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni, who is currently the chip leader, has opened with a standard raise to 300. While the pot odds are 2:1 to call, J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I have a healthy stack of 170 big blinds and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 3h 4c on the button (small blind) in a heads-up situation. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While heads-up play requires a wide range, 3-4 offsuit is at the very bottom of that range. Given the current stack sizes and Minni's activity level, there is no reason to risk chips with 4-high. Folding is the most disciplined play here to wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with K♠T♥ — a very playable hand heads-up with good equity and postflop playability. Pot is 150, to call 50, min-raise is 200. With 230bb effective I want to seize initiative and reduce the BB’s easy odds to play a wide defending range. A standard 3xBB-sized open (300 total) is consistent with my table image and gives good fold equity while keeping many worse hands in. Plan: if Flash calls, play position — c-bet frequently on dry boards, proceed aggressively when I pair or pick up strong draws, and check/control when an Ace or heavy action indicates I’m likely behind. If Flash 3-bets large, KTo is often dominated so I’ll fold to extreme aggression; I may call small-to-medium 3-bets depending on sizing and read. Raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot I can play in position.

I have been dealt a premium hand, Ace-Queen of spades, in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of 16,850 and such a strong hand, I want to build a pot and take control of the hand. Re-raising (3-betting) is standard here to extract value and potentially end the hand pre-flop. A raise to 1,000 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on Minni's opening range while keeping the pot manageable if she calls.

I call to 1000. I’m on the button with K♠T♥ after opening to 300 and getting a 3‑bet to 1000. I’m in position and the stacks are deep, so KTo has good equity and post‑flop maneuverability heads‑up. The pot is ~1300 and I must call 700, so break‑even equity is 700/(1300+700)=35%. Versus a realistic BB 3‑bet range (wide in heads‑up: Ax, Kx, broadways, mid pairs, some connectors) KTo is roughly at or above that threshold and plays very well in position. 4‑betting here is thin — a small 4‑bet won’t fold enough hands and a big shove is an overcommit with a marginal holding — and folding would be too tight given the pot odds. Postflop plan: use position to c‑bet dry boards and realize equity on draws; value‑bet when I pair a K or T; be cautious on Ace‑high or heavily coordinated textures and fold to strong continued aggression if I miss.

On a flop of 7h-8h-Ac, I have flopped top pair with a strong Queen kicker. As the pre-flop 3-better, this board favors my range significantly. I want to bet here for value and to protect my hand against the various heart draws and straight draws (like 9-10 or 6-5) that Minni might have called with. A bet of 1,000 (50% of the pot) is a standard continuation bet sizing that extracts value from weaker Aces and draws while maintaining pot control.

Pot is 3000, Flash bets 1000 — I need 25% equity to justify a call (1000/(3000+1000)=25%). I'm in position with K♠T♥ on 7♥8♥A♣. I currently have no pair (only a single heart and backdoor/runner possibilities) but decent showdown equity vs a wide heads‑up 3‑bet/c‑bet range (which will include a lot of missed broadways, Kx, some pairs and bluffs as well as Ax). With deep stacks and position I can realize extra equity and make correct fold/call decisions on the turn. Raising here is thin — Flash 3‑bet preflop and is betting an Ace‑high flop; a raise would be representing an Ace but will be called by many better hands and build a pot with marginal equity. Folding surrenders too much given the pot odds. Plan: call the 1000 to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression unless I improve (pair K/T or pick up heart/other meaningful outs), and if I do improve take a value/semi‑bluff line accordingly.

On the turn, the Seven of Diamonds pairs the board. My current hand is top pair with an Ace and a Queen kicker. While the Seven is unlikely to have improved Minni's hand given the pre-flop 3-bet and flop action, it is a card that could potentially slow down the action. By checking, I am exercising pot control and providing Minni an opportunity to bluff with her draws (like heart draws or straight draws) or to check behind with a weaker Ace. I am comfortably ahead of most of her calling range from the flop, but checking allows me to evaluate her reaction to the board pairing before committing more chips to the pot.

Board: 7♥ 8♥ A♣ 7♦. I hold K♠ T♥ — no pair but a strong heart draw (I have one heart; two hearts are on board). Pot = 4,000, stacks deep (effective ~14,950). Villain 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop but checked the turn, which reads as either pot‑control with an Ace/pair or a missed/medium holding. I have ~10 clean heart outs to make a flush (~21.7% on the river) and additional non‑heart K/T outs (combined ≈ 30–33% to improve to at least a pair), so I have substantial equity but not a made hand. Betting here is a standard in‑position semi‑bluff: it (a) gets value/folds from many worse hands and unpaired broadways, (b) builds a pot when I hit, and (c) keeps control vs a possible Ace. I size to ~1/2 pot (2,000) — enough to generate fold equity without overcommitting the deep stacks. If called I can barrel the river when I complete; if I miss and face large aggression I can let go.

I have top pair with a very strong kicker (Ace-Queen) on a board of 7-8-A-7. After 3-betting pre-flop and leading the flop, I checked the turn to exercise pot control and provide Minni an opportunity to bluff with her various draws (heart draws or straight draws like 9-10 or 6-5). Minni's bet of 2,000 into a 4,000 pot is a standard sizing for both value and bluffs. While the board pairing the Seven is a potential scare card, it is unlikely to have improved her range significantly given her call of the pre-flop 3-bet. Raising here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (trips or better) and would fold out her bluffs. Calling allows me to keep her bluffing range in and see a river card efficiently.

On the river, the board is 7h-8h-Ac-7d-9s. I have top pair (Aces) with a Queen kicker. The board has paired the Sevens, which reduces the likelihood of my opponent holding a Seven, although it is still possible. After 3-betting pre-flop and leading the flop, I checked the turn to exercise pot control and called Minni's bet of 2,000. Checking the river is the most prudent play here. It allows me to see a showdown cheaply if she checks behind with a weaker Ace or a pocket pair, and it keeps her range wide, potentially inducing a bluff from missed heart draws or straight draws. Betting myself would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like trips or a slow-played straight) and would force her to fold the very bluffs I want her to make.

Flash 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the A♥7♥8♥ flop, then checked the turn and called my 2,000 semi‑bluff. That line is consistent with one‑pair hands (Ax, 7x, 8x), some Kx, and a few two‑heart draws that would have been drawing on the turn. The river 9♠ is a blank for my K♠T♥ — I still only have King‑high. Betting here would be a pure bluff: most worse hands (pure air) that fold are already thin in his range after he called my turn raise, while most hands that beat me (A, any pair of 7/8/9, made straights) will simply call. With pot = 8,000 and deep stacks, there’s little fold equity and minimal value to gain, so the +EV line is to check and go to showdown rather than fire chips into a likely call or a costly bluff fold.

A10o is a very strong hand in heads-up play. As the button (SB), I have a significant range advantage and position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the correct play to build a pot with a premium hand and take the initiative in the hand.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with Kc9d in the big blind. Heads-up this is a defendable hand and I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal 3-bet on deep stacks. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives ~33% immediate pot odds and keeps the range wide; I can continue on favorable flops (top pair, good draws) and fold to extreme aggression on dry/unfavorable textures. Plan: call and evaluate the flop rather than 3-bet or fold.

I called the 3x open preflop to keep my range wide. Flop is 5d Qs 5c and I hold Kc9d — I’ve completely missed and I have no realistic draws (with only one club in my hand and one club on the flop a flush by river is impossible). Flash (the preflop aggressor) checked, which can be pot‑control with Qx or small pairs, or a slow/weak line — but it also means a lot of his range (small pockets, Qx, some overcards) will call a probe. With deep stacks and being out of position, bluffing here is low EV because the paired board connects with many parts of his range and a called bluff puts me in a horrible spot on later streets. I’ll control the pot and see a turn: check and fold to meaningful flop aggression; if checked through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only fire as a bluff on a clearly fold‑inducing card or start extracting value if I pair up (K or 9).

I have a range advantage on this dry, paired board (Q-5-5) as the pre-flop raiser. While Ace-Ten high has some showdown value, a continuation bet of 50% pot (300) allows me to maintain the initiative, represent a Queen or a pocket pair, and potentially fold out hands that have equity against me, such as King-high or smaller Ace-high combinations. If Minni calls, the pot stays manageable, and I can look to realize my showdown value on later streets.

I defended preflop to keep my range wide, then checked the flop as planned. The board (5d Qs 5c) completely misses K9 and heavily connects with Flash’s calling/raising range (Qx, small pockets, some slow-played 5x). He sizes a pot-1/3 bet of 300 into ~900 — calling would require 300 to win 1500, so I need ~20% equity. My actual equity vs his likely value/flatting range is well below that and, being out of position on a paired board, calling or bluff-raising leads to difficult, low-expected-value turns. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also posting the SB) — this spot calls for a very wide opening range. 4h6c is marginal, but in position I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call and play out of position postflop. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to get fold equity, define ranges, and keep the pot size manageable with deep stacks. If Flash calls or 3-bets I can proceed cautiously postflop and use position to realize equity or apply pressure.

In a heads-up situation, J6o is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a standard 3x open. The pot odds of 3:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) make this a standard call to see a flop, despite being out of position.

On a very coordinated board of Ac-Qh-Th, my Jack-six offsuit has a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). This texture strongly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, which likely includes many Aces, high pairs, and broadway cards. I will check to see how Minni reacts, as leading out into this range is generally not profitable without a stronger made hand or a more significant draw.

I opened wide preflop from the button and Flash just checked to me on Ac Th Qh. My hand (4h6c) has no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart — essentially no showdown value. As the preflop aggressor I still have meaningful fold equity, so I want to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. A half-pot c-bet (300 into a 600 pot) credibly represents an Ace/strong broadway or a heart draw, gives me a decent chance to take it down now, and doesn’t commit a large portion of my stack if I’m called. Plan: fold to a sizable check-raise (negligible equity), and if called proceed cautiously — only continue on very favorable turns (real improvement) or switch to pot-control.

On this Ace-Queen-Ten flop, the board texture heavily favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, which includes many Broadway cards, sets, and two-pair combinations. My hand, Jack-six offsuit, has a gutshot straight draw (needing a King), but even if I hit, the board would be extremely coordinated, and Minni could easily hold a King-Jack for a higher straight or at least share the pot. With only about 8% equity to hit my gutshot on the turn and facing a half-pot bet, the pot odds (calling 300 to win a total of 1200, requiring 25% equity) do not justify a call, especially since I am out of position and likely to face further pressure on later streets. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice here.

In heads-up play, holding 3-5 suited on the button is a playable hand. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. Minni has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop pressure in previous hands. If called, I have a hand that can flop draws or low pairs, allowing for post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive button strategy.

Heads-up on the big blind facing a 3x open to 300 (pot 400, I need 200 more). I hold Q♠2♥ — a marginal hand but in heads-up many opens are very wide. Calling 200 into a 600 pot gives me ~33% break-even equity; versus a wide button range Q2o has close to that and can realize equity postflop. I won’t 3-bet with this dominated, poorly‑playable hand. Plan: defend the blind by calling (total committed 300), see the flop, fold to heavy turn/river aggression on dry, unpaired boards, and only pursue the hand if I pair the Q or pick up real equity.

I hold Q♠2♥ on Qc As Qh — that’s trips (QQQ) but with a very weak kicker. Flash opened wide preflop in heads‑up, so his range contains lots of A‑x and some Q‑x/AQ combos. The hands that actually beat me on this flop are full houses (AA, AQ) which are possible but not extremely frequent; many of his A‑hands and worse Q‑hands are behind. I should extract value rather than slow‑play: bet to get called by A‑high and other worse hands while charging any bluffs. I choose a ~2/3 pot bet (400 into 600) — big enough to get value from most Ax and keep weaker Qx paying, but not so huge that only the very best hands call. If Flash raises big, that will heavily indicate a full house and I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to an all‑in or large polarizing shove; call smaller raises to see the turn).

On a flop of Qc-As-Qh, my opponent Minni led into me for 400 after calling my pre-flop raise. This 'donk bet' typically indicates either a strong hand (like a Queen or an Ace) or a speculative hand trying to take control of the pot. Given that I have 5-high with no draws or pair potential on this board, I have zero equity. Continuing would require a high-risk bluff against a range that likely connected with this board. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with pocket Queens (QhQc) — one of the best hands preflop. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to build the pot and isolate the BB while using my positional advantage postflop. A standard heads-up open of ~2.5–3x the big blind gets value from worse hands (AK, AQ, KQ, medium pairs, suited broadways) and denies good odds to speculative holdings. Stacks are deep (~193bb vs ~205bb) so no reason to jam or slowplay — raise for value and to define ranges. I’ll raise to 300 total (top up 250) and then re-evaluate vs a 3‑bet: call small/medium 3‑bets or 4‑bet for value against light 3‑bet sizing; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value.

Ace-five offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up match, especially against a standard 3x button opening. It is well ahead of a typical button's wide range. While I could 3-bet to leverage the Ace blocker, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown a willingness to call 3-bets in the past.

On a low board of 2-7-3 with two hearts, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play here to see how they react to this texture. I have enough equity with an overcard and a straight draw to likely continue against a standard continuation bet, but leading out myself doesn't accomplish much against Minni's wide opening range.

I have QQ (QhQc) — a clean overpair to the 2d-7h-3h flop and I raised preflop, so I have positional advantage. The board has two hearts but I block the Qh which reduces opponent heart combos; there is no made flush/straight yet. Flash’s flatting range heads‑up is wide (Ax, Kx, medium pairs, suited hands), so a c-bet extracts value from worse hands and charges draws. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to get value and protect my hand while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (call small/medium bluffs, fold to a polarized large 3‑bet depending on sizing).

Flash holds Ace-high with an overcard and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 2d-7h-3h. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 600. With a gutshot and an overcard, Flash has enough equity to continue. Folding would be too tight given the wide ranges in heads-up play, and raising as a semi-bluff is an option but calling is a more controlled way to realize equity and see how the turn develops.

I have improved to top pair on the turn with the Ace of Spades. However, the board now features three hearts, which completes potential flushes. Given that I am out of position and my kicker (5) is weak, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts to the Ace. My hand is strong enough to call a reasonable bet, but checking keeps the pot manageable and allows Minni to continue bluffing if she missed.

Turn A♥ changes the texture — it’s an overcard to my QQ and it completes three hearts on board. I was ahead on the flop but the Ace now puts a lot of villain’s calling/flatting range (Ax hands) ahead of me, and anyone holding two hearts has made a flush on the turn. Flash checked the turn, which is ambiguous (pot control with an Ace, missed draws, or a disguised strong hand), so betting here would often bloat the pot when I’m frequently behind and would only get value from very thin/rare hands. I also block some heart combos with Q♥, but not enough to justify turning this into a big pot. With deep stacks and the board now dangerous, I prefer pot control — check and see the river, then re-evaluate based on whether I improve or face pressure.

Flash has top pair with a weak kicker (5). The board texture is somewhat dangerous with three hearts appearing on the turn, but Minni's check behind on the turn suggests she likely does not have a flush or a high-kicker Ace. The river deuce pairs the board, which is generally a safe card. Checking the river is the optimal play here to act as a bluff-catcher; it induces bluffs from Minni's missed draws (like hearts or a straight draw) and allows Flash to reach showdown cheaply if Minni is holding a marginal hand like a 7 or a mid-pocket pair.

Board is 2♦-7♥-3♥-A♥-2♣; I hold Q♥Q♣. I c-bet the flop and was called, but the turn A♥ is a damaging overcard that brings many Ax hands and completes heart draws. Flash’s passive line (call flop, check turn, check river) is ambiguous — could be pot-control with an Ace or a missed draw — but it keeps a lot of better hands in his range. The river pair (second deuce) actually increases the number of hands that now beat me (any deuce for trips, pocket 3s/7s or AA for full houses) while only a narrow subset of worse hands (Kx, low pairs) will call a river bet. I also block some heart combos with Q♥ which reduces, but doesn’t eliminate, the chance of a villain flush. Given deep stacks and the high likelihood I’m often behind if called, I prefer pot control and take showdown by checking rather than bloating the pot or opening myself to a check-raise.

In heads-up play, holding a suited hand like 9c 2c on the button provides enough equity and playability to justify an open-raise. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the small blind to maintain pressure and take control of the pot early. Minni has been defending her big blind, but 92s is strong enough to play against her range, especially with the positional advantage.

Heads-up vs a 3x button open to 300. I have 5♣6♦ in the big blind — a marginal but defensible holding heads-up with good straight/runner potential and decent equity against a wide opening range. Pot = 400; call 200 more (total committed 300) makes the pot 600, so I’m getting 3:1 (need ~25% equity). With deep stacks (~185 BB) the implied odds are attractive and I can realize equity postflop. A 3-bet here would be a thin bluff and unnecessarily bloats the pot out of position; folding is too tight. Plan: call preflop (put in 200 more, total 300), see the flop, play carefully OOP — c‑bet only when I connect or have a strong draw, fold to heavy resistance when missed.

Flop: 9♠ 5♠ 8♠. I hold 5♣6♦ — middle pair (pair of 5s) with a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs). There are three spades on board so many of the opponent’s calling/raising range contains spades and strong draws. Pot is 600 and Flash checked to me. I connected with the flop and should protect/extract value rather than give free cards to spade draws or unpaired broadways. Betting denies equity to flush and overcard draws and gets value from worse pairs, one-pair hands, and calls from spade draws that pay to chase. A half-pot sizing is efficient here: it charges draws, keeps worse hands in, and gives good fold equity against pure floats. If I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a strong check-raise representing a made flush). Action: bet ~50% pot (300).

I have top pair with 9c 2c on a 9s 5s 8s board. While top pair is usually a strong hand in heads-up play, the monotone spade board makes it extremely vulnerable, especially since I do not hold a spade myself. Minni's lead (donk bet) of 300 into the 600 pot could represent a made flush, a strong draw, or a pair with a spade redraw. However, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, which is sufficient to see a turn card. If the turn is a spade or a card that completes straight draws (like a 6, 7, 10, or J), I will likely have to fold to further aggression. For now, calling is the most prudent way to realize my equity without overcommitting.

Turn (T♦) is a blank for my hand — I have a vulnerable one-pair (5s) OOP on a three-spade flop. On the flop Flash called my half‑pot bet, and with three spades already showing any spade in his hand was a made flush on the flop. His calling range therefore contains a lot of hands that already beat me (many flushes) plus some 9/8/T combos and sets; the Ten of diamonds mostly helps those stronger parts of his range or gives him additional pair combos. Betting again here would largely get called by hands that dominate me and would only fold out a few overcards — poor risk/reward OOP. I’ll check for pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: check and see his action; call small probing bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 400) to reach showdown, fold to larger, committed bets that represent a strong made hand (>= ~1/2 pot).

On the turn Td, the board has become significantly more dangerous. Not only are there three spades on board, but the Ten now outranks my pair of Nines, and several straights (7-6, Q-J, J-7) have potentially completed. Minni's check after donking the flop suggests she might be slowing down with a medium-strength hand (like a Nine with a spade) or gave up on a pure bluff. My 9c 2c has some showdown value but cannot realistically bet for value, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and reach a cheap showdown or reassess on the river.

The river 7♣ actually completes my hand: with 5♣6♦ I now have a 5-6-7-8-9 straight. Villain opened wide preflop, called my half‑pot flop bet, then checked the turn and checked the river — a passive/calling line that contains many one‑pair and medium hands plus some two‑spade combos (flopped flushes) and occasional made straights. Checking behind would lose value to the many worse pairs/floaters that will call a river bet. I should therefore bet for value. I size to ~2/3 pot (800) to extract calls from top pairs and weaker holdings while being large enough to charge and deny equity to marginal hands; if Flash raises big I’ll re‑evaluate since raises are often the flush/higher‑straight combos that beat me.

On the river, the board is 9s-5s-8s-Td-7c. My pair of Nines, which was top pair on the flop, has become significantly devalued. The runout has completed several possible straights (any 6 or any Jack) and a flush (any spade). Additionally, the Ten on the turn provides a higher pair than my Nines. Minni led the flop, checked a scary turn, and is now betting 800 into 1200 on the river. This betting pattern and the highly coordinated board make it very likely that my hand is beaten by a better pair, a straight, or a flush. Calling with one of the lowest possible pairs on this board against a 2/3 pot bet is not profitable in the long run.

I’m on the button (small blind) with Ad4h heads-up. This is a standard open-raise spot — A4o has an Ace blocker and reasonable postflop playability in position. Limping hands here surrenders initiative and gives the BB cheap odds; a raise sizes the pot, folds out weaker hands, and keeps the initiative. I’ll raise to the common 3x sizing (300) to apply pressure; plan is to c-bet favorable flops and fold to a large 3-bet if Flash shows significant aggression.

In a heads-up situation, I'm holding 9c 4c in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2:1 odds. Given the suited nature of my hand and the pot odds, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop and evaluate from there.

I have missed the flop completely with my 9-high. While I have a backdoor club flush draw and some very marginal backdoor straight possibilities, I am first to act against the pre-flop raiser. The standard and most prudent play here is to check and see how Minni responds. If she bets, I will likely fold given the lack of direct equity.

I raised preflop with Ad4h and see a dry, low flop 2d-3c-Jh. I have an Ace blocker and only a backdoor diamond draw, so I don’t want to build a huge bloated pot with just A‑high. Standard continuation bet in position is correct: a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) applies pressure to folds (many BB calling ranges miss this board) while keeping the price low if I get called. If Flash folds I take it down; if called I can realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn (continue only with improvement or good turn action). If Flash raises large I’ll give up my A‑high rather than get committed out of position.

I have completely missed this flop with nine-high and no significant draws. The pot is 800 and it costs 200 to call, but I have almost no equity against any of Minni's continuation betting range. Given that I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw (other than a weak backdoor one), I should not be putting more chips into the pot. A fold is the correct decision here.

In a heads-up situation with only 15 hands remaining, maintaining aggression from the button (SB) is key. Queen-five offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position to exert pressure on the big blind and take advantage of position post-flop. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I have QQ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (3x). Heads-up QQ is a very strong hand — ahead of most of Flash’s opening range (aside from AA/KK, maybe AK). I’m out of position postflop, so I want to take the initiative preflop, get value, and fold out speculative hands. A 3-bet to 1,000 (~3.3x the open) is a value/pressure size: large enough to charge Ax, Kx and medium pairs and to gain fold equity, but not so huge that I overcommit if he 4-bets. If he calls I’ll navigate a sizable pot OOP; if he 4-bets very large I’ll re-evaluate (respect a polarized shove from AA/KK/AK), and if it’s a smaller 4-bet I can consider calling. Overall 3-betting here maximizes EV against a wide opening range.

Queen-five offsuit is a weak hand to continue with after facing a 3-bet. Minni's 3-bet to 1000 is a significant increase, and Q5o is likely dominated by her continuing range. Although I have position post-flop, the hand lacks the equity and playability to justify calling 700 more in a bloated pot. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my chip lead with only 15 hands remaining.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 5s2s. Against a single opponent I should open a very wide range — 5-2s is weak but suited and has some postflop playability. Limping (calling 50 more) is too passive: a raise takes the initiative, denies the BB correct odds to see flops cheaply, and lets me use position to c-bet or fold small to resistance. I’ll make a standard min-open to 200 total (add 150 more) and re-evaluate on the flop or to a 3-bet.

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit (Ah, 9d) in the Big Blind during heads-up play. Minni has opened from the button with a min-raise to 200. A9o is a very strong hand in a heads-up context, significantly ahead of a standard button opening range. While calling is an option to keep the pot controlled, I prefer a 3-bet here to take initiative and extract value from weaker Ax, Kx, and Broadway hands she might call with, or to end the hand immediately. I will raise to 700 chips.

I opened wide from the button with 5♠2♠, but Flash 3‑bet to 700. The pot is 900 and I must call 500 more (calling would make the pot 1400), so I need ≈35.7% equity to justify a call. 5‑2s is very weak vs a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, A‑x and broadways dominate my hand); even though I have position, my equity is likely well below the required threshold and post‑flop playability is poor. A 4‑bet bluff to the min (1200) is too spewy with this garbage and won’t fold enough hands to be +EV, while a larger shove is unnecessary and risky given deep stacks. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in heads-up play, especially when on the button. It is well ahead of a typical big blind defending range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on Minni, consistent with my previous aggressive play from the button.

Heads-up vs a frequent opener who raised to 300. I’m in the big blind with 5♥9♠ — a weak but playable hand heads-up. Calling 200 (total commitment 300 including my BB) gives ~3:1 pot odds and lets me see a flop versus a likely wide opening range. With deep stacks I don’t want to 3-bet light with 9-5o; calling preserves stack, realizes equity, and keeps weaker hands in the pot. Plan: see the flop and evaluate — continue aggressively if I pair or pick up good draws, fold to heavy action on dry boards where I miss.

Heads-up on a K♠ Q♣ 7♠ flop with 9♠5♥ — I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw (need runner-runner spades). Flash is a frequent opener who raised preflop but checked the flop, which signals a lot of missed hands and gives me an opportunity to take the pot now. Pot is 600; a half-pot probe (300) applies pressure to his wide checking range (overcards, small pairs, air) while keeping the commitment manageable if he responds strongly. Plan: bet 300 to pick up the pot often; if he raises I will fold (I have almost no showdown equity), if he calls I will re-evaluate on the turn for pair/draw or barrel as a bluff catcher only with improving cards.

On a flop of Ks-7s-Qc, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot. My Ace of Spades gives me the nut backdoor flush draw and I still have Ace-high, which holds some showdown value in heads-up play. While I am likely behind a King or a Queen, a fold here would be too tight given the wide ranges in a 1v1 scenario and the fact that I have position. Raising is an option, but calling allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially improve or evaluate Minni's aggression on the next street. I will call the 300.

I c-bet the flop as a probe and Flash called, which usually shows some real showdown value (Kx/Qx/small pair or a draw). The turn 3♥ is a blank for my 9♠5♥ — I have no pair and only a remote runner-runner flush possibility (~4% to make a flush by the river). With deep stacks and an opponent who willingly called the flop, barreling again without improving or strong blockers is too risky. I’ll check to control the pot, avoid committing chips with near-zero equity, and plan to fold to sizeable turn/raise or re-evaluate on the river if checked to (look for a favorable bluff-card or a cheap showdown).

On the turn 3h, which is a complete brick, Minni has checked after leading on the flop. My Ace-high (As 9d) still holds some showdown value against her draws and smaller pairs, and I have the nut flush draw blocker (As). By checking back here, I control the size of the pot and get to see the river for free. If she was bluffing the flop or has a weak made hand like a 7, checking back allows me to potentially win at showdown or evaluate her action on the river without committing more chips into a board that favors her flatting and donking range.

The river pairs the King (Ks ... Kh) and I hold 9♠5♥ — I have no pair and essentially no showdown equity. Flash called my flop probe and both players checked the turn; his check‑check line is consistent with Qx, a small pocket pair, or missed spade draws. The Kh on the river actually helps many of his plausible hands (and if he held a King he could easily have checked as a trap), and I hold no meaningful blockers to make a believable bluff. Pot is small (1,200) relative to stacks, so risking chips with a thin river bluff here is unnecessary. I'll check and take the free showdown; if Flash now leads out I will fold to any sizeable value/raise because my equity is effectively zero.

On the river, the board has paired the King (Kh), and Minni has checked for the second street in a row after leading on the flop. My Ace-high (As 9d) holds significant showdown value in a heads-up match, especially given the check-check action on the turn and river. I am beating all of her missed draws (spades, straight draws) and potentially lower Ace-high hands. While I could attempt to bluff by representing the King, it is safer to take the free showdown and see if my Ace-high is good, as betting doesn't clearly fold out better hands like a 7 or a weak Queen, nor does it get called by worse hands.

Heads-up on the button with 3♥9♥ (suited). In heads-up I should be opening a very wide range — raising is better than just calling the extra 50 because it takes the initiative, gains fold equity, and lets me play the rest of the hand in position. 3♥9♥ has flush/backdoor straight potential and is perfectly acceptable as an open in this spot. Stack is deep (~190 BB) so we can realize equity post-flop if called. I’ll make a standard-sized open to avoid giving the BB too-good odds to defend (use ~3x BB here). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or pot-control depending on sizing); if he calls, I’ll play post-flop in position.

AQo is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni opened to 300 from the small blind. I will 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and take the initiative in the hand. This size is consistent with my recent 3-betting history and puts pressure on her opening range while building a pot for my strong holding.

I opened wide from the button with 3♥9♥ and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. Stacks are very deep (~187 BB) so calling 700 (total committed 1000) is a small price to see a flop in position. 3♥9♥ has suited/connected playability and decent implied odds heads‑up; 4‑betting as a bluff here is too thin and folding is too tight. Pot odds: 700 into a ~1300 pot (2000 after call) requires ~35% equity — plus I get to use position post‑flop. Plan: call to 1000, evaluate flop (press with hearts or strong connectivity, fold or pot‑control vs heavy aggression on dry boards).

On a dry board of 4d-Tc-7h, I still maintain a range advantage as the pre-flop 3-better. While I haven't paired up, Ace-Queen is often the best hand or has significant equity against Minni's calling range. I will place a continuation bet of 800 (40% of the pot) to exert pressure on her likely holdings, such as small pocket pairs or suited connectors that missed this flop, while also building a pot for if I improve on the turn.

I raised a wide range preflop and called Flash’s 3‑bet to see a flop in position. On 4♦ T♣ 7♥ I hold 9♥3♥ — no pair but two hearts give me backdoor flush potential and decent equity to hit a pair by the river. Flash’s bet is small (800 into ~2,000) and is consistent with a high-frequency c‑bet; raising here is a timed semi‑bluff: it gets folds from unpaired overcards and some medium holdings, gains initiative in position, and turns my hand into a bluff with real running outs. I’ll make the minimum raise to 1,600 (an extra 800) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. If he jams big, I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn—bet/raise when I pick up meaningful equity (a heart or a pair) and control/block when I miss.

Minni has min-raised my continuation bet on a relatively dry board. I am getting excellent pot odds (5.5:1) to call with Ace-high. Folding would be much too tight given the price and my potential equity if I hit an Ace or Queen. I will call the 800 more and reassess on the turn, keeping the pot manageable for now.

The Queen of Diamonds on the turn is an excellent card for me, as it gives me top pair with the best kicker (Top Pair, Top Kicker). On the flop, Minni min-raised my continuation bet, which could indicate a Ten, a set (77, 44), or a semi-bluff/draw like 98 or J9. Since Minni became the aggressor on the flop, I will check to her on the turn to see how she reacts to the overcard. This allows me to control the size of the pot, induce a continuation of a bluff, or trap a weaker hand that might bet for value. I am not folding this hand to a single bet on this turn.

I raised the flop as a semi‑bluff but the turn (Qd) completely bricks my backdoor heart — with only one card to come I can no longer make the flush, leaving me with just 9‑high. Flash called my flop raise and then checked the turn; the Qd actually helps his calling range (Qx, broadways, T/7, pocket pairs) more than mine, so a second barrel is thin and will often be called by better hands. With deep stacks and no real equity, I should control the pot and preserve chips. I’ll check behind to see the river and only continue if I pick up real equity (a pair or a very favorable runout) or get a clear bluffing opportunity.

On the river, the board is 4d-Tc-7h-Qd-Jd. I hold top pair with the best kicker (Ace-Queen). After Minni min-raised me on the flop and then checked back the turn, her range is most likely capped at a medium-strength hand like a Ten (KT, AT) or a missed draw (98). The Jack on the river completes a potential straight (J9), but given her check on the turn, it's less likely she was sitting on a monster. I will check to induce a bluff from her missed straight draws or to get to a cheap showdown with my top pair. If she checks behind, my Queens are very likely the best hand. If she bets, I will likely call a standard-sized value bet given the strength of my holding and the pot odds.

I have 9♥3♥ and missed the turn and river — no pair and no flush. I raised the flop as a semi‑bluff but the Q on the turn killed my backdoor heart outs; I never improved. Opponent 3‑bet pre and called my flop raise, then played very passively on the turn and river — a line consistent with Tx, 7x, Qx or medium pocket pairs that will often call a river bluff. I have poor blockers to diamonds (the board has three diamonds) so representing a believable diamond flush is weak and a river bluff is likely to be called too often. With the pot at 5,200 and deep stacks, betting here is low EV; best to preserve stack and check to showdown.

I am on the button with a strong suited connector, 8d 9d. In a heads-up situation, this hand is well above the average starting hand strength. I want to raise to build the pot and take the initiative, which has been a successful strategy throughout the tournament. A standard raise to 300 has been my go-to sizing and should either take the blinds or set up a manageable pot for post-flop play.

Heads-up, villain (Flash) on the button/SB opened to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total with my 100 BB). I have Q♠4♠ — a suited queen with a useful queen-blocker and decent multi-street playability versus a wide SB opening range. Pot odds on a call are excellent: risking 200 to win ~600 (about 25% equity required). A small 3-bet is feasible, but OOP with a marginal hand and deep stacks makes a 3-bet lower EV here (villain will call a lot and outplay me postflop). I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

Check. I have bottom pair (4s) with a decent queen blocker and backdoor spade possibilities, but I’m behind any Ten, Seven, or higher pocket pair and don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand. I called preflop to keep the pot controllable; on this relatively dry 4♦7♠10♦ board I’ll check to realize equity and let the button (preflop aggressor) fire barrels with bluffs/draws. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (~≤1/2 pot) to extract value from worse and beat bluffs, fold to very large overbets/shoves, and if checked to on later streets consider a turn stab or a delayed value bet if the situation improves. This approach preserves stack and keeps options open.

I have flopped a massive combo draw with an open-ended straight draw (8-9-7-T) and a flush draw (8d-9d on 4d-7s-Td). With approximately 15 outs to a very strong hand, I have significant equity. Since Minni checked to me, I will lead out with a continuation bet to build the pot and take the initiative. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a standard sizing that applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I need to see a turn card.

Flash (button/SB) is opening wide and has c-bet small (400 into a 1000 pot ≈40%). I have Q♠4♠ — bottom pair with a decent queen blocker and backdoor spade equity. The bet gives me ~28–29% pot odds (call 400 to win ~1000), so a call is +EV to realize equity and to get value from Ax/Kx/KQ-type hands and pure bluffs. Raising OOP with a marginal bottom pair is ill-advised — villain will call a raise too often and outplay me postflop; folding here surrenders too much equity given the price. Plan: call the 400 (I’ve already committed 300 preflop so the total I’ll have in the pot after calling is 700), see the turn, and proceed cautiously: insist on improvement (4/Q/spade) to get aggressive or fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

I have bottom pair (4♠) with a queen kicker on 4♦ 7♠ T♦ 3♣. Flash opened preflop and c-bet the flop; he now checked the turn. I didn’t improve on the turn and betting OOP with a marginal pair will bloat the pot and get called by most hands that beat me (T/7/overpairs) while only folding out some overcards. With deep stacks and a reasonable pot (1,400) I prefer pot control and to realize equity — check back, see the river, and only get aggressive if I improve (Q or 4) or if I catch clear weakness. If Flash fires a small river bet I’ll consider a call; I’ll fold to large river aggression.

I have a massive combo draw with 15 outs (flush draw and open-ended straight draw). Minni has checked twice, suggesting she likely has a marginal pair or a draw herself. By betting the turn, I continue to apply pressure as the aggressor. A bet here can win the pot immediately if she folds her weaker holdings, and even if she calls, I have significant equity to hit a very strong hand on the river. A sizing of 800 into 1400 is consistent with my previous aggression and puts her marginal hands in a difficult spot.

Board: 4♦ 7♠ T♦ 3♣. I hold Q♠4♠ — bottom pair with a decent queen blocker but very few clean river outs. Pot is 2200 and it costs 800 to call (break‑even equity ≈ 800 / (2200+800) ≈ 26.7%). With one card to come my only realistic improvements are: any Q (3 outs) or one of the two remaining 4s (2 outs) = 5 outs ≈ 10.9% equity. That is well below the required ~27% to profitably call. Flash’s line (preflop open, flop continuation, turn aggression) is weighted toward value (tens, sevens, overpairs, maybe sets) and he’s deep enough to get paid off — I’m rarely ahead. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair is poor strategy (I’d face calls from better hands and be dominated). Given the pot odds, my poor improvement chances, and villain’s likely value-heavy range, folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a wide range and taking the initiative. Q5o is marginal but fine to raise in position — it gains fold equity, keeps me in control post‑flop, and I can maneuver with a deep stack. Calling 50 is passive and gives the BB cheap chance to realize equity; a standard 2x BB open to 200 is the right balance of pressure and pot control. If Flash 3-bets I will reassess (likely fold vs a large 3-bet, or continue cautiously vs a small squeeze).

I am currently the chip leader with 24,150 chips against Minni's 15,550 with 10 hands remaining in the tournament. I am holding 6d 8d in the Big Blind. Minni has made a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot that will be 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. 86s is a solid hand to defend in a heads-up situation, especially with such favorable odds. I will call and look to see a flop, maintaining my stack advantage while not folding away equity unnecessarily.

I have a strong semi-bluffing hand with both a flush draw (6d, 8d, Td, 5d) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete 5-6-7-8-9). In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I will check here to see Minni's reaction to this wet board. Given my significant equity with the draws, I am prepared to call a bet or even check-raise if the sizing is appropriate, but checking first is the most flexible play to gauge her hand strength.

I opened preflop and the flop gave me a made pair (5s) on 9h–Td–5d. Heads-up this is a hand worth continuing with initiative: it has value vs worse pairs and will deny cheap cards to flush/straight draws. I’ll bet for value and protection — roughly half the pot is the right balance here. Bet 200 into 400 to charge diamonds and weaker hands; if Flash check-raises large I’ll heavily favor folding this marginal pair, but if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed depending on the card.

I have a very strong drawing hand with both a flush draw (6d 8d with Td 5d on board) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete 5-6-x-8-9). Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot, giving me excellent pot odds of 3:1. With approximately 45-50% equity against most of her range, I have an easy call here. While a check-raise is a strong option with this much equity, calling allows me to see the turn cheaply and realize my equity while maintaining my significant chip lead with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament. I will call the 200 and evaluate on the turn.

I am the first to act on the turn. The board is 9h-Td-5d-Ac. I currently hold 6d-8d, giving me a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 outs total). The Ace is a significant card that likely hits Minni's pre-flop raising range. I will check to see how Minni responds to the Ace. My draw is strong enough to call a standard bet to see the river, but leading out here into an Ace could be risky. Given my substantial chip lead and the few hands remaining in the tournament, I want to minimize unnecessary risk while still having the potential to win a large pot if my draw hits.

Pot is 800 and my stack is deep (~15k) but the turn Ac is a major scare card that vastly improves the BB’s calling range (any Ax, A9/AT, some two-pair/sets, and the occasional float). I only have second pair (5s) with a weak Q kicker — a vulnerable hand that doesn’t want to build the pot facing an opponent who called a flop c-bet and then checked the turn. Betting here would commit more with little protection (and could fold out worse hands) while exposing me to a check-raise I should fold to. Optimal line is pot control: check the turn, see a river for free, and then evaluate — call small river bets for pot odds as a bluff-catcher and fold to significant river pressure. Therefore I check and keep the pot manageable.

I have completed a Jack-high flush on the river (Td, Jd, 8d, 6d, 5d). In a heads-up match with 10 hands remaining and a significant chip lead, this is a very strong hand. Minni's check on the turn (Ac) suggests she may not have a top-tier hand or was playing cautiously. I will lead out with a value bet of 500 into the 800 pot to extract value from her marginal hands, such as an Ace, a straight, or two-pair, while remaining wary of a potential raise from a higher flush.

I call 500. I have Qd5s — second pair (5s) with a decent queen kicker. Line: I opened, c‑bet the flop and he called; I checked the Ace on the turn to control the pot and he checked back; on the river he fires a small/half‑pot probe (500). That sizing and the turn check are consistent with a mixed range: thin value (Ax, AT/AJ, Jx/Tx) plus bluffs (missed two‑card diamond draws, floats). Pot math: calling 500 to win ~1800 requires ~27.8% equity, and I expect my hand to have at least that vs a river‑betting range that contains a meaningful bluff frequency. Raising would commit chips with a marginal hand and be inverse‑polarized vs his likely value-heavy combos, while folding surrenders too much when the price is reasonable. So I take the cheap showdown and call.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial lead over Minni, my goal is to maintain pressure and play fundamentally sound poker. JTo is well within the top range of hands to raise from the button. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing and is appropriate here to build the pot while in position.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open, I’ll defend a wide range. T♥6♠ is marginal but has decent equity and playability against a wide button opening range, especially with deep stacks (147 BB). Calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds and lets me see a flop with potential to hit a ten, pair/some straights, or draws. A 3-bet with this weak kicker would bloat the pot out of position and is unnecessary; folding is too tight heads-up. Plan: call, evaluate flop and proceed cautiously OOP (continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure when completely missed).

I’m OOP (BB) with T♠6♥ on K♠ K♣ A♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw and I don’t block A/K. Flash was the pre‑flop raiser and checked the flop; that check is ambiguous (could be weakness or a slowplay). With deep stacks and essentially zero equity, a bluff c‑bet here is high variance — he’ll call with many hands and can check‑raise polar (very strong) or call with A/K and beat me. Best, low‑risk line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I get a free turn card and can re‑evaluate; if he bets I’ll release unless it’s a tiny probe I can confidently call.

The flop of Ks-Kc-Ah is extremely favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range, especially in a heads-up situation where I raised from the button. I have a significant range advantage here, as my range contains all combinations of AA, AK, AQ, and AJ, which Minni likely would have 3-bet pre-flop. Since Minni checked, I will place a standard continuation bet of 1/3 the pot. This size is efficient for forcing folds from her 'air' hands and pocket pairs lower than an Ace, while keeping the pot manageable if she does happen to have a King or an Ace.

I have T♠6♥ on a Ks Kc Ah flop — no pair, no real draw and I don’t block A/K. Flash checked then made a very small continuation bet (200 into an ~800 pot). That sizing gives me very good pot odds: calling 200 to potentially win ~1200 requires only ~16.7% equity. My chance to hit a pair by the river (6 outs) is roughly 24%, so a flat call to realize equity and keep the pot under control is the best line. Raising would be reckless out of position with no showdown value; folding to such a small probe is too tight. Plan: call now (total committed 500), see the turn, and fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I improve (pair or a clear draw), otherwise re-evaluate if board/runout changes the story.

Board is K♠ K♣ A♥ 3♥ and I hold T♠6♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Villain opened pre and took a small stab on the flop that I called; that line is consistent with Kx/Ax/value hands and occasional floats. From the turn I only have 6 outs (three tens + three sixes) — ~13% to hit the river — and I don’t block A or K, so bluffing or raising out of position would bloat the pot with virtually no showdown value. Best play is pot control: check and see the river. If villain barrels a sizable bet on the river I will fold; I’ll only call small river probes or continue if I actually pair up.

On a board of Ks-Kc-Ah, my pre-flop range as the button raiser is very strong, but Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a weak Ace or a pocket pair. The turn 3h is a blank. Since my Jack-high has no real draw and limited showdown value, I will check behind to control the pot size and see the river for free. With a substantial lead and few hands remaining in the tournament, minimizing unnecessary risk is the priority.

Check. On K♠ K♣ A♥ 3♥ Q♠ with T♠6♥ I have no pair or meaningful showdown equity and didn’t improve on the river. Villain opened and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn — a line consistent with Kx/Ax/value hands and some floats. Bluffing out of position here is negative‑EV: any bet needs substantial fold equity (fold rate > b/(b+1000)) which is unlikely versus this opponent who has been calling down. Best play is pot‑control — check and fold to any sizable river betting, only consider calling very small probes.

On the river, the board is Ks-Kc-Ah-3h-Qs. My hole cards are Td-Jh, which completes a Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T). This is an exceptionally strong hand, although the paired Kings on the board mean I must be wary of full houses (like AA, QQ, AK, or KQ). However, Minni has checked on every street, including the river. If she held a King or a full house, she would most likely have bet the river for value or attempted a check-raise earlier. Her passive line strongly suggests a weak Ace, a Queen, or a missed hand. Since I have the best hand almost all of the time here, I will place a value bet of 500 (half the pot) to entice a call from an Ace or a Queen. Given my significant chip lead and the few hands remaining, this maximizes value while keeping risk relatively low.

Pot is 1500 and I’m facing a 500 river bet (need ~25% equity to call). I hold T♠6♥ on K♠ K♣ A♥ 3♥ Q♠ — I missed completely and have essentially zero showdown equity except vs pure bluffs. Opponent raised pre, made a small flop c-bet, checked the turn and now fires a small river probe; that line is weighted toward value (Kx/trips or A+K/strong Ax) and Flash has shown a tendency to call down and small‑value bet in this match. Calling requires Villain to be bluffing a large portion (>25%) of the time, which is unlikely here; raising as a bluff OOP with no equity is too risky. I’ll fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

Heads‑up on the button with 8♥5♦ — marginal but perfectly playable here. In heads‑up play I want initiative and to pressure the big blind, but I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak, offsuit hand. I’ll open‑raise to the minimum allowed (200 total). That gives fold equity and keeps pot size controlled. Plan: fold to a substantial 3‑bet/shove; if Flash just calls I’ll c‑bet favorable, dry boards to take it down and play cautiously (check/control or fold) when faced with heavy resistance or overcard‑heavy textures.

Nine-three offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am getting favorable pot odds (3:1) to see a flop, this hand realization is extremely low and it is often dominated. Given that I have a substantial chip lead and we are very close to the end of the tournament (Hand 143 of 150), there is no need to play marginal or sub-marginal hands out of position. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip advantage.

I am on the button with Jack-Four of spades, which is a playable hand in heads-up play, especially with a significant chip lead. I have been consistently raising to 300 in this position to maintain pressure on Minni. Given the tournament is nearing its end and I want to leverage my stack advantage, I will continue with a standard opening raise.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB while I’m in the big blind with K♦T♣. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (so I’d have 300 total committed).
