Calling 200 to win a 600 pot gives ~33% breakeven equity; KTo has good equity vs a wide button open in heads-up, so defending is +EV. A min 3‑bet to 500 is too small to gain meaningful fold equity and a larger 3‑bet would commit more OOP than I want with a marginal hand. I’ll call to realize equity and play the flop cautiously OOP—look to check/call vs standard c-bets, and get aggressive only when I connect or get the right read.

I defended preflop with K♦T♣ but the flop A♦ A♠ 8♥ heavily favors the opener’s range (lots of Ax and strong pairs). I have no pair and no meaningful draw, so leading or bluff‑raising out of position is a thin play into a range that dominates me. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack. Plan: if Flash fires a small c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot) I may call to realize equity and try to improve on the turn; I will fold to larger bets unless I pair up or see a clear bluff line. If Flash checks back I get a free turn and can re‑evaluate.

On a very dry, paired board like Ace-Ace-Eight, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage. My Jack-high has some showdown value against her total air, but more importantly, a continuation bet here will likely force folds from most of Minni's non-pair hands. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain pressure and exploit the board texture.

Call 300. Flash's small 1/3-pot c-bet (300 into 900) gives me 25% pot odds to continue (300 to win 1200). I have K♦T♣ with no pair on A♦ A♠ 8♥, so I'm behind any Ace and many value hands, but the c-bet size is consistent with a wide continuation-betting range that includes a lot of non-A hands and bluffs. Raising OOP with KTo is thin and would commit too much against a likely Ace-heavy responding range; folding here surrenders too much equity for the price. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable — plan is to fold to a sizable flop raise or heavy turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a meaningful draw. If I pair a King or Ten on the turn I can start extracting value; if the turn bricks and Flash barrels again big, I’ll fold.

Board: A♦ A♠ 8♥ 2♥. I hold K♦ T♣ and there is no bet to me on the turn (pot 1,200). I have no pair and no meaningful draw — only clean outs are three kings and three tens, and even if I pair I can still lose to any Ace. Flash checked the turn after a small flop c‑bet, which often indicates a wide/marginal range or a check‑behind line rather than a strong slowplay that wants to build the pot. Betting here would be a thin bluff that builds a pot I don’t want to play OOP with essentially no equity; checking keeps the pot under control and preserves my ability to bluff or fold on the river depending on the card and action. Plan: check the turn, see the river — if Flash bets big on the river I’ll fold unless I improve; if they check back I can consider a river bluff if the card is favorable.

After Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, it is likely she has a piece of the board, such as an eight or a pocket pair, or is potentially slow-playing an Ace. The turn card (Deuce of hearts) is relatively blank and doesn't change the board texture significantly. My Jack-high has very little showdown value, but since she showed resistance on the flop, a second barrel is less likely to succeed unless I want to commit to a large multi-street bluff. I will check behind to control the pot size and see a free river card, maintaining my substantial chip lead as the tournament nears its conclusion.

Board: Ad As 8h 2h 3h. I hold K♦T♣ — no pair, no heart. Opponent opened preflop and made a small 1/3-pot c‑bet on the flop (I called), then checked the turn. That line is consistent with a wide raising/c‑betting range that contains a fair number of Ax and small/medium pairs plus some bluffs. The river is a heart but only a holder of two hearts makes a flush (relatively rare). My showdown value is essentially zero: I only beat pure air/missed broadways. To bluff profitably I need meaningful fold equity — a half‑pot bet (~600) requires >33% folds to be +EV, a smaller bet ~400 needs >25% folds. Given Flash’s line, a large portion of his checking range still beats me (Ax, pairs that called the flop), so fold equity is marginal. The line I took (call flop, check turn) doesn’t credibly represent a made flush or a strong value hand enough to guarantee the needed fold frequency. With deep stacks and a small pot relative to stacks, preserving chips and avoiding a thin bluff is the correct, +EV choice. I check to reach showdown.

The board is Ad-As-8h-2h-3h. I have Jack-high. Minni called my flop continuation bet but has checked through the turn and river. While an Ace is in my range, my check on the turn weakened the story that I have one. Minni could be holding a small pocket pair or an Eight that she is willing to take to showdown. Given that we are at hand 144 of 150 and I have a significant chip lead, I see no reason to turn my hand into a bluff in a relatively small pot. I will check behind and take the showdown value, preserving my stack for the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Q♠7♦. Heads-up the button should open a very wide range — Q7o is strong enough to raise rather than limp/call because I want the initiative and fold equity. A 3x BB open is standard here and matches my table pattern, so I’ll raise to 300 total. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold Q7o (he’s representing a much stronger range); if he calls I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting favorable runouts and exercising pot control on dry boards. Raising to 300 maximizes immediate EV while keeping options flexible for the remaining hands.

K9o is a solid defense hand in heads-up play, especially against a 3x open. With a significant chip lead and only five hands remaining in the tournament, I want to avoid high-variance pre-flop confrontations unless I have a premium hand. Calling allows me to play the hand in position on later streets or check-call on the flop depending on the texture. Folding is too tight given the strength of my hand relative to a typical heads-up opening range, and raising might bloat the pot unnecessarily against a potentially aggressive opponent.

I have flopped top pair with a Nine kicker on a paired board (K-8-8). This is a very strong holding in heads-up play. The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they will continuation bet. Leading into the aggressor (donk-betting) often narrows my range and might cause her to fold her air. By checking, I allow her to represent the King or attempt to steal the pot with a wide range of hands, allowing me to extract more value later in the hand.

I make a small continuation bet of 200 (≈1/3 pot). I opened preflop with a wide heads‑up range so I still have significant range advantage; Flash’s cold‑call from the BB is wide and a large portion of his holdings (overcards, missed broadways, small pairs) will fold on this dry, paired‑8 flop. The board (Ks 8c 8h) is static and dangerous for bluffing too large — it’s checked by many strong hands (Kx, 8x/trips) looking to trap — so I keep the size small to preserve fold equity while avoiding bloating the pot with Q7o, which has very little showdown equity and no draws. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check back often unless I improve or get a clear foldable card); if he check‑raises I’ll fold to avoid committing with a marginal hand.

I have flopped top pair with a Nine kicker on a paired board (K-8-8). This is an exceptionally strong hand in a heads-up scenario. Minni's small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard play that could represent a wide range of hands, including total air, middle pairs, or a King. Calling is the most effective play here because it keeps the pot size manageable while allowing Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands on future streets. Raising at this point would likely only be called by better hands (like an 8 or a King with a better kicker) and would force out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. With a significant chip lead and few hands remaining, I am content to see a turn and evaluate based on the next card.

I have top pair with a King on a board of K-8-8-5. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up match. I checked and called the flop to allow Minni to continue with her bluffs and to keep the pot controlled. The Five on the turn is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I will continue with the strategy of checking to Minni, giving her another opportunity to bet with a wide range of hands, including bluffs or weaker pairs that she might be trying to turn into a bluff or value bet. This allows me to maximize value from her wider range while minimizing risk.

Check. I opened wide preflop and made a small 1/3-pot c‑bet on Ks 8c 8h which was called; the 5♦ on the turn is a blank for my Q♠7♦. Villain’s check turns up a calling/defensive line that contains a lot of Kx, small-to-medium pocket pairs, and the occasional 8 — hands that beat me and will often call a turn stab. I have only 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13%) to improve, so betting here has poor fold equity and simply bloats the pot when I’m behind. With pot control (pot = 1,000; my stack = 14,550) the prudent play is to check back, realize equity if I pair on the river, and avoid committing chips with a marginal hand. If Villain check‑raises the turn I’d fold; if he bets the river I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and the river card.

On the river, the board is Ks-8c-8h-5d-3s. I have top pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker. After calling a small bet on the flop, Minni checked back on the turn, which indicates she likely has a medium-strength hand with showdown value or has given up on a bluff. By checking the river, I am providing her with one last opportunity to bluff with any missed draws or air in her range, or to value bet a hand that is weaker than mine (like a lower pocket pair). Given that I am out of position and the tournament is nearing its end with me holding a significant lead, checking allows me to safely reach showdown while potentially inducing an extra bet from an aggressive opponent.

Pot is 1,000, stacks are deep (I: 14,550; Villain: 24,450). I have Q♠7♦ on Ks 8c 8h 5d 3s. I opened wide pre, c‑bet small on the flop and got called — Villain’s calling range is likely sticky (Kx, small/medium pairs, the occasional 8) and will call most river bluffs. My hand has almost no showdown value and there’s very little fold equity on this dry river; betting only bloats the pot when I’m probably behind. Preserve stack and realize showdown value: check and avoid a losing bluff.

I have pocket tens (TT), which is an extremely strong starting hand in heads-up play. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead, my goal is to continue playing solid, aggressive poker without taking unnecessary risks. A standard raise from the button is the most appropriate move here to build the pot and maintain pressure on Minni. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the match.

Heads-up vs the button's standard open to 300. I hold K♥J♠ — a strong heads-up hand but out of position. 3-betting OOP with KJ deep-stacked will bloat the pot and is unnecessarily committal; a min-raise is too small to gain fold equity and a larger 3-bet puts me in big OOP pots. I’ll call the extra 200 (total commitment 300) to keep the pot controllable, realize equity against a likely wide opening range, and evaluate the flop. On favorable flops (top pair, good draws, or backdoor hearts) I’ll continue for value; on dry, hostile boards I’ll check and fold to heavy aggression.

Heads-up on the big blind with K♥J♠ on 6♣-5♣-9♦. I have two overcards but no club or straight draw and effectively no realistic backdoor flush — this is a marginal hand OOP. Flash opened preflop (wide) but checked the flop, which can be weakness or a trap; either way I don't want to bloat a deep, OOP pot with pure air. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: if he bets a small C-bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) I’ll call to see a turn and re-evaluate; if he barrels larger sizing (≈≥1/2 pot) I’ll fold most of the time, preserving stack for better spots. If he checks back I get a free turn and can value-bet or bluff-turn if I pair up or the texture becomes favorable.

I have an overpair with pocket tens on a 9-6-5 rainbow board with two clubs. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially against a wide calling range from the big blind. Minni checked, which is standard. I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to extract value from top pairs with weaker kickers (like A9 or K9), middle pairs (like 88, 77, or 6x), and various straight and flush draws. This bet also provides protection against overcards and draws that might improve on the turn.

Flop: 6♣-5♣-9♦, I hold K♥J♠ and I checked. Flash bets 300 into the ~600 pot (pot is 900, I must call 300). Calling 300 puts 300 into a 1200 pot after my call, so my pot‑odds requirement is ~25%. My raw chance to pair a K or J by the river is ~24%, and versus a wide button opening/c‑bet range my overall equity is likely at or just above that. The additional cost is small relative to my 14,250 stack and folding here would be too nitty; raising OOP on this dry board is unnecessarily committal. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot controllable, then fold to a large turn shove/barrel if I miss, or start extracting value/turn-bluff if I improve.

Check. On 6♣-5♣-9♦-A♣ with K♥J♠ I have no club draw and only 6 outs to pair (≈13% to hit the river). The turn Ace helps the button’s continuing range (many Ax and two‑pair combos), so my hand has very little showdown value OOP. Betting here would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in and turn marginal decisions into big commitments. I’ll control the pot and see a river for free — check and re‑evaluate on the river (call small bets if I pair or have a clear bluffcatch, fold to large pressure).

The turn card is an Ace, which is an overcard to my pocket tens and often hits the range of a player defending the big blind. While I still have an overpair to the rest of the board, the Ace is a significant scare card. I have also picked up a flush draw with the Ten of clubs. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to realize my equity for free. This move prevents me from being check-raised off a hand that still has decent showdown value and a strong draw, and it keeps the pot manageable as the tournament nears its end.

River summary: I have K♥J♠ on 6♣-5♣-9♦-A♣-K♦ — top pair with a decent kicker. Villain opened pre, c‑bet the flop (I called) then checked the turn and checked the river; that line points to a lot of medium paired hands or missed draws (9x, 6x, 5x, some Tx or missed clubs) and fewer strong Ax or nut‑club flushes. Checking behind surrenders thin value; a small value bet will get called by those worse single‑pair hands while keeping the price manageable if I’m raised. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (400) to extract value. Plan if action changes: fold to a large river raise (they’ll typically have A/K/two‑pair/trips or a made flush); consider calling only very small raises that are cheap relative to the pot.

I have pocket tens, which were an overpair on the flop (6-5-9) but are now the third-best pair on the board after an Ace turned and a King rivered. In the current hand, I bet the flop and checked back the turn when the Ace appeared to control the pot size and realize my equity. On the river, the King is another overcard, and Minni has led out for 400 into a 1200 pot. This small 1/3 pot sizing often indicates a blocking bet with a marginal hand like a 9, a weak King, or sometimes a total bluff. Given that I am getting 4:1 odds on a call and my pocket tens still beat a significant portion of Minni's potential range (like 9x, 88, 77, or missed straight draws), a call is the most appropriate action. With only a few hands remaining and a substantial chip lead, I want to avoid folding the best hand to a small bet while also not risking a raise that could be met with a shove from a hand that actually holds an Ace or King.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠9♠ — a strong, playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to a wide BB defending range. A standard 3x-ish open (total 300) gives good fold equity, keeps the pot size manageable if called, and lets me play postflop in position with a suited queen and decent connectivity. Plan: raise to 300 total; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold (or reassess given sizing), if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to control the pot. This balances stealing the blinds and playing a hand with good postflop playability.

I hold Ace-Queen of clubs, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent raised from the button, and I should 3-bet for both value and to take the initiative. A standard 3-bet size here is around 1,000 to put pressure on Minni's wide opening range and build a pot with a strong hand.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♠9♠ and Flash 3-bet to 1000. Pot is 1300 and I need 700 to call (minimum 4-bet to 1700). Q9s is a very playable hand heads-up in position: it has suitedness, decent connectivity and useful blockers to Qx/spade combos in his 3-bet range. Calling 700 into 1300 gives ~35% pot odds to see the flop and, with position and implied odds on deeper stacks, is +EV against a wide-to-moderate 3-bet range. A 4-bet here would need to be polarized (big value or pure bluff); Q9s is not strong enough for a value 4-bet and a small 4-bet (1700) won’t credibly fold out many hands, while a large 4-bet commits too much with a speculative holding. Folding is overly tight after we opened. Plan: call and use position postflop — c-bet thin on dry boards, continue/aggress with made hands and strong draws, and exercise pot control or fold to heavy resistance on dangerous runouts.

I have flopped top pair, top kicker (TPTK) on a very dry Ace-high board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, I represent a very strong range that includes many Aces. This board is excellent for my range. I will place a continuation bet of 800 (40% of the pot) to extract value from weaker Aces (like A-J or A-T), pocket pairs, or gutshot straight draws that Minni might have called the 3-bet with.

Call 800. The flop bet is very small relative to the pot (800 into 2800) so calling gives ~22% break-even equity to see a turn — a cheap price with position. I opened preflop and called his 3-bet, so Flash’s range still contains a lot of Ax, medium pairs and missed broadway/connector combos as well as pure bluffs. Q♠9♠ is not strong now but has useful blockers (Q and spade), some showdown value and the ability to realize equity from position. A 4‑bet/raise here would be too polarised and overcommittal with a speculative hand; folding is too nitty given the odds and the SPR — I can continue on many turn cards or fold to clear strength. Plan: call and use position — fold to heavy turn action when I have no improvement, but barrel/raise when I pick up a pair, a favorable spade, or a clear opportunity to bluff on safe runouts.

On the turn, the King of Hearts is a relatively neutral card. While it completes AK, it's more likely to be in my range as the pre-flop 3-better than in Minni's. I still have Top Pair with an excellent kicker (AQ). I'm betting 1,800 into the 3,600 pot (50% pot) to continue extracting value from weaker Aces (AJ, AT, A9) and to discourage any gutshot draws (like 3-5) from seeing a cheap river. Given the tournament stage, I want to maintain control of the pot while maximizing value from my strong holdings.

Fold. I opened with Q♠9♠ and called the 3‑bet and flop, but the K♥ on the turn gives me no pair and no realistic draw. The pot is 5,400 and Flash bet 1,800 — calling would require ~25% equity (1,800 / 7,200). I have only 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 nines) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river, well below the break‑even. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn upsized bet) heavily favors Ax/Kx and other value hands; a raise would be a thin, high‑risk bluff with poor blockers and a big chance of getting called or reraised. This matches my plan to fold to heavy turn action when I haven’t improved — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, 3s4h. My current stack is 26,200, more than double Minni's 13,650. With only 3 hands remaining in the tournament (148, 149, 150), my primary goal is to maintain my lead and minimize unnecessary losses. Raising with this hand is too loose, and calling the blind to see a flop is a waste of chips given the lack of post-flop potential. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack and protect my lead into the final hands.

Heads-up on the button with J♠5♦ is a reasonable open — it plays well in position and has fold equity vs a wide BB. Flash has a big stack and defends often, but I want to apply pressure and take the initiative. I’ll raise to a standard 3x-ish sizing (300 total) to deny good odds to a cold call, pick up the blinds often, and keep the pot manageable with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a big 3-bet, otherwise play the flop in position — c-bet favorable textures, and control size when behind.

With a massive chip lead and only two hands remaining in the tournament, my primary objective is to avoid any unnecessary risks. I hold Jack-Deuce offsuit, a very weak hand, and I am facing a 3x raise from Minni. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal hand. Given the tournament context, folding is the safest way to preserve my stack and secure the win.

This is the final hand of the match. I have a substantial chip lead over Minni (26,050 vs 13,800). Holding 4h 6c on the button (Small Blind), it is a weak hand, but given the massive stack advantage and the fact that it is the last hand, I can afford to play it to see a flop or simply fold to preserve my lead. However, a standard professional play would be to open wide from the button or complete. Since 64o is quite weak and I'm looking to finish the tournament with my current lead, folding is the safest way to ensure I don't lose a significant portion of my stack in a large final hand confrontation. Given the tournament status, I will fold and lock in the victory.
